src/backend/utils/adt/selfuncs.c
1/*-------------------------------------------------------------------------
2 *
3 * selfuncs.c
4 *	  Selectivity functions and index cost estimation functions for
5 *	  standard operators and index access methods.
6 *
7 *	  Selectivity routines are registered in the pg_operator catalog
8 *	  in the "oprrest" and "oprjoin" attributes.
9 *
10 *	  Index cost functions are located via the index AM's API struct,
11 *	  which is obtained from the handler function registered in pg_am.
12 *
13 * Portions Copyright (c) 1996-2025, PostgreSQL Global Development Group
14 * Portions Copyright (c) 1994, Regents of the University of California
15 *
16 *
17 * IDENTIFICATION
18 *	  src/backend/utils/adt/selfuncs.c
19 *
20 *-------------------------------------------------------------------------
21 */
22
23/*----------
24 * Operator selectivity estimation functions are called to estimate the
25 * selectivity of WHERE clauses whose top-level operator is their operator.
26 * We divide the problem into two cases:
27 *		Restriction clause estimation: the clause involves vars of just
28 *			one relation.
29 *		Join clause estimation: the clause involves vars of multiple rels.
30 * Join selectivity estimation is far more difficult and usually less accurate
31 * than restriction estimation.
32 *
33 * When dealing with the inner scan of a nestloop join, we consider the
34 * join's joinclauses as restriction clauses for the inner relation, and
35 * treat vars of the outer relation as parameters (a/k/a constants of unknown
36 * values).  So, restriction estimators need to be able to accept an argument
37 * telling which relation is to be treated as the variable.
38 *
39 * The call convention for a restriction estimator (oprrest function) is
40 *
41 *		Selectivity oprrest (PlannerInfo *root,
42 *							 Oid operator,
43 *							 List *args,
44 *							 int varRelid);
45 *
46 * root: general information about the query (rtable and RelOptInfo lists
47 * are particularly important for the estimator).
48 * operator: OID of the specific operator in question.
49 * args: argument list from the operator clause.
50 * varRelid: if not zero, the relid (rtable index) of the relation to
51 * be treated as the variable relation.  May be zero if the args list
52 * is known to contain vars of only one relation.
53 *
54 * This is represented at the SQL level (in pg_proc) as
55 *
56 *		float8 oprrest (internal, oid, internal, int4);
57 *
58 * The result is a selectivity, that is, a fraction (0 to 1) of the rows
59 * of the relation that are expected to produce a TRUE result for the
60 * given operator.
61 *
62 * The call convention for a join estimator (oprjoin function) is similar
63 * except that varRelid is not needed, and instead join information is
64 * supplied:
65 *
66 *		Selectivity oprjoin (PlannerInfo *root,
67 *							 Oid operator,
68 *							 List *args,
69 *							 JoinType jointype,
70 *							 SpecialJoinInfo *sjinfo);
71 *
72 *		float8 oprjoin (internal, oid, internal, int2, internal);
73 *
74 * (Before Postgres 8.4, join estimators had only the first four of these
75 * parameters.  That signature is still allowed, but deprecated.)  The
76 * relationship between jointype and sjinfo is explained in the comments for
77 * clause_selectivity() --- the short version is that jointype is usually
78 * best ignored in favor of examining sjinfo.
79 *
80 * Join selectivity for regular inner and outer joins is defined as the
81 * fraction (0 to 1) of the cross product of the relations that is expected
82 * to produce a TRUE result for the given operator.  For both semi and anti
83 * joins, however, the selectivity is defined as the fraction of the left-hand
84 * side relation's rows that are expected to have a match (ie, at least one
85 * row with a TRUE result) in the right-hand side.
86 *
87 * For both oprrest and oprjoin functions, the operator's input collation OID
88 * (if any) is passed using the standard fmgr mechanism, so that the estimator
89 * function can fetch it with PG_GET_COLLATION().  Note, however, that all
90 * statistics in pg_statistic are currently built using the relevant column's
91 * collation.
92 *----------
93 */
94

225
226/*
227 *		eqsel			- Selectivity of "=" for any data types.
228 *
229 * Note: this routine is also used to estimate selectivity for some
230 * operators that are not "=" but have comparable selectivity behavior,
231 * such as "~=" (geometric approximate-match).  Even for "=", we must
232 * keep in mind that the left and right datatypes may differ.
233 */
234Datum
235eqsel(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
236{
237	PG_RETURN_FLOAT8((float8) eqsel_internal(fcinfo, false));
238}
239
240/*
241 * Common code for eqsel() and neqsel()
242 */
243static double
244eqsel_internal(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS, bool negate)
245{
246	PlannerInfo *root = (PlannerInfo *) PG_GETARG_POINTER(0);
247	Oid			operator = PG_GETARG_OID(1);
248	List	   *args = (List *) PG_GETARG_POINTER(2);
249	int			varRelid = PG_GETARG_INT32(3);
250	Oid			collation = PG_GET_COLLATION();
251	VariableStatData vardata;
252	Node	   *other;
253	bool		varonleft;
254	double		selec;
255
256	/*
257	 * When asked about <>, we do the estimation using the corresponding =
258	 * operator, then convert to <> via "1.0 - eq_selectivity - nullfrac".
259	 */
260	if (negate)
261	{
262		operator = get_negator(operator);
263		if (!OidIsValid(operator))
264		{
265			/* Use default selectivity (should we raise an error instead?) */
266			return 1.0 - DEFAULT_EQ_SEL;
267		}
268	}
269
270	/*
271	 * If expression is not variable = something or something = variable, then
272	 * punt and return a default estimate.
273	 */
274	if (!get_restriction_variable(root, args, varRelid,
275								  &vardata, &other, &varonleft))
276		return negate ? (1.0 - DEFAULT_EQ_SEL) : DEFAULT_EQ_SEL;
277
278	/*
279	 * We can do a lot better if the something is a constant.  (Note: the
280	 * Const might result from estimation rather than being a simple constant
281	 * in the query.)
282	 */
283	if (IsA(other, Const))
284		selec = var_eq_const(&vardata, operator, collation,
285							 ((Const *) other)->constvalue,
286							 ((Const *) other)->constisnull,
287							 varonleft, negate);
288	else
289		selec = var_eq_non_const(&vardata, operator, collation, other,
290								 varonleft, negate);
291
292	ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
293
294	return selec;
295}
296
297/*
298 * var_eq_const --- eqsel for var = const case
299 *
300 * This is exported so that some other estimation functions can use it.
301 */
302double
303var_eq_const(VariableStatData *vardata, Oid oproid, Oid collation,
304			 Datum constval, bool constisnull,
305			 bool varonleft, bool negate)
306{
307	double		selec;
308	double		nullfrac = 0.0;
309	bool		isdefault;
310	Oid			opfuncoid;
311
312	/*
313	 * If the constant is NULL, assume operator is strict and return zero, ie,
314	 * operator will never return TRUE.  (It's zero even for a negator op.)
315	 */
316	if (constisnull)
317		return 0.0;
318
319	/*
320	 * Grab the nullfrac for use below.  Note we allow use of nullfrac
321	 * regardless of security check.
322	 */
323	if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata->statsTuple))
324	{
325		Form_pg_statistic stats;
326
327		stats = (Form_pg_statistic) GETSTRUCT(vardata->statsTuple);
328		nullfrac = stats->stanullfrac;
329	}
330
331	/*
332	 * If we matched the var to a unique index, DISTINCT or GROUP-BY clause,
333	 * assume there is exactly one match regardless of anything else.  (This
334	 * is slightly bogus, since the index or clause's equality operator might
335	 * be different from ours, but it's much more likely to be right than
336	 * ignoring the information.)
337	 */
338	if (vardata->isunique && vardata->rel && vardata->rel->tuples >= 1.0)
339	{
340		selec = 1.0 / vardata->rel->tuples;
341	}
342	else if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata->statsTuple) &&
343			 statistic_proc_security_check(vardata,
344										   (opfuncoid = get_opcode(oproid))))
345	{
346		AttStatsSlot sslot;
347		bool		match = false;
348		int			i;
349
350		/*
351		 * Is the constant "=" to any of the column's most common values?
352		 * (Although the given operator may not really be "=", we will assume
353		 * that seeing whether it returns TRUE is an appropriate test.  If you
354		 * don't like this, maybe you shouldn't be using eqsel for your
355		 * operator...)
356		 */
357		if (get_attstatsslot(&sslot, vardata->statsTuple,
358							 STATISTIC_KIND_MCV, InvalidOid,
359							 ATTSTATSSLOT_VALUES | ATTSTATSSLOT_NUMBERS))
360		{
361			LOCAL_FCINFO(fcinfo, 2);
362			FmgrInfo	eqproc;
363
364			fmgr_info(opfuncoid, &eqproc);
365
366			/*
367			 * Save a few cycles by setting up the fcinfo struct just once.
368			 * Using FunctionCallInvoke directly also avoids failure if the
369			 * eqproc returns NULL, though really equality functions should
370			 * never do that.
371			 */
372			InitFunctionCallInfoData(*fcinfo, &eqproc, 2, collation,
373									 NULL, NULL);
374			fcinfo->args[0].isnull = false;
375			fcinfo->args[1].isnull = false;
376			/* be careful to apply operator right way 'round */
377			if (varonleft)
378				fcinfo->args[1].value = constval;
379			else
380				fcinfo->args[0].value = constval;
381
382			for (i = 0; i < sslot.nvalues; i++)
383			{
384				Datum		fresult;
385
386				if (varonleft)
387					fcinfo->args[0].value = sslot.values[i];
388				else
389					fcinfo->args[1].value = sslot.values[i];
390				fcinfo->isnull = false;
391				fresult = FunctionCallInvoke(fcinfo);
392				if (!fcinfo->isnull && DatumGetBool(fresult))
393				{
394					match = true;
395					break;
396				}
397			}
398		}
399		else
400		{
401			/* no most-common-value info available */
402			i = 0;				/* keep compiler quiet */
403		}
404
405		if (match)
406		{
407			/*
408			 * Constant is "=" to this common value.  We know selectivity
409			 * exactly (or as exactly as ANALYZE could calculate it, anyway).
410			 */
411			selec = sslot.numbers[i];
412		}
413		else
414		{
415			/*
416			 * Comparison is against a constant that is neither NULL nor any
417			 * of the common values.  Its selectivity cannot be more than
418			 * this:
419			 */
420			double		sumcommon = 0.0;
421			double		otherdistinct;
422
423			for (i = 0; i < sslot.nnumbers; i++)
424				sumcommon += sslot.numbers[i];
425			selec = 1.0 - sumcommon - nullfrac;
426			CLAMP_PROBABILITY(selec);
427
428			/*
429			 * and in fact it's probably a good deal less. We approximate that
430			 * all the not-common values share this remaining fraction
431			 * equally, so we divide by the number of other distinct values.
432			 */
433			otherdistinct = get_variable_numdistinct(vardata, &isdefault) -
434				sslot.nnumbers;
435			if (otherdistinct > 1)
436				selec /= otherdistinct;
437
438			/*
439			 * Another cross-check: selectivity shouldn't be estimated as more
440			 * than the least common "most common value".
441			 */
442			if (sslot.nnumbers > 0 && selec > sslot.numbers[sslot.nnumbers - 1])
443				selec = sslot.numbers[sslot.nnumbers - 1];
444		}
445
446		free_attstatsslot(&sslot);
447	}
448	else
449	{
450		/*
451		 * No ANALYZE stats available, so make a guess using estimated number
452		 * of distinct values and assuming they are equally common. (The guess
453		 * is unlikely to be very good, but we do know a few special cases.)
454		 */
455		selec = 1.0 / get_variable_numdistinct(vardata, &isdefault);
456	}
457
458	/* now adjust if we wanted <> rather than = */
459	if (negate)
460		selec = 1.0 - selec - nullfrac;
461
462	/* result should be in range, but make sure... */
463	CLAMP_PROBABILITY(selec);
464
465	return selec;
466}
467
468/*
469 * var_eq_non_const --- eqsel for var = something-other-than-const case
470 *
471 * This is exported so that some other estimation functions can use it.
472 */
473double
474var_eq_non_const(VariableStatData *vardata, Oid oproid, Oid collation,
475				 Node *other,
476				 bool varonleft, bool negate)
477{
478	double		selec;
479	double		nullfrac = 0.0;
480	bool		isdefault;
481
482	/*
483	 * Grab the nullfrac for use below.
484	 */
485	if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata->statsTuple))
486	{
487		Form_pg_statistic stats;
488
489		stats = (Form_pg_statistic) GETSTRUCT(vardata->statsTuple);
490		nullfrac = stats->stanullfrac;
491	}
492
493	/*
494	 * If we matched the var to a unique index, DISTINCT or GROUP-BY clause,
495	 * assume there is exactly one match regardless of anything else.  (This
496	 * is slightly bogus, since the index or clause's equality operator might
497	 * be different from ours, but it's much more likely to be right than
498	 * ignoring the information.)
499	 */
500	if (vardata->isunique && vardata->rel && vardata->rel->tuples >= 1.0)
501	{
502		selec = 1.0 / vardata->rel->tuples;
503	}
504	else if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata->statsTuple))
505	{
506		double		ndistinct;
507		AttStatsSlot sslot;
508
509		/*
510		 * Search is for a value that we do not know a priori, but we will
511		 * assume it is not NULL.  Estimate the selectivity as non-null
512		 * fraction divided by number of distinct values, so that we get a
513		 * result averaged over all possible values whether common or
514		 * uncommon.  (Essentially, we are assuming that the not-yet-known
515		 * comparison value is equally likely to be any of the possible
516		 * values, regardless of their frequency in the table.  Is that a good
517		 * idea?)
518		 */
519		selec = 1.0 - nullfrac;
520		ndistinct = get_variable_numdistinct(vardata, &isdefault);
521		if (ndistinct > 1)
522			selec /= ndistinct;
523
524		/*
525		 * Cross-check: selectivity should never be estimated as more than the
526		 * most common value's.
527		 */
528		if (get_attstatsslot(&sslot, vardata->statsTuple,
529							 STATISTIC_KIND_MCV, InvalidOid,
530							 ATTSTATSSLOT_NUMBERS))
531		{
532			if (sslot.nnumbers > 0 && selec > sslot.numbers[0])
533				selec = sslot.numbers[0];
534			free_attstatsslot(&sslot);
535		}
536	}
537	else
538	{
539		/*
540		 * No ANALYZE stats available, so make a guess using estimated number
541		 * of distinct values and assuming they are equally common. (The guess
542		 * is unlikely to be very good, but we do know a few special cases.)
543		 */
544		selec = 1.0 / get_variable_numdistinct(vardata, &isdefault);
545	}
546
547	/* now adjust if we wanted <> rather than = */
548	if (negate)
549		selec = 1.0 - selec - nullfrac;
550
551	/* result should be in range, but make sure... */
552	CLAMP_PROBABILITY(selec);
553
554	return selec;
555}
556
557/*
558 *		neqsel			- Selectivity of "!=" for any data types.
559 *
560 * This routine is also used for some operators that are not "!="
561 * but have comparable selectivity behavior.  See above comments
562 * for eqsel().
563 */
564Datum
565neqsel(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
566{
567	PG_RETURN_FLOAT8((float8) eqsel_internal(fcinfo, true));
568}
569
570/*
571 *	scalarineqsel		- Selectivity of "<", "<=", ">", ">=" for scalars.
572 *
573 * This is the guts of scalarltsel/scalarlesel/scalargtsel/scalargesel.
574 * The isgt and iseq flags distinguish which of the four cases apply.
575 *
576 * The caller has commuted the clause, if necessary, so that we can treat
577 * the variable as being on the left.  The caller must also make sure that
578 * the other side of the clause is a non-null Const, and dissect that into
579 * a value and datatype.  (This definition simplifies some callers that
580 * want to estimate against a computed value instead of a Const node.)
581 *
582 * This routine works for any datatype (or pair of datatypes) known to
583 * convert_to_scalar().  If it is applied to some other datatype,
584 * it will return an approximate estimate based on assuming that the constant
585 * value falls in the middle of the bin identified by binary search.
586 */
587static double
588scalarineqsel(PlannerInfo *root, Oid operator, bool isgt, bool iseq,
589			  Oid collation,
590			  VariableStatData *vardata, Datum constval, Oid consttype)
591{
592	Form_pg_statistic stats;
593	FmgrInfo	opproc;
594	double		mcv_selec,
595				hist_selec,
596				sumcommon;
597	double		selec;
598
599	if (!HeapTupleIsValid(vardata->statsTuple))
600	{
601		/*
602		 * No stats are available.  Typically this means we have to fall back
603		 * on the default estimate; but if the variable is CTID then we can
604		 * make an estimate based on comparing the constant to the table size.
605		 */
606		if (vardata->var && IsA(vardata->var, Var) &&
607			((Var *) vardata->var)->varattno == SelfItemPointerAttributeNumber)
608		{
609			ItemPointer itemptr;
610			double		block;
611			double		density;
612
613			/*
614			 * If the relation's empty, we're going to include all of it.
615			 * (This is mostly to avoid divide-by-zero below.)
616			 */
617			if (vardata->rel->pages == 0)
618				return 1.0;
619
620			itemptr = (ItemPointer) DatumGetPointer(constval);
621			block = ItemPointerGetBlockNumberNoCheck(itemptr);
622
623			/*
624			 * Determine the average number of tuples per page (density).
625			 *
626			 * Since the last page will, on average, be only half full, we can
627			 * estimate it to have half as many tuples as earlier pages.  So
628			 * give it half the weight of a regular page.
629			 */
630			density = vardata->rel->tuples / (vardata->rel->pages - 0.5);
631
632			/* If target is the last page, use half the density. */
633			if (block >= vardata->rel->pages - 1)
634				density *= 0.5;
635
636			/*
637			 * Using the average tuples per page, calculate how far into the
638			 * page the itemptr is likely to be and adjust block accordingly,
639			 * by adding that fraction of a whole block (but never more than a
640			 * whole block, no matter how high the itemptr's offset is).  Here
641			 * we are ignoring the possibility of dead-tuple line pointers,
642			 * which is fairly bogus, but we lack the info to do better.
643			 */
644			if (density > 0.0)
645			{
646				OffsetNumber offset = ItemPointerGetOffsetNumberNoCheck(itemptr);
647
648				block += Min(offset / density, 1.0);
649			}
650
651			/*
652			 * Convert relative block number to selectivity.  Again, the last
653			 * page has only half weight.
654			 */
655			selec = block / (vardata->rel->pages - 0.5);
656
657			/*
658			 * The calculation so far gave us a selectivity for the "<=" case.
659			 * We'll have one fewer tuple for "<" and one additional tuple for
660			 * ">=", the latter of which we'll reverse the selectivity for
661			 * below, so we can simply subtract one tuple for both cases.  The
662			 * cases that need this adjustment can be identified by iseq being
663			 * equal to isgt.
664			 */
665			if (iseq == isgt && vardata->rel->tuples >= 1.0)
666				selec -= (1.0 / vardata->rel->tuples);
667
668			/* Finally, reverse the selectivity for the ">", ">=" cases. */
669			if (isgt)
670				selec = 1.0 - selec;
671
672			CLAMP_PROBABILITY(selec);
673			return selec;
674		}
675
676		/* no stats available, so default result */
677		return DEFAULT_INEQ_SEL;
678	}
679	stats = (Form_pg_statistic) GETSTRUCT(vardata->statsTuple);
680
681	fmgr_info(get_opcode(operator), &opproc);
682
683	/*
684	 * If we have most-common-values info, add up the fractions of the MCV
685	 * entries that satisfy MCV OP CONST.  These fractions contribute directly
686	 * to the result selectivity.  Also add up the total fraction represented
687	 * by MCV entries.
688	 */
689	mcv_selec = mcv_selectivity(vardata, &opproc, collation, constval, true,
690								&sumcommon);
691
692	/*
693	 * If there is a histogram, determine which bin the constant falls in, and
694	 * compute the resulting contribution to selectivity.
695	 */
696	hist_selec = ineq_histogram_selectivity(root, vardata,
697											operator, &opproc, isgt, iseq,
698											collation,
699											constval, consttype);
700
701	/*
702	 * Now merge the results from the MCV and histogram calculations,
703	 * realizing that the histogram covers only the non-null values that are
704	 * not listed in MCV.
705	 */
706	selec = 1.0 - stats->stanullfrac - sumcommon;
707
708	if (hist_selec >= 0.0)
709		selec *= hist_selec;
710	else
711	{
712		/*
713		 * If no histogram but there are values not accounted for by MCV,
714		 * arbitrarily assume half of them will match.
715		 */
716		selec *= 0.5;
717	}
718
719	selec += mcv_selec;
720
721	/* result should be in range, but make sure... */
722	CLAMP_PROBABILITY(selec);
723
724	return selec;
725}
726
727/*
728 *	mcv_selectivity			- Examine the MCV list for selectivity estimates
729 *
730 * Determine the fraction of the variable's MCV population that satisfies
731 * the predicate (VAR OP CONST), or (CONST OP VAR) if !varonleft.  Also
732 * compute the fraction of the total column population represented by the MCV
733 * list.  This code will work for any boolean-returning predicate operator.
734 *
735 * The function result is the MCV selectivity, and the fraction of the
736 * total population is returned into *sumcommonp.  Zeroes are returned
737 * if there is no MCV list.
738 */
739double
740mcv_selectivity(VariableStatData *vardata, FmgrInfo *opproc, Oid collation,
741				Datum constval, bool varonleft,
742				double *sumcommonp)
743{
744	double		mcv_selec,
745				sumcommon;
746	AttStatsSlot sslot;
747	int			i;
748
749	mcv_selec = 0.0;
750	sumcommon = 0.0;
751
752	if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata->statsTuple) &&
753		statistic_proc_security_check(vardata, opproc->fn_oid) &&
754		get_attstatsslot(&sslot, vardata->statsTuple,
755						 STATISTIC_KIND_MCV, InvalidOid,
756						 ATTSTATSSLOT_VALUES | ATTSTATSSLOT_NUMBERS))
757	{
758		LOCAL_FCINFO(fcinfo, 2);
759
760		/*
761		 * We invoke the opproc "by hand" so that we won't fail on NULL
762		 * results.  Such cases won't arise for normal comparison functions,
763		 * but generic_restriction_selectivity could perhaps be used with
764		 * operators that can return NULL.  A small side benefit is to not
765		 * need to re-initialize the fcinfo struct from scratch each time.
766		 */
767		InitFunctionCallInfoData(*fcinfo, opproc, 2, collation,
768								 NULL, NULL);
769		fcinfo->args[0].isnull = false;
770		fcinfo->args[1].isnull = false;
771		/* be careful to apply operator right way 'round */
772		if (varonleft)
773			fcinfo->args[1].value = constval;
774		else
775			fcinfo->args[0].value = constval;
776
777		for (i = 0; i < sslot.nvalues; i++)
778		{
779			Datum		fresult;
780
781			if (varonleft)
782				fcinfo->args[0].value = sslot.values[i];
783			else
784				fcinfo->args[1].value = sslot.values[i];
785			fcinfo->isnull = false;
786			fresult = FunctionCallInvoke(fcinfo);
787			if (!fcinfo->isnull && DatumGetBool(fresult))
788				mcv_selec += sslot.numbers[i];
789			sumcommon += sslot.numbers[i];
790		}
791		free_attstatsslot(&sslot);
792	}
793
794	*sumcommonp = sumcommon;
795	return mcv_selec;
796}
797
798/*
799 *	histogram_selectivity	- Examine the histogram for selectivity estimates
800 *
801 * Determine the fraction of the variable's histogram entries that satisfy
802 * the predicate (VAR OP CONST), or (CONST OP VAR) if !varonleft.
803 *
804 * This code will work for any boolean-returning predicate operator, whether
805 * or not it has anything to do with the histogram sort operator.  We are
806 * essentially using the histogram just as a representative sample.  However,
807 * small histograms are unlikely to be all that representative, so the caller
808 * should be prepared to fall back on some other estimation approach when the
809 * histogram is missing or very small.  It may also be prudent to combine this
810 * approach with another one when the histogram is small.
811 *
812 * If the actual histogram size is not at least min_hist_size, we won't bother
813 * to do the calculation at all.  Also, if the n_skip parameter is > 0, we
814 * ignore the first and last n_skip histogram elements, on the grounds that
815 * they are outliers and hence not very representative.  Typical values for
816 * these parameters are 10 and 1.
817 *
818 * The function result is the selectivity, or -1 if there is no histogram
819 * or it's smaller than min_hist_size.
820 *
821 * The output parameter *hist_size receives the actual histogram size,
822 * or zero if no histogram.  Callers may use this number to decide how
823 * much faith to put in the function result.
824 *
825 * Note that the result disregards both the most-common-values (if any) and
826 * null entries.  The caller is expected to combine this result with
827 * statistics for those portions of the column population.  It may also be
828 * prudent to clamp the result range, ie, disbelieve exact 0 or 1 outputs.
829 */
830double
831histogram_selectivity(VariableStatData *vardata,
832					  FmgrInfo *opproc, Oid collation,
833					  Datum constval, bool varonleft,
834					  int min_hist_size, int n_skip,
835					  int *hist_size)
836{
837	double		result;
838	AttStatsSlot sslot;
839
840	/* check sanity of parameters */
841	Assert(n_skip >= 0);
842	Assert(min_hist_size > 2 * n_skip);
843
844	if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata->statsTuple) &&
845		statistic_proc_security_check(vardata, opproc->fn_oid) &&
846		get_attstatsslot(&sslot, vardata->statsTuple,
847						 STATISTIC_KIND_HISTOGRAM, InvalidOid,
848						 ATTSTATSSLOT_VALUES))
849	{
850		*hist_size = sslot.nvalues;
851		if (sslot.nvalues >= min_hist_size)
852		{
853			LOCAL_FCINFO(fcinfo, 2);
854			int			nmatch = 0;
855			int			i;
856
857			/*
858			 * We invoke the opproc "by hand" so that we won't fail on NULL
859			 * results.  Such cases won't arise for normal comparison
860			 * functions, but generic_restriction_selectivity could perhaps be
861			 * used with operators that can return NULL.  A small side benefit
862			 * is to not need to re-initialize the fcinfo struct from scratch
863			 * each time.
864			 */
865			InitFunctionCallInfoData(*fcinfo, opproc, 2, collation,
866									 NULL, NULL);
867			fcinfo->args[0].isnull = false;
868			fcinfo->args[1].isnull = false;
869			/* be careful to apply operator right way 'round */
870			if (varonleft)
871				fcinfo->args[1].value = constval;
872			else
873				fcinfo->args[0].value = constval;
874
875			for (i = n_skip; i < sslot.nvalues - n_skip; i++)
876			{
877				Datum		fresult;
878
879				if (varonleft)
880					fcinfo->args[0].value = sslot.values[i];
881				else
882					fcinfo->args[1].value = sslot.values[i];
883				fcinfo->isnull = false;
884				fresult = FunctionCallInvoke(fcinfo);
885				if (!fcinfo->isnull && DatumGetBool(fresult))
886					nmatch++;
887			}
888			result = ((double) nmatch) / ((double) (sslot.nvalues - 2 * n_skip));
889		}
890		else
891			result = -1;
892		free_attstatsslot(&sslot);
893	}
894	else
895	{
896		*hist_size = 0;
897		result = -1;
898	}
899
900	return result;
901}
902
903/*
904 *	generic_restriction_selectivity		- Selectivity for almost anything
905 *
906 * This function estimates selectivity for operators that we don't have any
907 * special knowledge about, but are on data types that we collect standard
908 * MCV and/or histogram statistics for.  (Additional assumptions are that
909 * the operator is strict and immutable, or at least stable.)
910 *
911 * If we have "VAR OP CONST" or "CONST OP VAR", selectivity is estimated by
912 * applying the operator to each element of the column's MCV and/or histogram
913 * stats, and merging the results using the assumption that the histogram is
914 * a reasonable random sample of the column's non-MCV population.  Note that
915 * if the operator's semantics are related to the histogram ordering, this
916 * might not be such a great assumption; other functions such as
917 * scalarineqsel() are probably a better match in such cases.
918 *
919 * Otherwise, fall back to the default selectivity provided by the caller.
920 */
921double
922generic_restriction_selectivity(PlannerInfo *root, Oid oproid, Oid collation,
923								List *args, int varRelid,
924								double default_selectivity)
925{
926	double		selec;
927	VariableStatData vardata;
928	Node	   *other;
929	bool		varonleft;
930
931	/*
932	 * If expression is not variable OP something or something OP variable,
933	 * then punt and return the default estimate.
934	 */
935	if (!get_restriction_variable(root, args, varRelid,
936								  &vardata, &other, &varonleft))
937		return default_selectivity;
938
939	/*
940	 * If the something is a NULL constant, assume operator is strict and
941	 * return zero, ie, operator will never return TRUE.
942	 */
943	if (IsA(other, Const) &&
944		((Const *) other)->constisnull)
945	{
946		ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
947		return 0.0;
948	}
949
950	if (IsA(other, Const))
951	{
952		/* Variable is being compared to a known non-null constant */
953		Datum		constval = ((Const *) other)->constvalue;
954		FmgrInfo	opproc;
955		double		mcvsum;
956		double		mcvsel;
957		double		nullfrac;
958		int			hist_size;
959
960		fmgr_info(get_opcode(oproid), &opproc);
961
962		/*
963		 * Calculate the selectivity for the column's most common values.
964		 */
965		mcvsel = mcv_selectivity(&vardata, &opproc, collation,
966								 constval, varonleft,
967								 &mcvsum);
968
969		/*
970		 * If the histogram is large enough, see what fraction of it matches
971		 * the query, and assume that's representative of the non-MCV
972		 * population.  Otherwise use the default selectivity for the non-MCV
973		 * population.
974		 */
975		selec = histogram_selectivity(&vardata, &opproc, collation,
976									  constval, varonleft,
977									  10, 1, &hist_size);
978		if (selec < 0)
979		{
980			/* Nope, fall back on default */
981			selec = default_selectivity;
982		}
983		else if (hist_size < 100)
984		{
985			/*
986			 * For histogram sizes from 10 to 100, we combine the histogram
987			 * and default selectivities, putting increasingly more trust in
988			 * the histogram for larger sizes.
989			 */
990			double		hist_weight = hist_size / 100.0;
991
992			selec = selec * hist_weight +
993				default_selectivity * (1.0 - hist_weight);
994		}
995
996		/* In any case, don't believe extremely small or large estimates. */
997		if (selec < 0.0001)
998			selec = 0.0001;
999		else if (selec > 0.9999)
1000			selec = 0.9999;
1001
1002		/* Don't forget to account for nulls. */
1003		if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata.statsTuple))
1004			nullfrac = ((Form_pg_statistic) GETSTRUCT(vardata.statsTuple))->stanullfrac;
1005		else
1006			nullfrac = 0.0;
1007
1008		/*
1009		 * Now merge the results from the MCV and histogram calculations,
1010		 * realizing that the histogram covers only the non-null values that
1011		 * are not listed in MCV.
1012		 */
1013		selec *= 1.0 - nullfrac - mcvsum;
1014		selec += mcvsel;
1015	}
1016	else
1017	{
1018		/* Comparison value is not constant, so we can't do anything */
1019		selec = default_selectivity;
1020	}
1021
1022	ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
1023
1024	/* result should be in range, but make sure... */
1025	CLAMP_PROBABILITY(selec);
1026
1027	return selec;
1028}
1029
1030/*
1031 *	ineq_histogram_selectivity	- Examine the histogram for scalarineqsel
1032 *
1033 * Determine the fraction of the variable's histogram population that
1034 * satisfies the inequality condition, ie, VAR < (or <=, >, >=) CONST.
1035 * The isgt and iseq flags distinguish which of the four cases apply.
1036 *
1037 * While opproc could be looked up from the operator OID, common callers
1038 * also need to call it separately, so we make the caller pass both.
1039 *
1040 * Returns -1 if there is no histogram (valid results will always be >= 0).
1041 *
1042 * Note that the result disregards both the most-common-values (if any) and
1043 * null entries.  The caller is expected to combine this result with
1044 * statistics for those portions of the column population.
1045 *
1046 * This is exported so that some other estimation functions can use it.
1047 */
1048double
1049ineq_histogram_selectivity(PlannerInfo *root,
1050						   VariableStatData *vardata,
1051						   Oid opoid, FmgrInfo *opproc, bool isgt, bool iseq,
1052						   Oid collation,
1053						   Datum constval, Oid consttype)
1054{
1055	double		hist_selec;
1056	AttStatsSlot sslot;
1057
1058	hist_selec = -1.0;
1059
1060	/*
1061	 * Someday, ANALYZE might store more than one histogram per rel/att,
1062	 * corresponding to more than one possible sort ordering defined for the
1063	 * column type.  Right now, we know there is only one, so just grab it and
1064	 * see if it matches the query.
1065	 *
1066	 * Note that we can't use opoid as search argument; the staop appearing in
1067	 * pg_statistic will be for the relevant '<' operator, but what we have
1068	 * might be some other inequality operator such as '>='.  (Even if opoid
1069	 * is a '<' operator, it could be cross-type.)  Hence we must use
1070	 * comparison_ops_are_compatible() to see if the operators match.
1071	 */
1072	if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata->statsTuple) &&
1073		statistic_proc_security_check(vardata, opproc->fn_oid) &&
1074		get_attstatsslot(&sslot, vardata->statsTuple,
1075						 STATISTIC_KIND_HISTOGRAM, InvalidOid,
1076						 ATTSTATSSLOT_VALUES))
1077	{
1078		if (sslot.nvalues > 1 &&
1079			sslot.stacoll == collation &&
1080			comparison_ops_are_compatible(sslot.staop, opoid))
1081		{
1082			/*
1083			 * Use binary search to find the desired location, namely the
1084			 * right end of the histogram bin containing the comparison value,
1085			 * which is the leftmost entry for which the comparison operator
1086			 * succeeds (if isgt) or fails (if !isgt).
1087			 *
1088			 * In this loop, we pay no attention to whether the operator iseq
1089			 * or not; that detail will be mopped up below.  (We cannot tell,
1090			 * anyway, whether the operator thinks the values are equal.)
1091			 *
1092			 * If the binary search accesses the first or last histogram
1093			 * entry, we try to replace that endpoint with the true column min
1094			 * or max as found by get_actual_variable_range().  This
1095			 * ameliorates misestimates when the min or max is moving as a
1096			 * result of changes since the last ANALYZE.  Note that this could
1097			 * result in effectively including MCVs into the histogram that
1098			 * weren't there before, but we don't try to correct for that.
1099			 */
1100			double		histfrac;
1101			int			lobound = 0;	/* first possible slot to search */
1102			int			hibound = sslot.nvalues;	/* last+1 slot to search */
1103			bool		have_end = false;
1104
1105			/*
1106			 * If there are only two histogram entries, we'll want up-to-date
1107			 * values for both.  (If there are more than two, we need at most
1108			 * one of them to be updated, so we deal with that within the
1109			 * loop.)
1110			 */
1111			if (sslot.nvalues == 2)
1112				have_end = get_actual_variable_range(root,
1113													 vardata,
1114													 sslot.staop,
1115													 collation,
1116													 &sslot.values[0],
1117													 &sslot.values[1]);
1118
1119			while (lobound < hibound)
1120			{
1121				int			probe = (lobound + hibound) / 2;
1122				bool		ltcmp;
1123
1124				/*
1125				 * If we find ourselves about to compare to the first or last
1126				 * histogram entry, first try to replace it with the actual
1127				 * current min or max (unless we already did so above).
1128				 */
1129				if (probe == 0 && sslot.nvalues > 2)
1130					have_end = get_actual_variable_range(root,
1131														 vardata,
1132														 sslot.staop,
1133														 collation,
1134														 &sslot.values[0],
1135														 NULL);
1136				else if (probe == sslot.nvalues - 1 && sslot.nvalues > 2)
1137					have_end = get_actual_variable_range(root,
1138														 vardata,
1139														 sslot.staop,
1140														 collation,
1141														 NULL,
1142														 &sslot.values[probe]);
1143
1144				ltcmp = DatumGetBool(FunctionCall2Coll(opproc,
1145													   collation,
1146													   sslot.values[probe],
1147													   constval));
1148				if (isgt)
1149					ltcmp = !ltcmp;
1150				if (ltcmp)
1151					lobound = probe + 1;
1152				else
1153					hibound = probe;
1154			}
1155
1156			if (lobound <= 0)
1157			{
1158				/*
1159				 * Constant is below lower histogram boundary.  More
1160				 * precisely, we have found that no entry in the histogram
1161				 * satisfies the inequality clause (if !isgt) or they all do
1162				 * (if isgt).  We estimate that that's true of the entire
1163				 * table, so set histfrac to 0.0 (which we'll flip to 1.0
1164				 * below, if isgt).
1165				 */
1166				histfrac = 0.0;
1167			}
1168			else if (lobound >= sslot.nvalues)
1169			{
1170				/*
1171				 * Inverse case: constant is above upper histogram boundary.
1172				 */
1173				histfrac = 1.0;
1174			}
1175			else
1176			{
1177				/* We have values[i-1] <= constant <= values[i]. */
1178				int			i = lobound;
1179				double		eq_selec = 0;
1180				double		val,
1181							high,
1182							low;
1183				double		binfrac;
1184
1185				/*
1186				 * In the cases where we'll need it below, obtain an estimate
1187				 * of the selectivity of "x = constval".  We use a calculation
1188				 * similar to what var_eq_const() does for a non-MCV constant,
1189				 * ie, estimate that all distinct non-MCV values occur equally
1190				 * often.  But multiplication by "1.0 - sumcommon - nullfrac"
1191				 * will be done by our caller, so we shouldn't do that here.
1192				 * Therefore we can't try to clamp the estimate by reference
1193				 * to the least common MCV; the result would be too small.
1194				 *
1195				 * Note: since this is effectively assuming that constval
1196				 * isn't an MCV, it's logically dubious if constval in fact is
1197				 * one.  But we have to apply *some* correction for equality,
1198				 * and anyway we cannot tell if constval is an MCV, since we
1199				 * don't have a suitable equality operator at hand.
1200				 */
1201				if (i == 1 || isgt == iseq)
1202				{
1203					double		otherdistinct;
1204					bool		isdefault;
1205					AttStatsSlot mcvslot;
1206
1207					/* Get estimated number of distinct values */
1208					otherdistinct = get_variable_numdistinct(vardata,
1209															 &isdefault);
1210
1211					/* Subtract off the number of known MCVs */
1212					if (get_attstatsslot(&mcvslot, vardata->statsTuple,
1213										 STATISTIC_KIND_MCV, InvalidOid,
1214										 ATTSTATSSLOT_NUMBERS))
1215					{
1216						otherdistinct -= mcvslot.nnumbers;
1217						free_attstatsslot(&mcvslot);
1218					}
1219
1220					/* If result doesn't seem sane, leave eq_selec at 0 */
1221					if (otherdistinct > 1)
1222						eq_selec = 1.0 / otherdistinct;
1223				}
1224
1225				/*
1226				 * Convert the constant and the two nearest bin boundary
1227				 * values to a uniform comparison scale, and do a linear
1228				 * interpolation within this bin.
1229				 */
1230				if (convert_to_scalar(constval, consttype, collation,
1231									  &val,
1232									  sslot.values[i - 1], sslot.values[i],
1233									  vardata->vartype,
1234									  &low, &high))
1235				{
1236					if (high <= low)
1237					{
1238						/* cope if bin boundaries appear identical */
1239						binfrac = 0.5;
1240					}
1241					else if (val <= low)
1242						binfrac = 0.0;
1243					else if (val >= high)
1244						binfrac = 1.0;
1245					else
1246					{
1247						binfrac = (val - low) / (high - low);
1248
1249						/*
1250						 * Watch out for the possibility that we got a NaN or
1251						 * Infinity from the division.  This can happen
1252						 * despite the previous checks, if for example "low"
1253						 * is -Infinity.
1254						 */
1255						if (isnan(binfrac) ||
1256							binfrac < 0.0 || binfrac > 1.0)
1257							binfrac = 0.5;
1258					}
1259				}
1260				else
1261				{
1262					/*
1263					 * Ideally we'd produce an error here, on the grounds that
1264					 * the given operator shouldn't have scalarXXsel
1265					 * registered as its selectivity func unless we can deal
1266					 * with its operand types.  But currently, all manner of
1267					 * stuff is invoking scalarXXsel, so give a default
1268					 * estimate until that can be fixed.
1269					 */
1270					binfrac = 0.5;
1271				}
1272
1273				/*
1274				 * Now, compute the overall selectivity across the values
1275				 * represented by the histogram.  We have i-1 full bins and
1276				 * binfrac partial bin below the constant.
1277				 */
1278				histfrac = (double) (i - 1) + binfrac;
1279				histfrac /= (double) (sslot.nvalues - 1);
1280
1281				/*
1282				 * At this point, histfrac is an estimate of the fraction of
1283				 * the population represented by the histogram that satisfies
1284				 * "x <= constval".  Somewhat remarkably, this statement is
1285				 * true regardless of which operator we were doing the probes
1286				 * with, so long as convert_to_scalar() delivers reasonable
1287				 * results.  If the probe constant is equal to some histogram
1288				 * entry, we would have considered the bin to the left of that
1289				 * entry if probing with "<" or ">=", or the bin to the right
1290				 * if probing with "<=" or ">"; but binfrac would have come
1291				 * out as 1.0 in the first case and 0.0 in the second, leading
1292				 * to the same histfrac in either case.  For probe constants
1293				 * between histogram entries, we find the same bin and get the
1294				 * same estimate with any operator.
1295				 *
1296				 * The fact that the estimate corresponds to "x <= constval"
1297				 * and not "x < constval" is because of the way that ANALYZE
1298				 * constructs the histogram: each entry is, effectively, the
1299				 * rightmost value in its sample bucket.  So selectivity
1300				 * values that are exact multiples of 1/(histogram_size-1)
1301				 * should be understood as estimates including a histogram
1302				 * entry plus everything to its left.
1303				 *
1304				 * However, that breaks down for the first histogram entry,
1305				 * which necessarily is the leftmost value in its sample
1306				 * bucket.  That means the first histogram bin is slightly
1307				 * narrower than the rest, by an amount equal to eq_selec.
1308				 * Another way to say that is that we want "x <= leftmost" to
1309				 * be estimated as eq_selec not zero.  So, if we're dealing
1310				 * with the first bin (i==1), rescale to make that true while
1311				 * adjusting the rest of that bin linearly.
1312				 */
1313				if (i == 1)
1314					histfrac += eq_selec * (1.0 - binfrac);
1315
1316				/*
1317				 * "x <= constval" is good if we want an estimate for "<=" or
1318				 * ">", but if we are estimating for "<" or ">=", we now need
1319				 * to decrease the estimate by eq_selec.
1320				 */
1321				if (isgt == iseq)
1322					histfrac -= eq_selec;
1323			}
1324
1325			/*
1326			 * Now the estimate is finished for "<" and "<=" cases.  If we are
1327			 * estimating for ">" or ">=", flip it.
1328			 */
1329			hist_selec = isgt ? (1.0 - histfrac) : histfrac;
1330
1331			/*
1332			 * The histogram boundaries are only approximate to begin with,
1333			 * and may well be out of date anyway.  Therefore, don't believe
1334			 * extremely small or large selectivity estimates --- unless we
1335			 * got actual current endpoint values from the table, in which
1336			 * case just do the usual sanity clamp.  Somewhat arbitrarily, we
1337			 * set the cutoff for other cases at a hundredth of the histogram
1338			 * resolution.
1339			 */
1340			if (have_end)
1341				CLAMP_PROBABILITY(hist_selec);
1342			else
1343			{
1344				double		cutoff = 0.01 / (double) (sslot.nvalues - 1);
1345
1346				if (hist_selec < cutoff)
1347					hist_selec = cutoff;
1348				else if (hist_selec > 1.0 - cutoff)
1349					hist_selec = 1.0 - cutoff;
1350			}
1351		}
1352		else if (sslot.nvalues > 1)
1353		{
1354			/*
1355			 * If we get here, we have a histogram but it's not sorted the way
1356			 * we want.  Do a brute-force search to see how many of the
1357			 * entries satisfy the comparison condition, and take that
1358			 * fraction as our estimate.  (This is identical to the inner loop
1359			 * of histogram_selectivity; maybe share code?)
1360			 */
1361			LOCAL_FCINFO(fcinfo, 2);
1362			int			nmatch = 0;
1363
1364			InitFunctionCallInfoData(*fcinfo, opproc, 2, collation,
1365									 NULL, NULL);
1366			fcinfo->args[0].isnull = false;
1367			fcinfo->args[1].isnull = false;
1368			fcinfo->args[1].value = constval;
1369			for (int i = 0; i < sslot.nvalues; i++)
1370			{
1371				Datum		fresult;
1372
1373				fcinfo->args[0].value = sslot.values[i];
1374				fcinfo->isnull = false;
1375				fresult = FunctionCallInvoke(fcinfo);
1376				if (!fcinfo->isnull && DatumGetBool(fresult))
1377					nmatch++;
1378			}
1379			hist_selec = ((double) nmatch) / ((double) sslot.nvalues);
1380
1381			/*
1382			 * As above, clamp to a hundredth of the histogram resolution.
1383			 * This case is surely even less trustworthy than the normal one,
1384			 * so we shouldn't believe exact 0 or 1 selectivity.  (Maybe the
1385			 * clamp should be more restrictive in this case?)
1386			 */
1387			{
1388				double		cutoff = 0.01 / (double) (sslot.nvalues - 1);
1389
1390				if (hist_selec < cutoff)
1391					hist_selec = cutoff;
1392				else if (hist_selec > 1.0 - cutoff)
1393					hist_selec = 1.0 - cutoff;
1394			}
1395		}
1396
1397		free_attstatsslot(&sslot);
1398	}
1399
1400	return hist_selec;
1401}
1402
1403/*
1404 * Common wrapper function for the selectivity estimators that simply
1405 * invoke scalarineqsel().
1406 */
1407static Datum
1408scalarineqsel_wrapper(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS, bool isgt, bool iseq)
1409{
1410	PlannerInfo *root = (PlannerInfo *) PG_GETARG_POINTER(0);
1411	Oid			operator = PG_GETARG_OID(1);
1412	List	   *args = (List *) PG_GETARG_POINTER(2);
1413	int			varRelid = PG_GETARG_INT32(3);
1414	Oid			collation = PG_GET_COLLATION();
1415	VariableStatData vardata;
1416	Node	   *other;
1417	bool		varonleft;
1418	Datum		constval;
1419	Oid			consttype;
1420	double		selec;
1421
1422	/*
1423	 * If expression is not variable op something or something op variable,
1424	 * then punt and return a default estimate.
1425	 */
1426	if (!get_restriction_variable(root, args, varRelid,
1427								  &vardata, &other, &varonleft))
1428		PG_RETURN_FLOAT8(DEFAULT_INEQ_SEL);
1429
1430	/*
1431	 * Can't do anything useful if the something is not a constant, either.
1432	 */
1433	if (!IsA(other, Const))
1434	{
1435		ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
1436		PG_RETURN_FLOAT8(DEFAULT_INEQ_SEL);
1437	}
1438
1439	/*
1440	 * If the constant is NULL, assume operator is strict and return zero, ie,
1441	 * operator will never return TRUE.
1442	 */
1443	if (((Const *) other)->constisnull)
1444	{
1445		ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
1446		PG_RETURN_FLOAT8(0.0);
1447	}
1448	constval = ((Const *) other)->constvalue;
1449	consttype = ((Const *) other)->consttype;
1450
1451	/*
1452	 * Force the var to be on the left to simplify logic in scalarineqsel.
1453	 */
1454	if (!varonleft)
1455	{
1456		operator = get_commutator(operator);
1457		if (!operator)
1458		{
1459			/* Use default selectivity (should we raise an error instead?) */
1460			ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
1461			PG_RETURN_FLOAT8(DEFAULT_INEQ_SEL);
1462		}
1463		isgt = !isgt;
1464	}
1465
1466	/* The rest of the work is done by scalarineqsel(). */
1467	selec = scalarineqsel(root, operator, isgt, iseq, collation,
1468						  &vardata, constval, consttype);
1469
1470	ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
1471
1472	PG_RETURN_FLOAT8((float8) selec);
1473}
1474
1475/*
1476 *		scalarltsel		- Selectivity of "<" for scalars.
1477 */
1478Datum
1479scalarltsel(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
1480{
1481	return scalarineqsel_wrapper(fcinfo, false, false);
1482}
1483
1484/*
1485 *		scalarlesel		- Selectivity of "<=" for scalars.
1486 */
1487Datum
1488scalarlesel(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
1489{
1490	return scalarineqsel_wrapper(fcinfo, false, true);
1491}
1492
1493/*
1494 *		scalargtsel		- Selectivity of ">" for scalars.
1495 */
1496Datum
1497scalargtsel(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
1498{
1499	return scalarineqsel_wrapper(fcinfo, true, false);
1500}
1501
1502/*
1503 *		scalargesel		- Selectivity of ">=" for scalars.
1504 */
1505Datum
1506scalargesel(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
1507{
1508	return scalarineqsel_wrapper(fcinfo, true, true);
1509}
1510
1511/*
1512 *		boolvarsel		- Selectivity of Boolean variable.
1513 *
1514 * This can actually be called on any boolean-valued expression.  If it
1515 * involves only Vars of the specified relation, and if there are statistics
1516 * about the Var or expression (the latter is possible if it's indexed) then
1517 * we'll produce a real estimate; otherwise it's just a default.
1518 */
1519Selectivity
1520boolvarsel(PlannerInfo *root, Node *arg, int varRelid)
1521{
1522	VariableStatData vardata;
1523	double		selec;
1524
1525	examine_variable(root, arg, varRelid, &vardata);
1526	if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata.statsTuple))
1527	{
1528		/*
1529		 * A boolean variable V is equivalent to the clause V = 't', so we
1530		 * compute the selectivity as if that is what we have.
1531		 */
1532		selec = var_eq_const(&vardata, BooleanEqualOperator, InvalidOid,
1533							 BoolGetDatum(true), false, true, false);
1534	}
1535	else
1536	{
1537		/* Otherwise, the default estimate is 0.5 */
1538		selec = 0.5;
1539	}
1540	ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
1541	return selec;
1542}
1543
1544/*
1545 *		booltestsel		- Selectivity of BooleanTest Node.
1546 */
1547Selectivity
1548booltestsel(PlannerInfo *root, BoolTestType booltesttype, Node *arg,
1549			int varRelid, JoinType jointype, SpecialJoinInfo *sjinfo)
1550{
1551	VariableStatData vardata;
1552	double		selec;
1553
1554	examine_variable(root, arg, varRelid, &vardata);
1555
1556	if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata.statsTuple))
1557	{
1558		Form_pg_statistic stats;
1559		double		freq_null;
1560		AttStatsSlot sslot;
1561
1562		stats = (Form_pg_statistic) GETSTRUCT(vardata.statsTuple);
1563		freq_null = stats->stanullfrac;
1564
1565		if (get_attstatsslot(&sslot, vardata.statsTuple,
1566							 STATISTIC_KIND_MCV, InvalidOid,
1567							 ATTSTATSSLOT_VALUES | ATTSTATSSLOT_NUMBERS)
1568			&& sslot.nnumbers > 0)
1569		{
1570			double		freq_true;
1571			double		freq_false;
1572
1573			/*
1574			 * Get first MCV frequency and derive frequency for true.
1575			 */
1576			if (DatumGetBool(sslot.values[0]))
1577				freq_true = sslot.numbers[0];
1578			else
1579				freq_true = 1.0 - sslot.numbers[0] - freq_null;
1580
1581			/*
1582			 * Next derive frequency for false. Then use these as appropriate
1583			 * to derive frequency for each case.
1584			 */
1585			freq_false = 1.0 - freq_true - freq_null;
1586
1587			switch (booltesttype)
1588			{
1589				case IS_UNKNOWN:
1590					/* select only NULL values */
1591					selec = freq_null;
1592					break;
1593				case IS_NOT_UNKNOWN:
1594					/* select non-NULL values */
1595					selec = 1.0 - freq_null;
1596					break;
1597				case IS_TRUE:
1598					/* select only TRUE values */
1599					selec = freq_true;
1600					break;
1601				case IS_NOT_TRUE:
1602					/* select non-TRUE values */
1603					selec = 1.0 - freq_true;
1604					break;
1605				case IS_FALSE:
1606					/* select only FALSE values */
1607					selec = freq_false;
1608					break;
1609				case IS_NOT_FALSE:
1610					/* select non-FALSE values */
1611					selec = 1.0 - freq_false;
1612					break;
1613				default:
1614					elog(ERROR, "unrecognized booltesttype: %d",
1615						 (int) booltesttype);
1616					selec = 0.0;	/* Keep compiler quiet */
1617					break;
1618			}
1619
1620			free_attstatsslot(&sslot);
1621		}
1622		else
1623		{
1624			/*
1625			 * No most-common-value info available. Still have null fraction
1626			 * information, so use it for IS [NOT] UNKNOWN. Otherwise adjust
1627			 * for null fraction and assume a 50-50 split of TRUE and FALSE.
1628			 */
1629			switch (booltesttype)
1630			{
1631				case IS_UNKNOWN:
1632					/* select only NULL values */
1633					selec = freq_null;
1634					break;
1635				case IS_NOT_UNKNOWN:
1636					/* select non-NULL values */
1637					selec = 1.0 - freq_null;
1638					break;
1639				case IS_TRUE:
1640				case IS_FALSE:
1641					/* Assume we select half of the non-NULL values */
1642					selec = (1.0 - freq_null) / 2.0;
1643					break;
1644				case IS_NOT_TRUE:
1645				case IS_NOT_FALSE:
1646					/* Assume we select NULLs plus half of the non-NULLs */
1647					/* equiv. to freq_null + (1.0 - freq_null) / 2.0 */
1648					selec = (freq_null + 1.0) / 2.0;
1649					break;
1650				default:
1651					elog(ERROR, "unrecognized booltesttype: %d",
1652						 (int) booltesttype);
1653					selec = 0.0;	/* Keep compiler quiet */
1654					break;
1655			}
1656		}
1657	}
1658	else
1659	{
1660		/*
1661		 * If we can't get variable statistics for the argument, perhaps
1662		 * clause_selectivity can do something with it.  We ignore the
1663		 * possibility of a NULL value when using clause_selectivity, and just
1664		 * assume the value is either TRUE or FALSE.
1665		 */
1666		switch (booltesttype)
1667		{
1668			case IS_UNKNOWN:
1669				selec = DEFAULT_UNK_SEL;
1670				break;
1671			case IS_NOT_UNKNOWN:
1672				selec = DEFAULT_NOT_UNK_SEL;
1673				break;
1674			case IS_TRUE:
1675			case IS_NOT_FALSE:
1676				selec = (double) clause_selectivity(root, arg,
1677													varRelid,
1678													jointype, sjinfo);
1679				break;
1680			case IS_FALSE:
1681			case IS_NOT_TRUE:
1682				selec = 1.0 - (double) clause_selectivity(root, arg,
1683														  varRelid,
1684														  jointype, sjinfo);
1685				break;
1686			default:
1687				elog(ERROR, "unrecognized booltesttype: %d",
1688					 (int) booltesttype);
1689				selec = 0.0;	/* Keep compiler quiet */
1690				break;
1691		}
1692	}
1693
1694	ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
1695
1696	/* result should be in range, but make sure... */
1697	CLAMP_PROBABILITY(selec);
1698
1699	return (Selectivity) selec;
1700}
1701
1702/*
1703 *		nulltestsel		- Selectivity of NullTest Node.
1704 */
1705Selectivity
1706nulltestsel(PlannerInfo *root, NullTestType nulltesttype, Node *arg,
1707			int varRelid, JoinType jointype, SpecialJoinInfo *sjinfo)
1708{
1709	VariableStatData vardata;
1710	double		selec;
1711
1712	examine_variable(root, arg, varRelid, &vardata);
1713
1714	if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata.statsTuple))
1715	{
1716		Form_pg_statistic stats;
1717		double		freq_null;
1718
1719		stats = (Form_pg_statistic) GETSTRUCT(vardata.statsTuple);
1720		freq_null = stats->stanullfrac;
1721
1722		switch (nulltesttype)
1723		{
1724			case IS_NULL:
1725
1726				/*
1727				 * Use freq_null directly.
1728				 */
1729				selec = freq_null;
1730				break;
1731			case IS_NOT_NULL:
1732
1733				/*
1734				 * Select not unknown (not null) values. Calculate from
1735				 * freq_null.
1736				 */
1737				selec = 1.0 - freq_null;
1738				break;
1739			default:
1740				elog(ERROR, "unrecognized nulltesttype: %d",
1741					 (int) nulltesttype);
1742				return (Selectivity) 0; /* keep compiler quiet */
1743		}
1744	}
1745	else if (vardata.var && IsA(vardata.var, Var) &&
1746			 ((Var *) vardata.var)->varattno < 0)
1747	{
1748		/*
1749		 * There are no stats for system columns, but we know they are never
1750		 * NULL.
1751		 */
1752		selec = (nulltesttype == IS_NULL) ? 0.0 : 1.0;
1753	}
1754	else
1755	{
1756		/*
1757		 * No ANALYZE stats available, so make a guess
1758		 */
1759		switch (nulltesttype)
1760		{
1761			case IS_NULL:
1762				selec = DEFAULT_UNK_SEL;
1763				break;
1764			case IS_NOT_NULL:
1765				selec = DEFAULT_NOT_UNK_SEL;
1766				break;
1767			default:
1768				elog(ERROR, "unrecognized nulltesttype: %d",
1769					 (int) nulltesttype);
1770				return (Selectivity) 0; /* keep compiler quiet */
1771		}
1772	}
1773
1774	ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
1775
1776	/* result should be in range, but make sure... */
1777	CLAMP_PROBABILITY(selec);
1778
1779	return (Selectivity) selec;
1780}
1781
1782/*
1783 * strip_array_coercion - strip binary-compatible relabeling from an array expr
1784 *
1785 * For array values, the parser normally generates ArrayCoerceExpr conversions,
1786 * but it seems possible that RelabelType might show up.  Also, the planner
1787 * is not currently tense about collapsing stacked ArrayCoerceExpr nodes,
1788 * so we need to be ready to deal with more than one level.
1789 */
1790static Node *
1791strip_array_coercion(Node *node)
1792{
1793	for (;;)
1794	{
1795		if (node && IsA(node, ArrayCoerceExpr))
1796		{
1797			ArrayCoerceExpr *acoerce = (ArrayCoerceExpr *) node;
1798
1799			/*
1800			 * If the per-element expression is just a RelabelType on top of
1801			 * CaseTestExpr, then we know it's a binary-compatible relabeling.
1802			 */
1803			if (IsA(acoerce->elemexpr, RelabelType) &&
1804				IsA(((RelabelType *) acoerce->elemexpr)->arg, CaseTestExpr))
1805				node = (Node *) acoerce->arg;
1806			else
1807				break;
1808		}
1809		else if (node && IsA(node, RelabelType))
1810		{
1811			/* We don't really expect this case, but may as well cope */
1812			node = (Node *) ((RelabelType *) node)->arg;
1813		}
1814		else
1815			break;
1816	}
1817	return node;
1818}
1819
1820/*
1821 *		scalararraysel		- Selectivity of ScalarArrayOpExpr Node.
1822 */
1823Selectivity
1824scalararraysel(PlannerInfo *root,
1825			   ScalarArrayOpExpr *clause,
1826			   bool is_join_clause,
1827			   int varRelid,
1828			   JoinType jointype,
1829			   SpecialJoinInfo *sjinfo)
1830{
1831	Oid			operator = clause->opno;
1832	bool		useOr = clause->useOr;
1833	bool		isEquality = false;
1834	bool		isInequality = false;
1835	Node	   *leftop;
1836	Node	   *rightop;
1837	Oid			nominal_element_type;
1838	Oid			nominal_element_collation;
1839	TypeCacheEntry *typentry;
1840	RegProcedure oprsel;
1841	FmgrInfo	oprselproc;
1842	Selectivity s1;
1843	Selectivity s1disjoint;
1844
1845	/* First, deconstruct the expression */
1846	Assert(list_length(clause->args) == 2);
1847	leftop = (Node *) linitial(clause->args);
1848	rightop = (Node *) lsecond(clause->args);
1849
1850	/* aggressively reduce both sides to constants */
1851	leftop = estimate_expression_value(root, leftop);
1852	rightop = estimate_expression_value(root, rightop);
1853
1854	/* get nominal (after relabeling) element type of rightop */
1855	nominal_element_type = get_base_element_type(exprType(rightop));
1856	if (!OidIsValid(nominal_element_type))
1857		return (Selectivity) 0.5;	/* probably shouldn't happen */
1858	/* get nominal collation, too, for generating constants */
1859	nominal_element_collation = exprCollation(rightop);
1860
1861	/* look through any binary-compatible relabeling of rightop */
1862	rightop = strip_array_coercion(rightop);
1863
1864	/*
1865	 * Detect whether the operator is the default equality or inequality
1866	 * operator of the array element type.
1867	 */
1868	typentry = lookup_type_cache(nominal_element_type, TYPECACHE_EQ_OPR);
1869	if (OidIsValid(typentry->eq_opr))
1870	{
1871		if (operator == typentry->eq_opr)
1872			isEquality = true;
1873		else if (get_negator(operator) == typentry->eq_opr)
1874			isInequality = true;
1875	}
1876
1877	/*
1878	 * If it is equality or inequality, we might be able to estimate this as a
1879	 * form of array containment; for instance "const = ANY(column)" can be
1880	 * treated as "ARRAY[const] <@ column".  scalararraysel_containment tries
1881	 * that, and returns the selectivity estimate if successful, or -1 if not.
1882	 */
1883	if ((isEquality || isInequality) && !is_join_clause)
1884	{
1885		s1 = scalararraysel_containment(root, leftop, rightop,
1886										nominal_element_type,
1887										isEquality, useOr, varRelid);
1888		if (s1 >= 0.0)
1889			return s1;
1890	}
1891
1892	/*
1893	 * Look up the underlying operator's selectivity estimator. Punt if it
1894	 * hasn't got one.
1895	 */
1896	if (is_join_clause)
1897		oprsel = get_oprjoin(operator);
1898	else
1899		oprsel = get_oprrest(operator);
1900	if (!oprsel)
1901		return (Selectivity) 0.5;
1902	fmgr_info(oprsel, &oprselproc);
1903
1904	/*
1905	 * In the array-containment check above, we must only believe that an
1906	 * operator is equality or inequality if it is the default btree equality
1907	 * operator (or its negator) for the element type, since those are the
1908	 * operators that array containment will use.  But in what follows, we can
1909	 * be a little laxer, and also believe that any operators using eqsel() or
1910	 * neqsel() as selectivity estimator act like equality or inequality.
1911	 */
1912	if (oprsel == F_EQSEL || oprsel == F_EQJOINSEL)
1913		isEquality = true;
1914	else if (oprsel == F_NEQSEL || oprsel == F_NEQJOINSEL)
1915		isInequality = true;
1916
1917	/*
1918	 * We consider three cases:
1919	 *
1920	 * 1. rightop is an Array constant: deconstruct the array, apply the
1921	 * operator's selectivity function for each array element, and merge the
1922	 * results in the same way that clausesel.c does for AND/OR combinations.
1923	 *
1924	 * 2. rightop is an ARRAY[] construct: apply the operator's selectivity
1925	 * function for each element of the ARRAY[] construct, and merge.
1926	 *
1927	 * 3. otherwise, make a guess ...
1928	 */
1929	if (rightop && IsA(rightop, Const))
1930	{
1931		Datum		arraydatum = ((Const *) rightop)->constvalue;
1932		bool		arrayisnull = ((Const *) rightop)->constisnull;
1933		ArrayType  *arrayval;
1934		int16		elmlen;
1935		bool		elmbyval;
1936		char		elmalign;
1937		int			num_elems;
1938		Datum	   *elem_values;
1939		bool	   *elem_nulls;
1940		int			i;
1941
1942		if (arrayisnull)		/* qual can't succeed if null array */
1943			return (Selectivity) 0.0;
1944		arrayval = DatumGetArrayTypeP(arraydatum);
1945		get_typlenbyvalalign(ARR_ELEMTYPE(arrayval),
1946							 &elmlen, &elmbyval, &elmalign);
1947		deconstruct_array(arrayval,
1948						  ARR_ELEMTYPE(arrayval),
1949						  elmlen, elmbyval, elmalign,
1950						  &elem_values, &elem_nulls, &num_elems);
1951
1952		/*
1953		 * For generic operators, we assume the probability of success is
1954		 * independent for each array element.  But for "= ANY" or "<> ALL",
1955		 * if the array elements are distinct (which'd typically be the case)
1956		 * then the probabilities are disjoint, and we should just sum them.
1957		 *
1958		 * If we were being really tense we would try to confirm that the
1959		 * elements are all distinct, but that would be expensive and it
1960		 * doesn't seem to be worth the cycles; it would amount to penalizing
1961		 * well-written queries in favor of poorly-written ones.  However, we
1962		 * do protect ourselves a little bit by checking whether the
1963		 * disjointness assumption leads to an impossible (out of range)
1964		 * probability; if so, we fall back to the normal calculation.
1965		 */
1966		s1 = s1disjoint = (useOr ? 0.0 : 1.0);
1967
1968		for (i = 0; i < num_elems; i++)
1969		{
1970			List	   *args;
1971			Selectivity s2;
1972
1973			args = list_make2(leftop,
1974							  makeConst(nominal_element_type,
1975										-1,
1976										nominal_element_collation,
1977										elmlen,
1978										elem_values[i],
1979										elem_nulls[i],
1980										elmbyval));
1981			if (is_join_clause)
1982				s2 = DatumGetFloat8(FunctionCall5Coll(&oprselproc,
1983													  clause->inputcollid,
1984													  PointerGetDatum(root),
1985													  ObjectIdGetDatum(operator),
1986													  PointerGetDatum(args),
1987													  Int16GetDatum(jointype),
1988													  PointerGetDatum(sjinfo)));
1989			else
1990				s2 = DatumGetFloat8(FunctionCall4Coll(&oprselproc,
1991													  clause->inputcollid,
1992													  PointerGetDatum(root),
1993													  ObjectIdGetDatum(operator),
1994													  PointerGetDatum(args),
1995													  Int32GetDatum(varRelid)));
1996
1997			if (useOr)
1998			{
1999				s1 = s1 + s2 - s1 * s2;
2000				if (isEquality)
2001					s1disjoint += s2;
2002			}
2003			else
2004			{
2005				s1 = s1 * s2;
2006				if (isInequality)
2007					s1disjoint += s2 - 1.0;
2008			}
2009		}
2010
2011		/* accept disjoint-probability estimate if in range */
2012		if ((useOr ? isEquality : isInequality) &&
2013			s1disjoint >= 0.0 && s1disjoint <= 1.0)
2014			s1 = s1disjoint;
2015	}
2016	else if (rightop && IsA(rightop, ArrayExpr) &&
2017			 !((ArrayExpr *) rightop)->multidims)
2018	{
2019		ArrayExpr  *arrayexpr = (ArrayExpr *) rightop;
2020		int16		elmlen;
2021		bool		elmbyval;
2022		ListCell   *l;
2023
2024		get_typlenbyval(arrayexpr->element_typeid,
2025						&elmlen, &elmbyval);
2026
2027		/*
2028		 * We use the assumption of disjoint probabilities here too, although
2029		 * the odds of equal array elements are rather higher if the elements
2030		 * are not all constants (which they won't be, else constant folding
2031		 * would have reduced the ArrayExpr to a Const).  In this path it's
2032		 * critical to have the sanity check on the s1disjoint estimate.
2033		 */
2034		s1 = s1disjoint = (useOr ? 0.0 : 1.0);
2035
2036		foreach(l, arrayexpr->elements)
2037		{
2038			Node	   *elem = (Node *) lfirst(l);
2039			List	   *args;
2040			Selectivity s2;
2041
2042			/*
2043			 * Theoretically, if elem isn't of nominal_element_type we should
2044			 * insert a RelabelType, but it seems unlikely that any operator
2045			 * estimation function would really care ...
2046			 */
2047			args = list_make2(leftop, elem);
2048			if (is_join_clause)
2049				s2 = DatumGetFloat8(FunctionCall5Coll(&oprselproc,
2050													  clause->inputcollid,
2051													  PointerGetDatum(root),
2052													  ObjectIdGetDatum(operator),
2053													  PointerGetDatum(args),
2054													  Int16GetDatum(jointype),
2055													  PointerGetDatum(sjinfo)));
2056			else
2057				s2 = DatumGetFloat8(FunctionCall4Coll(&oprselproc,
2058													  clause->inputcollid,
2059													  PointerGetDatum(root),
2060													  ObjectIdGetDatum(operator),
2061													  PointerGetDatum(args),
2062													  Int32GetDatum(varRelid)));
2063
2064			if (useOr)
2065			{
2066				s1 = s1 + s2 - s1 * s2;
2067				if (isEquality)
2068					s1disjoint += s2;
2069			}
2070			else
2071			{
2072				s1 = s1 * s2;
2073				if (isInequality)
2074					s1disjoint += s2 - 1.0;
2075			}
2076		}
2077
2078		/* accept disjoint-probability estimate if in range */
2079		if ((useOr ? isEquality : isInequality) &&
2080			s1disjoint >= 0.0 && s1disjoint <= 1.0)
2081			s1 = s1disjoint;
2082	}
2083	else
2084	{
2085		CaseTestExpr *dummyexpr;
2086		List	   *args;
2087		Selectivity s2;
2088		int			i;
2089
2090		/*
2091		 * We need a dummy rightop to pass to the operator selectivity
2092		 * routine.  It can be pretty much anything that doesn't look like a
2093		 * constant; CaseTestExpr is a convenient choice.
2094		 */
2095		dummyexpr = makeNode(CaseTestExpr);
2096		dummyexpr->typeId = nominal_element_type;
2097		dummyexpr->typeMod = -1;
2098		dummyexpr->collation = clause->inputcollid;
2099		args = list_make2(leftop, dummyexpr);
2100		if (is_join_clause)
2101			s2 = DatumGetFloat8(FunctionCall5Coll(&oprselproc,
2102												  clause->inputcollid,
2103												  PointerGetDatum(root),
2104												  ObjectIdGetDatum(operator),
2105												  PointerGetDatum(args),
2106												  Int16GetDatum(jointype),
2107												  PointerGetDatum(sjinfo)));
2108		else
2109			s2 = DatumGetFloat8(FunctionCall4Coll(&oprselproc,
2110												  clause->inputcollid,
2111												  PointerGetDatum(root),
2112												  ObjectIdGetDatum(operator),
2113												  PointerGetDatum(args),
2114												  Int32GetDatum(varRelid)));
2115		s1 = useOr ? 0.0 : 1.0;
2116
2117		/*
2118		 * Arbitrarily assume 10 elements in the eventual array value (see
2119		 * also estimate_array_length).  We don't risk an assumption of
2120		 * disjoint probabilities here.
2121		 */
2122		for (i = 0; i < 10; i++)
2123		{
2124			if (useOr)
2125				s1 = s1 + s2 - s1 * s2;
2126			else
2127				s1 = s1 * s2;
2128		}
2129	}
2130
2131	/* result should be in range, but make sure... */
2132	CLAMP_PROBABILITY(s1);
2133
2134	return s1;
2135}
2136
2137/*
2138 * Estimate number of elements in the array yielded by an expression.
2139 *
2140 * Note: the result is integral, but we use "double" to avoid overflow
2141 * concerns.  Most callers will use it in double-type expressions anyway.
2142 *
2143 * Note: in some code paths root can be passed as NULL, resulting in
2144 * slightly worse estimates.
2145 */
2146double
2147estimate_array_length(PlannerInfo *root, Node *arrayexpr)
2148{
2149	/* look through any binary-compatible relabeling of arrayexpr */
2150	arrayexpr = strip_array_coercion(arrayexpr);
2151
2152	if (arrayexpr && IsA(arrayexpr, Const))
2153	{
2154		Datum		arraydatum = ((Const *) arrayexpr)->constvalue;
2155		bool		arrayisnull = ((Const *) arrayexpr)->constisnull;
2156		ArrayType  *arrayval;
2157
2158		if (arrayisnull)
2159			return 0;
2160		arrayval = DatumGetArrayTypeP(arraydatum);
2161		return ArrayGetNItems(ARR_NDIM(arrayval), ARR_DIMS(arrayval));
2162	}
2163	else if (arrayexpr && IsA(arrayexpr, ArrayExpr) &&
2164			 !((ArrayExpr *) arrayexpr)->multidims)
2165	{
2166		return list_length(((ArrayExpr *) arrayexpr)->elements);
2167	}
2168	else if (arrayexpr && root)
2169	{
2170		/* See if we can find any statistics about it */
2171		VariableStatData vardata;
2172		AttStatsSlot sslot;
2173		double		nelem = 0;
2174
2175		/*
2176		 * Skip calling examine_variable for Var with varno 0, which has no
2177		 * valid relation entry and would error in find_base_rel.  Such a Var
2178		 * can appear when a nested set operation's output type doesn't match
2179		 * the parent's expected type, because recurse_set_operations builds a
2180		 * projection target list using generate_setop_tlist with varno 0, and
2181		 * if the required type coercion involves an ArrayCoerceExpr, we can
2182		 * be called on that Var.
2183		 */
2184		if (IsA(arrayexpr, Var) && ((Var *) arrayexpr)->varno == 0)
2185			return 10;			/* default guess, should match scalararraysel */
2186
2187		examine_variable(root, arrayexpr, 0, &vardata);
2188		if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata.statsTuple))
2189		{
2190			/*
2191			 * Found stats, so use the average element count, which is stored
2192			 * in the last stanumbers element of the DECHIST statistics.
2193			 * Actually that is the average count of *distinct* elements;
2194			 * perhaps we should scale it up somewhat?
2195			 */
2196			if (get_attstatsslot(&sslot, vardata.statsTuple,
2197								 STATISTIC_KIND_DECHIST, InvalidOid,
2198								 ATTSTATSSLOT_NUMBERS))
2199			{
2200				if (sslot.nnumbers > 0)
2201					nelem = clamp_row_est(sslot.numbers[sslot.nnumbers - 1]);
2202				free_attstatsslot(&sslot);
2203			}
2204		}
2205		ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
2206
2207		if (nelem > 0)
2208			return nelem;
2209	}
2210
2211	/* Else use a default guess --- this should match scalararraysel */
2212	return 10;
2213}
2214
2215/*
2216 *		rowcomparesel		- Selectivity of RowCompareExpr Node.
2217 *
2218 * We estimate RowCompare selectivity by considering just the first (high
2219 * order) columns, which makes it equivalent to an ordinary OpExpr.  While
2220 * this estimate could be refined by considering additional columns, it
2221 * seems unlikely that we could do a lot better without multi-column
2222 * statistics.
2223 */
2224Selectivity
2225rowcomparesel(PlannerInfo *root,
2226			  RowCompareExpr *clause,
2227			  int varRelid, JoinType jointype, SpecialJoinInfo *sjinfo)
2228{
2229	Selectivity s1;
2230	Oid			opno = linitial_oid(clause->opnos);
2231	Oid			inputcollid = linitial_oid(clause->inputcollids);
2232	List	   *opargs;
2233	bool		is_join_clause;
2234
2235	/* Build equivalent arg list for single operator */
2236	opargs = list_make2(linitial(clause->largs), linitial(clause->rargs));
2237
2238	/*
2239	 * Decide if it's a join clause.  This should match clausesel.c's
2240	 * treat_as_join_clause(), except that we intentionally consider only the
2241	 * leading columns and not the rest of the clause.
2242	 */
2243	if (varRelid != 0)
2244	{
2245		/*
2246		 * Caller is forcing restriction mode (eg, because we are examining an
2247		 * inner indexscan qual).
2248		 */
2249		is_join_clause = false;
2250	}
2251	else if (sjinfo == NULL)
2252	{
2253		/*
2254		 * It must be a restriction clause, since it's being evaluated at a
2255		 * scan node.
2256		 */
2257		is_join_clause = false;
2258	}
2259	else
2260	{
2261		/*
2262		 * Otherwise, it's a join if there's more than one base relation used.
2263		 */
2264		is_join_clause = (NumRelids(root, (Node *) opargs) > 1);
2265	}
2266
2267	if (is_join_clause)
2268	{
2269		/* Estimate selectivity for a join clause. */
2270		s1 = join_selectivity(root, opno,
2271							  opargs,
2272							  inputcollid,
2273							  jointype,
2274							  sjinfo);
2275	}
2276	else
2277	{
2278		/* Estimate selectivity for a restriction clause. */
2279		s1 = restriction_selectivity(root, opno,
2280									 opargs,
2281									 inputcollid,
2282									 varRelid);
2283	}
2284
2285	return s1;
2286}
2287
2288/*
2289 *		eqjoinsel		- Join selectivity of "="
2290 */
2291Datum
2292eqjoinsel(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
2293{
2294	PlannerInfo *root = (PlannerInfo *) PG_GETARG_POINTER(0);
2295	Oid			operator = PG_GETARG_OID(1);
2296	List	   *args = (List *) PG_GETARG_POINTER(2);
2297
2298#ifdef NOT_USED
2299	JoinType	jointype = (JoinType) PG_GETARG_INT16(3);
2300#endif
2301	SpecialJoinInfo *sjinfo = (SpecialJoinInfo *) PG_GETARG_POINTER(4);
2302	Oid			collation = PG_GET_COLLATION();
2303	double		selec;
2304	double		selec_inner;
2305	VariableStatData vardata1;
2306	VariableStatData vardata2;
2307	double		nd1;
2308	double		nd2;
2309	bool		isdefault1;
2310	bool		isdefault2;
2311	Oid			opfuncoid;
2312	AttStatsSlot sslot1;
2313	AttStatsSlot sslot2;
2314	Form_pg_statistic stats1 = NULL;
2315	Form_pg_statistic stats2 = NULL;
2316	bool		have_mcvs1 = false;
2317	bool		have_mcvs2 = false;
2318	bool		get_mcv_stats;
2319	bool		join_is_reversed;
2320	RelOptInfo *inner_rel;
2321
2322	get_join_variables(root, args, sjinfo,
2323					   &vardata1, &vardata2, &join_is_reversed);
2324
2325	nd1 = get_variable_numdistinct(&vardata1, &isdefault1);
2326	nd2 = get_variable_numdistinct(&vardata2, &isdefault2);
2327
2328	opfuncoid = get_opcode(operator);
2329
2330	memset(&sslot1, 0, sizeof(sslot1));
2331	memset(&sslot2, 0, sizeof(sslot2));
2332
2333	/*
2334	 * There is no use in fetching one side's MCVs if we lack MCVs for the
2335	 * other side, so do a quick check to verify that both stats exist.
2336	 */
2337	get_mcv_stats = (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata1.statsTuple) &&
2338					 HeapTupleIsValid(vardata2.statsTuple) &&
2339					 get_attstatsslot(&sslot1, vardata1.statsTuple,
2340									  STATISTIC_KIND_MCV, InvalidOid,
2341									  0) &&
2342					 get_attstatsslot(&sslot2, vardata2.statsTuple,
2343									  STATISTIC_KIND_MCV, InvalidOid,
2344									  0));
2345
2346	if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata1.statsTuple))
2347	{
2348		/* note we allow use of nullfrac regardless of security check */
2349		stats1 = (Form_pg_statistic) GETSTRUCT(vardata1.statsTuple);
2350		if (get_mcv_stats &&
2351			statistic_proc_security_check(&vardata1, opfuncoid))
2352			have_mcvs1 = get_attstatsslot(&sslot1, vardata1.statsTuple,
2353										  STATISTIC_KIND_MCV, InvalidOid,
2354										  ATTSTATSSLOT_VALUES | ATTSTATSSLOT_NUMBERS);
2355	}
2356
2357	if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata2.statsTuple))
2358	{
2359		/* note we allow use of nullfrac regardless of security check */
2360		stats2 = (Form_pg_statistic) GETSTRUCT(vardata2.statsTuple);
2361		if (get_mcv_stats &&
2362			statistic_proc_security_check(&vardata2, opfuncoid))
2363			have_mcvs2 = get_attstatsslot(&sslot2, vardata2.statsTuple,
2364										  STATISTIC_KIND_MCV, InvalidOid,
2365										  ATTSTATSSLOT_VALUES | ATTSTATSSLOT_NUMBERS);
2366	}
2367
2368	/* We need to compute the inner-join selectivity in all cases */
2369	selec_inner = eqjoinsel_inner(opfuncoid, collation,
2370								  &vardata1, &vardata2,
2371								  nd1, nd2,
2372								  isdefault1, isdefault2,
2373								  &sslot1, &sslot2,
2374								  stats1, stats2,
2375								  have_mcvs1, have_mcvs2);
2376
2377	switch (sjinfo->jointype)
2378	{
2379		case JOIN_INNER:
2380		case JOIN_LEFT:
2381		case JOIN_FULL:
2382			selec = selec_inner;
2383			break;
2384		case JOIN_SEMI:
2385		case JOIN_ANTI:
2386
2387			/*
2388			 * Look up the join's inner relation.  min_righthand is sufficient
2389			 * information because neither SEMI nor ANTI joins permit any
2390			 * reassociation into or out of their RHS, so the righthand will
2391			 * always be exactly that set of rels.
2392			 */
2393			inner_rel = find_join_input_rel(root, sjinfo->min_righthand);
2394
2395			if (!join_is_reversed)
2396				selec = eqjoinsel_semi(opfuncoid, collation,
2397									   &vardata1, &vardata2,
2398									   nd1, nd2,
2399									   isdefault1, isdefault2,
2400									   &sslot1, &sslot2,
2401									   stats1, stats2,
2402									   have_mcvs1, have_mcvs2,
2403									   inner_rel);
2404			else
2405			{
2406				Oid			commop = get_commutator(operator);
2407				Oid			commopfuncoid = OidIsValid(commop) ? get_opcode(commop) : InvalidOid;
2408
2409				selec = eqjoinsel_semi(commopfuncoid, collation,
2410									   &vardata2, &vardata1,
2411									   nd2, nd1,
2412									   isdefault2, isdefault1,
2413									   &sslot2, &sslot1,
2414									   stats2, stats1,
2415									   have_mcvs2, have_mcvs1,
2416									   inner_rel);
2417			}
2418
2419			/*
2420			 * We should never estimate the output of a semijoin to be more
2421			 * rows than we estimate for an inner join with the same input
2422			 * rels and join condition; it's obviously impossible for that to
2423			 * happen.  The former estimate is N1 * Ssemi while the latter is
2424			 * N1 * N2 * Sinner, so we may clamp Ssemi <= N2 * Sinner.  Doing
2425			 * this is worthwhile because of the shakier estimation rules we
2426			 * use in eqjoinsel_semi, particularly in cases where it has to
2427			 * punt entirely.
2428			 */
2429			selec = Min(selec, inner_rel->rows * selec_inner);
2430			break;
2431		default:
2432			/* other values not expected here */
2433			elog(ERROR, "unrecognized join type: %d",
2434				 (int) sjinfo->jointype);
2435			selec = 0;			/* keep compiler quiet */
2436			break;
2437	}
2438
2439	free_attstatsslot(&sslot1);
2440	free_attstatsslot(&sslot2);
2441
2442	ReleaseVariableStats(vardata1);
2443	ReleaseVariableStats(vardata2);
2444
2445	CLAMP_PROBABILITY(selec);
2446
2447	PG_RETURN_FLOAT8((float8) selec);
2448}
2449
2450/*
2451 * eqjoinsel_inner --- eqjoinsel for normal inner join
2452 *
2453 * We also use this for LEFT/FULL outer joins; it's not presently clear
2454 * that it's worth trying to distinguish them here.
2455 */
2456static double
2457eqjoinsel_inner(Oid opfuncoid, Oid collation,
2458				VariableStatData *vardata1, VariableStatData *vardata2,
2459				double nd1, double nd2,
2460				bool isdefault1, bool isdefault2,
2461				AttStatsSlot *sslot1, AttStatsSlot *sslot2,
2462				Form_pg_statistic stats1, Form_pg_statistic stats2,
2463				bool have_mcvs1, bool have_mcvs2)
2464{
2465	double		selec;
2466
2467	if (have_mcvs1 && have_mcvs2)
2468	{
2469		/*
2470		 * We have most-common-value lists for both relations.  Run through
2471		 * the lists to see which MCVs actually join to each other with the
2472		 * given operator.  This allows us to determine the exact join
2473		 * selectivity for the portion of the relations represented by the MCV
2474		 * lists.  We still have to estimate for the remaining population, but
2475		 * in a skewed distribution this gives us a big leg up in accuracy.
2476		 * For motivation see the analysis in Y. Ioannidis and S.
2477		 * Christodoulakis, "On the propagation of errors in the size of join
2478		 * results", Technical Report 1018, Computer Science Dept., University
2479		 * of Wisconsin, Madison, March 1991 (available from ftp.cs.wisc.edu).
2480		 */
2481		LOCAL_FCINFO(fcinfo, 2);
2482		FmgrInfo	eqproc;
2483		bool	   *hasmatch1;
2484		bool	   *hasmatch2;
