2485		double		nullfrac1 = stats1->stanullfrac;
2486		double		nullfrac2 = stats2->stanullfrac;
2487		double		matchprodfreq,
2488					matchfreq1,
2489					matchfreq2,
2490					unmatchfreq1,
2491					unmatchfreq2,
2492					otherfreq1,
2493					otherfreq2,
2494					totalsel1,
2495					totalsel2;
2496		int			i,
2497					nmatches;
2498
2499		fmgr_info(opfuncoid, &eqproc);
2500
2501		/*
2502		 * Save a few cycles by setting up the fcinfo struct just once. Using
2503		 * FunctionCallInvoke directly also avoids failure if the eqproc
2504		 * returns NULL, though really equality functions should never do
2505		 * that.
2506		 */
2507		InitFunctionCallInfoData(*fcinfo, &eqproc, 2, collation,
2508								 NULL, NULL);
2509		fcinfo->args[0].isnull = false;
2510		fcinfo->args[1].isnull = false;
2511
2512		hasmatch1 = (bool *) palloc0(sslot1->nvalues * sizeof(bool));
2513		hasmatch2 = (bool *) palloc0(sslot2->nvalues * sizeof(bool));
2514
2515		/*
2516		 * Note we assume that each MCV will match at most one member of the
2517		 * other MCV list.  If the operator isn't really equality, there could
2518		 * be multiple matches --- but we don't look for them, both for speed
2519		 * and because the math wouldn't add up...
2520		 */
2521		matchprodfreq = 0.0;
2522		nmatches = 0;
2523		for (i = 0; i < sslot1->nvalues; i++)
2524		{
2525			int			j;
2526
2527			fcinfo->args[0].value = sslot1->values[i];
2528
2529			for (j = 0; j < sslot2->nvalues; j++)
2530			{
2531				Datum		fresult;
2532
2533				if (hasmatch2[j])
2534					continue;
2535				fcinfo->args[1].value = sslot2->values[j];
2536				fcinfo->isnull = false;
2537				fresult = FunctionCallInvoke(fcinfo);
2538				if (!fcinfo->isnull && DatumGetBool(fresult))
2539				{
2540					hasmatch1[i] = hasmatch2[j] = true;
2541					matchprodfreq += sslot1->numbers[i] * sslot2->numbers[j];
2542					nmatches++;
2543					break;
2544				}
2545			}
2546		}
2547		CLAMP_PROBABILITY(matchprodfreq);
2548		/* Sum up frequencies of matched and unmatched MCVs */
2549		matchfreq1 = unmatchfreq1 = 0.0;
2550		for (i = 0; i < sslot1->nvalues; i++)
2551		{
2552			if (hasmatch1[i])
2553				matchfreq1 += sslot1->numbers[i];
2554			else
2555				unmatchfreq1 += sslot1->numbers[i];
2556		}
2557		CLAMP_PROBABILITY(matchfreq1);
2558		CLAMP_PROBABILITY(unmatchfreq1);
2559		matchfreq2 = unmatchfreq2 = 0.0;
2560		for (i = 0; i < sslot2->nvalues; i++)
2561		{
2562			if (hasmatch2[i])
2563				matchfreq2 += sslot2->numbers[i];
2564			else
2565				unmatchfreq2 += sslot2->numbers[i];
2566		}
2567		CLAMP_PROBABILITY(matchfreq2);
2568		CLAMP_PROBABILITY(unmatchfreq2);
2569		pfree(hasmatch1);
2570		pfree(hasmatch2);
2571
2572		/*
2573		 * Compute total frequency of non-null values that are not in the MCV
2574		 * lists.
2575		 */
2576		otherfreq1 = 1.0 - nullfrac1 - matchfreq1 - unmatchfreq1;
2577		otherfreq2 = 1.0 - nullfrac2 - matchfreq2 - unmatchfreq2;
2578		CLAMP_PROBABILITY(otherfreq1);
2579		CLAMP_PROBABILITY(otherfreq2);
2580
2581		/*
2582		 * We can estimate the total selectivity from the point of view of
2583		 * relation 1 as: the known selectivity for matched MCVs, plus
2584		 * unmatched MCVs that are assumed to match against random members of
2585		 * relation 2's non-MCV population, plus non-MCV values that are
2586		 * assumed to match against random members of relation 2's unmatched
2587		 * MCVs plus non-MCV values.
2588		 */
2589		totalsel1 = matchprodfreq;
2590		if (nd2 > sslot2->nvalues)
2591			totalsel1 += unmatchfreq1 * otherfreq2 / (nd2 - sslot2->nvalues);
2592		if (nd2 > nmatches)
2593			totalsel1 += otherfreq1 * (otherfreq2 + unmatchfreq2) /
2594				(nd2 - nmatches);
2595		/* Same estimate from the point of view of relation 2. */
2596		totalsel2 = matchprodfreq;
2597		if (nd1 > sslot1->nvalues)
2598			totalsel2 += unmatchfreq2 * otherfreq1 / (nd1 - sslot1->nvalues);
2599		if (nd1 > nmatches)
2600			totalsel2 += otherfreq2 * (otherfreq1 + unmatchfreq1) /
2601				(nd1 - nmatches);
2602
2603		/*
2604		 * Use the smaller of the two estimates.  This can be justified in
2605		 * essentially the same terms as given below for the no-stats case: to
2606		 * a first approximation, we are estimating from the point of view of
2607		 * the relation with smaller nd.
2608		 */
2609		selec = (totalsel1 < totalsel2) ? totalsel1 : totalsel2;
2610	}
2611	else
2612	{
2613		/*
2614		 * We do not have MCV lists for both sides.  Estimate the join
2615		 * selectivity as MIN(1/nd1,1/nd2)*(1-nullfrac1)*(1-nullfrac2). This
2616		 * is plausible if we assume that the join operator is strict and the
2617		 * non-null values are about equally distributed: a given non-null
2618		 * tuple of rel1 will join to either zero or N2*(1-nullfrac2)/nd2 rows
2619		 * of rel2, so total join rows are at most
2620		 * N1*(1-nullfrac1)*N2*(1-nullfrac2)/nd2 giving a join selectivity of
2621		 * not more than (1-nullfrac1)*(1-nullfrac2)/nd2. By the same logic it
2622		 * is not more than (1-nullfrac1)*(1-nullfrac2)/nd1, so the expression
2623		 * with MIN() is an upper bound.  Using the MIN() means we estimate
2624		 * from the point of view of the relation with smaller nd (since the
2625		 * larger nd is determining the MIN).  It is reasonable to assume that
2626		 * most tuples in this rel will have join partners, so the bound is
2627		 * probably reasonably tight and should be taken as-is.
2628		 *
2629		 * XXX Can we be smarter if we have an MCV list for just one side? It
2630		 * seems that if we assume equal distribution for the other side, we
2631		 * end up with the same answer anyway.
2632		 */
2633		double		nullfrac1 = stats1 ? stats1->stanullfrac : 0.0;
2634		double		nullfrac2 = stats2 ? stats2->stanullfrac : 0.0;
2635
2636		selec = (1.0 - nullfrac1) * (1.0 - nullfrac2);
2637		if (nd1 > nd2)
2638			selec /= nd1;
2639		else
2640			selec /= nd2;
2641	}
2642
2643	return selec;
2644}
2645
2646/*
2647 * eqjoinsel_semi --- eqjoinsel for semi join
2648 *
2649 * (Also used for anti join, which we are supposed to estimate the same way.)
2650 * Caller has ensured that vardata1 is the LHS variable.
2651 * Unlike eqjoinsel_inner, we have to cope with opfuncoid being InvalidOid.
2652 */
2653static double
2654eqjoinsel_semi(Oid opfuncoid, Oid collation,
2655			   VariableStatData *vardata1, VariableStatData *vardata2,
2656			   double nd1, double nd2,
2657			   bool isdefault1, bool isdefault2,
2658			   AttStatsSlot *sslot1, AttStatsSlot *sslot2,
2659			   Form_pg_statistic stats1, Form_pg_statistic stats2,
2660			   bool have_mcvs1, bool have_mcvs2,
2661			   RelOptInfo *inner_rel)
2662{
2663	double		selec;
2664
2665	/*
2666	 * We clamp nd2 to be not more than what we estimate the inner relation's
2667	 * size to be.  This is intuitively somewhat reasonable since obviously
2668	 * there can't be more than that many distinct values coming from the
2669	 * inner rel.  The reason for the asymmetry (ie, that we don't clamp nd1
2670	 * likewise) is that this is the only pathway by which restriction clauses
2671	 * applied to the inner rel will affect the join result size estimate,
2672	 * since set_joinrel_size_estimates will multiply SEMI/ANTI selectivity by
2673	 * only the outer rel's size.  If we clamped nd1 we'd be double-counting
2674	 * the selectivity of outer-rel restrictions.
2675	 *
2676	 * We can apply this clamping both with respect to the base relation from
2677	 * which the join variable comes (if there is just one), and to the
2678	 * immediate inner input relation of the current join.
2679	 *
2680	 * If we clamp, we can treat nd2 as being a non-default estimate; it's not
2681	 * great, maybe, but it didn't come out of nowhere either.  This is most
2682	 * helpful when the inner relation is empty and consequently has no stats.
2683	 */
2684	if (vardata2->rel)
2685	{
2686		if (nd2 >= vardata2->rel->rows)
2687		{
2688			nd2 = vardata2->rel->rows;
2689			isdefault2 = false;
2690		}
2691	}
2692	if (nd2 >= inner_rel->rows)
2693	{
2694		nd2 = inner_rel->rows;
2695		isdefault2 = false;
2696	}
2697
2698	if (have_mcvs1 && have_mcvs2 && OidIsValid(opfuncoid))
2699	{
2700		/*
2701		 * We have most-common-value lists for both relations.  Run through
2702		 * the lists to see which MCVs actually join to each other with the
2703		 * given operator.  This allows us to determine the exact join
2704		 * selectivity for the portion of the relations represented by the MCV
2705		 * lists.  We still have to estimate for the remaining population, but
2706		 * in a skewed distribution this gives us a big leg up in accuracy.
2707		 */
2708		LOCAL_FCINFO(fcinfo, 2);
2709		FmgrInfo	eqproc;
2710		bool	   *hasmatch1;
2711		bool	   *hasmatch2;
2712		double		nullfrac1 = stats1->stanullfrac;
2713		double		matchfreq1,
2714					uncertainfrac,
2715					uncertain;
2716		int			i,
2717					nmatches,
2718					clamped_nvalues2;
2719
2720		/*
2721		 * The clamping above could have resulted in nd2 being less than
2722		 * sslot2->nvalues; in which case, we assume that precisely the nd2
2723		 * most common values in the relation will appear in the join input,
2724		 * and so compare to only the first nd2 members of the MCV list.  Of
2725		 * course this is frequently wrong, but it's the best bet we can make.
2726		 */
2727		clamped_nvalues2 = Min(sslot2->nvalues, nd2);
2728
2729		fmgr_info(opfuncoid, &eqproc);
2730
2731		/*
2732		 * Save a few cycles by setting up the fcinfo struct just once. Using
2733		 * FunctionCallInvoke directly also avoids failure if the eqproc
2734		 * returns NULL, though really equality functions should never do
2735		 * that.
2736		 */
2737		InitFunctionCallInfoData(*fcinfo, &eqproc, 2, collation,
2738								 NULL, NULL);
2739		fcinfo->args[0].isnull = false;
2740		fcinfo->args[1].isnull = false;
2741
2742		hasmatch1 = (bool *) palloc0(sslot1->nvalues * sizeof(bool));
2743		hasmatch2 = (bool *) palloc0(clamped_nvalues2 * sizeof(bool));
2744
2745		/*
2746		 * Note we assume that each MCV will match at most one member of the
2747		 * other MCV list.  If the operator isn't really equality, there could
2748		 * be multiple matches --- but we don't look for them, both for speed
2749		 * and because the math wouldn't add up...
2750		 */
2751		nmatches = 0;
2752		for (i = 0; i < sslot1->nvalues; i++)
2753		{
2754			int			j;
2755
2756			fcinfo->args[0].value = sslot1->values[i];
2757
2758			for (j = 0; j < clamped_nvalues2; j++)
2759			{
2760				Datum		fresult;
2761
2762				if (hasmatch2[j])
2763					continue;
2764				fcinfo->args[1].value = sslot2->values[j];
2765				fcinfo->isnull = false;
2766				fresult = FunctionCallInvoke(fcinfo);
2767				if (!fcinfo->isnull && DatumGetBool(fresult))
2768				{
2769					hasmatch1[i] = hasmatch2[j] = true;
2770					nmatches++;
2771					break;
2772				}
2773			}
2774		}
2775		/* Sum up frequencies of matched MCVs */
2776		matchfreq1 = 0.0;
2777		for (i = 0; i < sslot1->nvalues; i++)
2778		{
2779			if (hasmatch1[i])
2780				matchfreq1 += sslot1->numbers[i];
2781		}
2782		CLAMP_PROBABILITY(matchfreq1);
2783		pfree(hasmatch1);
2784		pfree(hasmatch2);
2785
2786		/*
2787		 * Now we need to estimate the fraction of relation 1 that has at
2788		 * least one join partner.  We know for certain that the matched MCVs
2789		 * do, so that gives us a lower bound, but we're really in the dark
2790		 * about everything else.  Our crude approach is: if nd1 <= nd2 then
2791		 * assume all non-null rel1 rows have join partners, else assume for
2792		 * the uncertain rows that a fraction nd2/nd1 have join partners. We
2793		 * can discount the known-matched MCVs from the distinct-values counts
2794		 * before doing the division.
2795		 *
2796		 * Crude as the above is, it's completely useless if we don't have
2797		 * reliable ndistinct values for both sides.  Hence, if either nd1 or
2798		 * nd2 is default, punt and assume half of the uncertain rows have
2799		 * join partners.
2800		 */
2801		if (!isdefault1 && !isdefault2)
2802		{
2803			nd1 -= nmatches;
2804			nd2 -= nmatches;
2805			if (nd1 <= nd2 || nd2 < 0)
2806				uncertainfrac = 1.0;
2807			else
2808				uncertainfrac = nd2 / nd1;
2809		}
2810		else
2811			uncertainfrac = 0.5;
2812		uncertain = 1.0 - matchfreq1 - nullfrac1;
2813		CLAMP_PROBABILITY(uncertain);
2814		selec = matchfreq1 + uncertainfrac * uncertain;
2815	}
2816	else
2817	{
2818		/*
2819		 * Without MCV lists for both sides, we can only use the heuristic
2820		 * about nd1 vs nd2.
2821		 */
2822		double		nullfrac1 = stats1 ? stats1->stanullfrac : 0.0;
2823
2824		if (!isdefault1 && !isdefault2)
2825		{
2826			if (nd1 <= nd2 || nd2 < 0)
2827				selec = 1.0 - nullfrac1;
2828			else
2829				selec = (nd2 / nd1) * (1.0 - nullfrac1);
2830		}
2831		else
2832			selec = 0.5 * (1.0 - nullfrac1);
2833	}
2834
2835	return selec;
2836}
2837
2838/*
2839 *		neqjoinsel		- Join selectivity of "!="
2840 */
2841Datum
2842neqjoinsel(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
2843{
2844	PlannerInfo *root = (PlannerInfo *) PG_GETARG_POINTER(0);
2845	Oid			operator = PG_GETARG_OID(1);
2846	List	   *args = (List *) PG_GETARG_POINTER(2);
2847	JoinType	jointype = (JoinType) PG_GETARG_INT16(3);
2848	SpecialJoinInfo *sjinfo = (SpecialJoinInfo *) PG_GETARG_POINTER(4);
2849	Oid			collation = PG_GET_COLLATION();
2850	float8		result;
2851
2852	if (jointype == JOIN_SEMI || jointype == JOIN_ANTI)
2853	{
2854		/*
2855		 * For semi-joins, if there is more than one distinct value in the RHS
2856		 * relation then every non-null LHS row must find a row to join since
2857		 * it can only be equal to one of them.  We'll assume that there is
2858		 * always more than one distinct RHS value for the sake of stability,
2859		 * though in theory we could have special cases for empty RHS
2860		 * (selectivity = 0) and single-distinct-value RHS (selectivity =
2861		 * fraction of LHS that has the same value as the single RHS value).
2862		 *
2863		 * For anti-joins, if we use the same assumption that there is more
2864		 * than one distinct key in the RHS relation, then every non-null LHS
2865		 * row must be suppressed by the anti-join.
2866		 *
2867		 * So either way, the selectivity estimate should be 1 - nullfrac.
2868		 */
2869		VariableStatData leftvar;
2870		VariableStatData rightvar;
2871		bool		reversed;
2872		HeapTuple	statsTuple;
2873		double		nullfrac;
2874
2875		get_join_variables(root, args, sjinfo, &leftvar, &rightvar, &reversed);
2876		statsTuple = reversed ? rightvar.statsTuple : leftvar.statsTuple;
2877		if (HeapTupleIsValid(statsTuple))
2878			nullfrac = ((Form_pg_statistic) GETSTRUCT(statsTuple))->stanullfrac;
2879		else
2880			nullfrac = 0.0;
2881		ReleaseVariableStats(leftvar);
2882		ReleaseVariableStats(rightvar);
2883
2884		result = 1.0 - nullfrac;
2885	}
2886	else
2887	{
2888		/*
2889		 * We want 1 - eqjoinsel() where the equality operator is the one
2890		 * associated with this != operator, that is, its negator.
2891		 */
2892		Oid			eqop = get_negator(operator);
2893
2894		if (eqop)
2895		{
2896			result =
2897				DatumGetFloat8(DirectFunctionCall5Coll(eqjoinsel,
2898													   collation,
2899													   PointerGetDatum(root),
2900													   ObjectIdGetDatum(eqop),
2901													   PointerGetDatum(args),
2902													   Int16GetDatum(jointype),
2903													   PointerGetDatum(sjinfo)));
2904		}
2905		else
2906		{
2907			/* Use default selectivity (should we raise an error instead?) */
2908			result = DEFAULT_EQ_SEL;
2909		}
2910		result = 1.0 - result;
2911	}
2912
2913	PG_RETURN_FLOAT8(result);
2914}
2915
2916/*
2917 *		scalarltjoinsel - Join selectivity of "<" for scalars
2918 */
2919Datum
2920scalarltjoinsel(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
2921{
2922	PG_RETURN_FLOAT8(DEFAULT_INEQ_SEL);
2923}
2924
2925/*
2926 *		scalarlejoinsel - Join selectivity of "<=" for scalars
2927 */
2928Datum
2929scalarlejoinsel(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
2930{
2931	PG_RETURN_FLOAT8(DEFAULT_INEQ_SEL);
2932}
2933
2934/*
2935 *		scalargtjoinsel - Join selectivity of ">" for scalars
2936 */
2937Datum
2938scalargtjoinsel(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
2939{
2940	PG_RETURN_FLOAT8(DEFAULT_INEQ_SEL);
2941}
2942
2943/*
2944 *		scalargejoinsel - Join selectivity of ">=" for scalars
2945 */
2946Datum
2947scalargejoinsel(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
2948{
2949	PG_RETURN_FLOAT8(DEFAULT_INEQ_SEL);
2950}
2951
2952
2953/*
2954 * mergejoinscansel			- Scan selectivity of merge join.
2955 *
2956 * A merge join will stop as soon as it exhausts either input stream.
2957 * Therefore, if we can estimate the ranges of both input variables,
2958 * we can estimate how much of the input will actually be read.  This
2959 * can have a considerable impact on the cost when using indexscans.
2960 *
2961 * Also, we can estimate how much of each input has to be read before the
2962 * first join pair is found, which will affect the join's startup time.
2963 *
2964 * clause should be a clause already known to be mergejoinable.  opfamily,
2965 * cmptype, and nulls_first specify the sort ordering being used.
2966 *
2967 * The outputs are:
2968 *		*leftstart is set to the fraction of the left-hand variable expected
2969 *		 to be scanned before the first join pair is found (0 to 1).
2970 *		*leftend is set to the fraction of the left-hand variable expected
2971 *		 to be scanned before the join terminates (0 to 1).
2972 *		*rightstart, *rightend similarly for the right-hand variable.
2973 */
2974void
2975mergejoinscansel(PlannerInfo *root, Node *clause,
2976				 Oid opfamily, CompareType cmptype, bool nulls_first,
2977				 Selectivity *leftstart, Selectivity *leftend,
2978				 Selectivity *rightstart, Selectivity *rightend)
2979{
2980	Node	   *left,
2981			   *right;
2982	VariableStatData leftvar,
2983				rightvar;
2984	Oid			opmethod;
2985	int			op_strategy;
2986	Oid			op_lefttype;
2987	Oid			op_righttype;
2988	Oid			opno,
2989				collation,
2990				lsortop,
2991				rsortop,
2992				lstatop,
2993				rstatop,
2994				ltop,
2995				leop,
2996				revltop,
2997				revleop;
2998	StrategyNumber ltstrat,
2999				lestrat,
3000				gtstrat,
3001				gestrat;
3002	bool		isgt;
3003	Datum		leftmin,
3004				leftmax,
3005				rightmin,
3006				rightmax;
3007	double		selec;
3008
3009	/* Set default results if we can't figure anything out. */
3010	/* XXX should default "start" fraction be a bit more than 0? */
3011	*leftstart = *rightstart = 0.0;
3012	*leftend = *rightend = 1.0;
3013
3014	/* Deconstruct the merge clause */
3015	if (!is_opclause(clause))
3016		return;					/* shouldn't happen */
3017	opno = ((OpExpr *) clause)->opno;
3018	collation = ((OpExpr *) clause)->inputcollid;
3019	left = get_leftop((Expr *) clause);
3020	right = get_rightop((Expr *) clause);
3021	if (!right)
3022		return;					/* shouldn't happen */
3023
3024	/* Look for stats for the inputs */
3025	examine_variable(root, left, 0, &leftvar);
3026	examine_variable(root, right, 0, &rightvar);
3027
3028	opmethod = get_opfamily_method(opfamily);
3029
3030	/* Extract the operator's declared left/right datatypes */
3031	get_op_opfamily_properties(opno, opfamily, false,
3032							   &op_strategy,
3033							   &op_lefttype,
3034							   &op_righttype);
3035	Assert(IndexAmTranslateStrategy(op_strategy, opmethod, opfamily, true) == COMPARE_EQ);
3036
3037	/*
3038	 * Look up the various operators we need.  If we don't find them all, it
3039	 * probably means the opfamily is broken, but we just fail silently.
3040	 *
3041	 * Note: we expect that pg_statistic histograms will be sorted by the '<'
3042	 * operator, regardless of which sort direction we are considering.
3043	 */
3044	switch (cmptype)
3045	{
3046		case COMPARE_LT:
3047			isgt = false;
3048			ltstrat = IndexAmTranslateCompareType(COMPARE_LT, opmethod, opfamily, true);
3049			lestrat = IndexAmTranslateCompareType(COMPARE_LE, opmethod, opfamily, true);
3050			if (op_lefttype == op_righttype)
3051			{
3052				/* easy case */
3053				ltop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3054										   op_lefttype, op_righttype,
3055										   ltstrat);
3056				leop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3057										   op_lefttype, op_righttype,
3058										   lestrat);
3059				lsortop = ltop;
3060				rsortop = ltop;
3061				lstatop = lsortop;
3062				rstatop = rsortop;
3063				revltop = ltop;
3064				revleop = leop;
3065			}
3066			else
3067			{
3068				ltop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3069										   op_lefttype, op_righttype,
3070										   ltstrat);
3071				leop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3072										   op_lefttype, op_righttype,
3073										   lestrat);
3074				lsortop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3075											  op_lefttype, op_lefttype,
3076											  ltstrat);
3077				rsortop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3078											  op_righttype, op_righttype,
3079											  ltstrat);
3080				lstatop = lsortop;
3081				rstatop = rsortop;
3082				revltop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3083											  op_righttype, op_lefttype,
3084											  ltstrat);
3085				revleop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3086											  op_righttype, op_lefttype,
3087											  lestrat);
3088			}
3089			break;
3090		case COMPARE_GT:
3091			/* descending-order case */
3092			isgt = true;
3093			ltstrat = IndexAmTranslateCompareType(COMPARE_LT, opmethod, opfamily, true);
3094			gtstrat = IndexAmTranslateCompareType(COMPARE_GT, opmethod, opfamily, true);
3095			gestrat = IndexAmTranslateCompareType(COMPARE_GE, opmethod, opfamily, true);
3096			if (op_lefttype == op_righttype)
3097			{
3098				/* easy case */
3099				ltop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3100										   op_lefttype, op_righttype,
3101										   gtstrat);
3102				leop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3103										   op_lefttype, op_righttype,
3104										   gestrat);
3105				lsortop = ltop;
3106				rsortop = ltop;
3107				lstatop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3108											  op_lefttype, op_lefttype,
3109											  ltstrat);
3110				rstatop = lstatop;
3111				revltop = ltop;
3112				revleop = leop;
3113			}
3114			else
3115			{
3116				ltop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3117										   op_lefttype, op_righttype,
3118										   gtstrat);
3119				leop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3120										   op_lefttype, op_righttype,
3121										   gestrat);
3122				lsortop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3123											  op_lefttype, op_lefttype,
3124											  gtstrat);
3125				rsortop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3126											  op_righttype, op_righttype,
3127											  gtstrat);
3128				lstatop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3129											  op_lefttype, op_lefttype,
3130											  ltstrat);
3131				rstatop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3132											  op_righttype, op_righttype,
3133											  ltstrat);
3134				revltop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3135											  op_righttype, op_lefttype,
3136											  gtstrat);
3137				revleop = get_opfamily_member(opfamily,
3138											  op_righttype, op_lefttype,
3139											  gestrat);
3140			}
3141			break;
3142		default:
3143			goto fail;			/* shouldn't get here */
3144	}
3145
3146	if (!OidIsValid(lsortop) ||
3147		!OidIsValid(rsortop) ||
3148		!OidIsValid(lstatop) ||
3149		!OidIsValid(rstatop) ||
3150		!OidIsValid(ltop) ||
3151		!OidIsValid(leop) ||
3152		!OidIsValid(revltop) ||
3153		!OidIsValid(revleop))
3154		goto fail;				/* insufficient info in catalogs */
3155
3156	/* Try to get ranges of both inputs */
3157	if (!isgt)
3158	{
3159		if (!get_variable_range(root, &leftvar, lstatop, collation,
3160								&leftmin, &leftmax))
3161			goto fail;			/* no range available from stats */
3162		if (!get_variable_range(root, &rightvar, rstatop, collation,
3163								&rightmin, &rightmax))
3164			goto fail;			/* no range available from stats */
3165	}
3166	else
3167	{
3168		/* need to swap the max and min */
3169		if (!get_variable_range(root, &leftvar, lstatop, collation,
3170								&leftmax, &leftmin))
3171			goto fail;			/* no range available from stats */
3172		if (!get_variable_range(root, &rightvar, rstatop, collation,
3173								&rightmax, &rightmin))
3174			goto fail;			/* no range available from stats */
3175	}
3176
3177	/*
3178	 * Now, the fraction of the left variable that will be scanned is the
3179	 * fraction that's <= the right-side maximum value.  But only believe
3180	 * non-default estimates, else stick with our 1.0.
3181	 */
3182	selec = scalarineqsel(root, leop, isgt, true, collation, &leftvar,
3183						  rightmax, op_righttype);
3184	if (selec != DEFAULT_INEQ_SEL)
3185		*leftend = selec;
3186
3187	/* And similarly for the right variable. */
3188	selec = scalarineqsel(root, revleop, isgt, true, collation, &rightvar,
3189						  leftmax, op_lefttype);
3190	if (selec != DEFAULT_INEQ_SEL)
3191		*rightend = selec;
3192
3193	/*
3194	 * Only one of the two "end" fractions can really be less than 1.0;
3195	 * believe the smaller estimate and reset the other one to exactly 1.0. If
3196	 * we get exactly equal estimates (as can easily happen with self-joins),
3197	 * believe neither.
3198	 */
3199	if (*leftend > *rightend)
3200		*leftend = 1.0;
3201	else if (*leftend < *rightend)
3202		*rightend = 1.0;
3203	else
3204		*leftend = *rightend = 1.0;
3205
3206	/*
3207	 * Also, the fraction of the left variable that will be scanned before the
3208	 * first join pair is found is the fraction that's < the right-side
3209	 * minimum value.  But only believe non-default estimates, else stick with
3210	 * our own default.
3211	 */
3212	selec = scalarineqsel(root, ltop, isgt, false, collation, &leftvar,
3213						  rightmin, op_righttype);
3214	if (selec != DEFAULT_INEQ_SEL)
3215		*leftstart = selec;
3216
3217	/* And similarly for the right variable. */
3218	selec = scalarineqsel(root, revltop, isgt, false, collation, &rightvar,
3219						  leftmin, op_lefttype);
3220	if (selec != DEFAULT_INEQ_SEL)
3221		*rightstart = selec;
3222
3223	/*
3224	 * Only one of the two "start" fractions can really be more than zero;
3225	 * believe the larger estimate and reset the other one to exactly 0.0. If
3226	 * we get exactly equal estimates (as can easily happen with self-joins),
3227	 * believe neither.
3228	 */
3229	if (*leftstart < *rightstart)
3230		*leftstart = 0.0;
3231	else if (*leftstart > *rightstart)
3232		*rightstart = 0.0;
3233	else
3234		*leftstart = *rightstart = 0.0;
3235
3236	/*
3237	 * If the sort order is nulls-first, we're going to have to skip over any
3238	 * nulls too.  These would not have been counted by scalarineqsel, and we
3239	 * can safely add in this fraction regardless of whether we believe
3240	 * scalarineqsel's results or not.  But be sure to clamp the sum to 1.0!
3241	 */
3242	if (nulls_first)
3243	{
3244		Form_pg_statistic stats;
3245
3246		if (HeapTupleIsValid(leftvar.statsTuple))
3247		{
3248			stats = (Form_pg_statistic) GETSTRUCT(leftvar.statsTuple);
3249			*leftstart += stats->stanullfrac;
3250			CLAMP_PROBABILITY(*leftstart);
3251			*leftend += stats->stanullfrac;
3252			CLAMP_PROBABILITY(*leftend);
3253		}
3254		if (HeapTupleIsValid(rightvar.statsTuple))
3255		{
3256			stats = (Form_pg_statistic) GETSTRUCT(rightvar.statsTuple);
3257			*rightstart += stats->stanullfrac;
3258			CLAMP_PROBABILITY(*rightstart);
3259			*rightend += stats->stanullfrac;
3260			CLAMP_PROBABILITY(*rightend);
3261		}
3262	}
3263
3264	/* Disbelieve start >= end, just in case that can happen */
3265	if (*leftstart >= *leftend)
3266	{
3267		*leftstart = 0.0;
3268		*leftend = 1.0;
3269	}
3270	if (*rightstart >= *rightend)
3271	{
3272		*rightstart = 0.0;
3273		*rightend = 1.0;
3274	}
3275
3276fail:
3277	ReleaseVariableStats(leftvar);
3278	ReleaseVariableStats(rightvar);
3279}
3280
3281
3282/*
3283 *	matchingsel -- generic matching-operator selectivity support
3284 *
3285 * Use these for any operators that (a) are on data types for which we collect
3286 * standard statistics, and (b) have behavior for which the default estimate
3287 * (twice DEFAULT_EQ_SEL) is sane.  Typically that is good for match-like
3288 * operators.
3289 */
3290
3291Datum
3292matchingsel(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
3293{
3294	PlannerInfo *root = (PlannerInfo *) PG_GETARG_POINTER(0);
3295	Oid			operator = PG_GETARG_OID(1);
3296	List	   *args = (List *) PG_GETARG_POINTER(2);
3297	int			varRelid = PG_GETARG_INT32(3);
3298	Oid			collation = PG_GET_COLLATION();
3299	double		selec;
3300
3301	/* Use generic restriction selectivity logic. */
3302	selec = generic_restriction_selectivity(root, operator, collation,
3303											args, varRelid,
3304											DEFAULT_MATCHING_SEL);
3305
3306	PG_RETURN_FLOAT8((float8) selec);
3307}
3308
3309Datum
3310matchingjoinsel(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
3311{
3312	/* Just punt, for the moment. */
3313	PG_RETURN_FLOAT8(DEFAULT_MATCHING_SEL);
3314}
3315
3316
3317/*
3318 * Helper routine for estimate_num_groups: add an item to a list of
3319 * GroupVarInfos, but only if it's not known equal to any of the existing
3320 * entries.
3321 */
3322typedef struct
3323{
3324	Node	   *var;			/* might be an expression, not just a Var */
3325	RelOptInfo *rel;			/* relation it belongs to */
3326	double		ndistinct;		/* # distinct values */
3327	bool		isdefault;		/* true if DEFAULT_NUM_DISTINCT was used */
3328} GroupVarInfo;
3329
3330static List *
3331add_unique_group_var(PlannerInfo *root, List *varinfos,
3332					 Node *var, VariableStatData *vardata)
3333{
3334	GroupVarInfo *varinfo;
3335	double		ndistinct;
3336	bool		isdefault;
3337	ListCell   *lc;
3338
3339	ndistinct = get_variable_numdistinct(vardata, &isdefault);
3340
3341	/*
3342	 * The nullingrels bits within the var could cause the same var to be
3343	 * counted multiple times if it's marked with different nullingrels.  They
3344	 * could also prevent us from matching the var to the expressions in
3345	 * extended statistics (see estimate_multivariate_ndistinct).  So strip
3346	 * them out first.
3347	 */
3348	var = remove_nulling_relids(var, root->outer_join_rels, NULL);
3349
3350	foreach(lc, varinfos)
3351	{
3352		varinfo = (GroupVarInfo *) lfirst(lc);
3353
3354		/* Drop exact duplicates */
3355		if (equal(var, varinfo->var))
3356			return varinfos;
3357
3358		/*
3359		 * Drop known-equal vars, but only if they belong to different
3360		 * relations (see comments for estimate_num_groups).  We aren't too
3361		 * fussy about the semantics of "equal" here.
3362		 */
3363		if (vardata->rel != varinfo->rel &&
3364			exprs_known_equal(root, var, varinfo->var, InvalidOid))
3365		{
3366			if (varinfo->ndistinct <= ndistinct)
3367			{
3368				/* Keep older item, forget new one */
3369				return varinfos;
3370			}
3371			else
3372			{
3373				/* Delete the older item */
3374				varinfos = foreach_delete_current(varinfos, lc);
3375			}
3376		}
3377	}
3378
3379	varinfo = (GroupVarInfo *) palloc(sizeof(GroupVarInfo));
3380
3381	varinfo->var = var;
3382	varinfo->rel = vardata->rel;
3383	varinfo->ndistinct = ndistinct;
3384	varinfo->isdefault = isdefault;
3385	varinfos = lappend(varinfos, varinfo);
3386	return varinfos;
3387}
3388
3389/*
3390 * estimate_num_groups		- Estimate number of groups in a grouped query
3391 *
3392 * Given a query having a GROUP BY clause, estimate how many groups there
3393 * will be --- ie, the number of distinct combinations of the GROUP BY
3394 * expressions.
3395 *
3396 * This routine is also used to estimate the number of rows emitted by
3397 * a DISTINCT filtering step; that is an isomorphic problem.  (Note:
3398 * actually, we only use it for DISTINCT when there's no grouping or
3399 * aggregation ahead of the DISTINCT.)
3400 *
3401 * Inputs:
3402 *	root - the query
3403 *	groupExprs - list of expressions being grouped by
3404 *	input_rows - number of rows estimated to arrive at the group/unique
3405 *		filter step
3406 *	pgset - NULL, or a List** pointing to a grouping set to filter the
3407 *		groupExprs against
3408 *
3409 * Outputs:
3410 *	estinfo - When passed as non-NULL, the function will set bits in the
3411 *		"flags" field in order to provide callers with additional information
3412 *		about the estimation.  Currently, we only set the SELFLAG_USED_DEFAULT
3413 *		bit if we used any default values in the estimation.
3414 *
3415 * Given the lack of any cross-correlation statistics in the system, it's
3416 * impossible to do anything really trustworthy with GROUP BY conditions
3417 * involving multiple Vars.  We should however avoid assuming the worst
3418 * case (all possible cross-product terms actually appear as groups) since
3419 * very often the grouped-by Vars are highly correlated.  Our current approach
3420 * is as follows:
3421 *	1.  Expressions yielding boolean are assumed to contribute two groups,
3422 *		independently of their content, and are ignored in the subsequent
3423 *		steps.  This is mainly because tests like "col IS NULL" break the
3424 *		heuristic used in step 2 especially badly.
3425 *	2.  Reduce the given expressions to a list of unique Vars used.  For
3426 *		example, GROUP BY a, a + b is treated the same as GROUP BY a, b.
3427 *		It is clearly correct not to count the same Var more than once.
3428 *		It is also reasonable to treat f(x) the same as x: f() cannot
3429 *		increase the number of distinct values (unless it is volatile,
3430 *		which we consider unlikely for grouping), but it probably won't
3431 *		reduce the number of distinct values much either.
3432 *		As a special case, if a GROUP BY expression can be matched to an
3433 *		expressional index for which we have statistics, then we treat the
3434 *		whole expression as though it were just a Var.
3435 *	3.  If the list contains Vars of different relations that are known equal
3436 *		due to equivalence classes, then drop all but one of the Vars from each
3437 *		known-equal set, keeping the one with smallest estimated # of values
3438 *		(since the extra values of the others can't appear in joined rows).
3439 *		Note the reason we only consider Vars of different relations is that
3440 *		if we considered ones of the same rel, we'd be double-counting the
3441 *		restriction selectivity of the equality in the next step.
3442 *	4.  For Vars within a single source rel, we multiply together the numbers
3443 *		of values, clamp to the number of rows in the rel (divided by 10 if
3444 *		more than one Var), and then multiply by a factor based on the
3445 *		selectivity of the restriction clauses for that rel.  When there's
3446 *		more than one Var, the initial product is probably too high (it's the
3447 *		worst case) but clamping to a fraction of the rel's rows seems to be a
3448 *		helpful heuristic for not letting the estimate get out of hand.  (The
3449 *		factor of 10 is derived from pre-Postgres-7.4 practice.)  The factor
3450 *		we multiply by to adjust for the restriction selectivity assumes that
3451 *		the restriction clauses are independent of the grouping, which may not
3452 *		be a valid assumption, but it's hard to do better.
3453 *	5.  If there are Vars from multiple rels, we repeat step 4 for each such
3454 *		rel, and multiply the results together.
3455 * Note that rels not containing grouped Vars are ignored completely, as are
3456 * join clauses.  Such rels cannot increase the number of groups, and we
3457 * assume such clauses do not reduce the number either (somewhat bogus,
3458 * but we don't have the info to do better).
3459 */
3460double
3461estimate_num_groups(PlannerInfo *root, List *groupExprs, double input_rows,
3462					List **pgset, EstimationInfo *estinfo)
3463{
3464	List	   *varinfos = NIL;
3465	double		srf_multiplier = 1.0;
3466	double		numdistinct;
3467	ListCell   *l;
3468	int			i;
3469
3470	/* Zero the estinfo output parameter, if non-NULL */
3471	if (estinfo != NULL)
3472		memset(estinfo, 0, sizeof(EstimationInfo));
3473
3474	/*
3475	 * We don't ever want to return an estimate of zero groups, as that tends
3476	 * to lead to division-by-zero and other unpleasantness.  The input_rows
3477	 * estimate is usually already at least 1, but clamp it just in case it
3478	 * isn't.
3479	 */
3480	input_rows = clamp_row_est(input_rows);
3481
3482	/*
3483	 * If no grouping columns, there's exactly one group.  (This can't happen
3484	 * for normal cases with GROUP BY or DISTINCT, but it is possible for
3485	 * corner cases with set operations.)
3486	 */
3487	if (groupExprs == NIL || (pgset && *pgset == NIL))
3488		return 1.0;
3489
3490	/*
3491	 * Count groups derived from boolean grouping expressions.  For other
3492	 * expressions, find the unique Vars used, treating an expression as a Var
3493	 * if we can find stats for it.  For each one, record the statistical
3494	 * estimate of number of distinct values (total in its table, without
3495	 * regard for filtering).
3496	 */
3497	numdistinct = 1.0;
3498
3499	i = 0;
3500	foreach(l, groupExprs)
3501	{
3502		Node	   *groupexpr = (Node *) lfirst(l);
3503		double		this_srf_multiplier;
3504		VariableStatData vardata;
3505		List	   *varshere;
3506		ListCell   *l2;
3507
3508		/* is expression in this grouping set? */
3509		if (pgset && !list_member_int(*pgset, i++))
3510			continue;
3511
3512		/*
3513		 * Set-returning functions in grouping columns are a bit problematic.
3514		 * The code below will effectively ignore their SRF nature and come up
3515		 * with a numdistinct estimate as though they were scalar functions.
3516		 * We compensate by scaling up the end result by the largest SRF
3517		 * rowcount estimate.  (This will be an overestimate if the SRF
3518		 * produces multiple copies of any output value, but it seems best to
3519		 * assume the SRF's outputs are distinct.  In any case, it's probably
3520		 * pointless to worry too much about this without much better
3521		 * estimates for SRF output rowcounts than we have today.)
3522		 */
3523		this_srf_multiplier = expression_returns_set_rows(root, groupexpr);
3524		if (srf_multiplier < this_srf_multiplier)
3525			srf_multiplier = this_srf_multiplier;
3526
3527		/* Short-circuit for expressions returning boolean */
3528		if (exprType(groupexpr) == BOOLOID)
3529		{
3530			numdistinct *= 2.0;
3531			continue;
3532		}
3533
3534		/*
3535		 * If examine_variable is able to deduce anything about the GROUP BY
3536		 * expression, treat it as a single variable even if it's really more
3537		 * complicated.
3538		 *
3539		 * XXX This has the consequence that if there's a statistics object on
3540		 * the expression, we don't split it into individual Vars. This
3541		 * affects our selection of statistics in
3542		 * estimate_multivariate_ndistinct, because it's probably better to
3543		 * use more accurate estimate for each expression and treat them as
3544		 * independent, than to combine estimates for the extracted variables
3545		 * when we don't know how that relates to the expressions.
3546		 */
3547		examine_variable(root, groupexpr, 0, &vardata);
3548		if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata.statsTuple) || vardata.isunique)
3549		{
3550			varinfos = add_unique_group_var(root, varinfos,
3551											groupexpr, &vardata);
3552			ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
3553			continue;
3554		}
3555		ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
3556
3557		/*
3558		 * Else pull out the component Vars.  Handle PlaceHolderVars by
3559		 * recursing into their arguments (effectively assuming that the
3560		 * PlaceHolderVar doesn't change the number of groups, which boils
3561		 * down to ignoring the possible addition of nulls to the result set).
3562		 */
3563		varshere = pull_var_clause(groupexpr,
3564								   PVC_RECURSE_AGGREGATES |
3565								   PVC_RECURSE_WINDOWFUNCS |
3566								   PVC_RECURSE_PLACEHOLDERS);
3567
3568		/*
3569		 * If we find any variable-free GROUP BY item, then either it is a
3570		 * constant (and we can ignore it) or it contains a volatile function;
3571		 * in the latter case we punt and assume that each input row will
3572		 * yield a distinct group.
3573		 */
3574		if (varshere == NIL)
3575		{
3576			if (contain_volatile_functions(groupexpr))
3577				return input_rows;
3578			continue;
3579		}
3580
3581		/*
3582		 * Else add variables to varinfos list
3583		 */
3584		foreach(l2, varshere)
3585		{
3586			Node	   *var = (Node *) lfirst(l2);
3587
3588			examine_variable(root, var, 0, &vardata);
3589			varinfos = add_unique_group_var(root, varinfos, var, &vardata);
3590			ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
3591		}
3592	}
3593
3594	/*
3595	 * If now no Vars, we must have an all-constant or all-boolean GROUP BY
3596	 * list.
3597	 */
3598	if (varinfos == NIL)
3599	{
3600		/* Apply SRF multiplier as we would do in the long path */
3601		numdistinct *= srf_multiplier;
3602		/* Round off */
3603		numdistinct = ceil(numdistinct);
3604		/* Guard against out-of-range answers */
3605		if (numdistinct > input_rows)
3606			numdistinct = input_rows;
3607		if (numdistinct < 1.0)
3608			numdistinct = 1.0;
3609		return numdistinct;
3610	}
3611
3612	/*
3613	 * Group Vars by relation and estimate total numdistinct.
3614	 *
3615	 * For each iteration of the outer loop, we process the frontmost Var in
3616	 * varinfos, plus all other Vars in the same relation.  We remove these
3617	 * Vars from the newvarinfos list for the next iteration. This is the
3618	 * easiest way to group Vars of same rel together.
3619	 */
3620	do
3621	{
3622		GroupVarInfo *varinfo1 = (GroupVarInfo *) linitial(varinfos);
3623		RelOptInfo *rel = varinfo1->rel;
3624		double		reldistinct = 1;
3625		double		relmaxndistinct = reldistinct;
3626		int			relvarcount = 0;
3627		List	   *newvarinfos = NIL;
3628		List	   *relvarinfos = NIL;
3629
3630		/*
3631		 * Split the list of varinfos in two - one for the current rel, one
3632		 * for remaining Vars on other rels.
3633		 */
3634		relvarinfos = lappend(relvarinfos, varinfo1);
3635		for_each_from(l, varinfos, 1)
3636		{
3637			GroupVarInfo *varinfo2 = (GroupVarInfo *) lfirst(l);
3638
3639			if (varinfo2->rel == varinfo1->rel)
3640			{
3641				/* varinfos on current rel */
3642				relvarinfos = lappend(relvarinfos, varinfo2);
3643			}
3644			else
3645			{
3646				/* not time to process varinfo2 yet */
3647				newvarinfos = lappend(newvarinfos, varinfo2);
3648			}
3649		}
3650
3651		/*
3652		 * Get the numdistinct estimate for the Vars of this rel.  We
3653		 * iteratively search for multivariate n-distinct with maximum number
3654		 * of vars; assuming that each var group is independent of the others,
3655		 * we multiply them together.  Any remaining relvarinfos after no more
3656		 * multivariate matches are found are assumed independent too, so
3657		 * their individual ndistinct estimates are multiplied also.
3658		 *
3659		 * While iterating, count how many separate numdistinct values we
3660		 * apply.  We apply a fudge factor below, but only if we multiplied
3661		 * more than one such values.
3662		 */
3663		while (relvarinfos)
3664		{
3665			double		mvndistinct;
3666
3667			if (estimate_multivariate_ndistinct(root, rel, &relvarinfos,
3668												&mvndistinct))
3669			{
3670				reldistinct *= mvndistinct;
3671				if (relmaxndistinct < mvndistinct)
3672					relmaxndistinct = mvndistinct;
3673				relvarcount++;
3674			}
3675			else
3676			{
3677				foreach(l, relvarinfos)
3678				{
3679					GroupVarInfo *varinfo2 = (GroupVarInfo *) lfirst(l);
3680
3681					reldistinct *= varinfo2->ndistinct;
3682					if (relmaxndistinct < varinfo2->ndistinct)
3683						relmaxndistinct = varinfo2->ndistinct;
3684					relvarcount++;
3685
3686					/*
3687					 * When varinfo2's isdefault is set then we'd better set
3688					 * the SELFLAG_USED_DEFAULT bit in the EstimationInfo.
3689					 */
3690					if (estinfo != NULL && varinfo2->isdefault)
3691						estinfo->flags |= SELFLAG_USED_DEFAULT;
3692				}
3693
3694				/* we're done with this relation */
3695				relvarinfos = NIL;
3696			}
3697		}
3698
3699		/*
3700		 * Sanity check --- don't divide by zero if empty relation.
3701		 */
3702		Assert(IS_SIMPLE_REL(rel));
3703		if (rel->tuples > 0)
3704		{
3705			/*
3706			 * Clamp to size of rel, or size of rel / 10 if multiple Vars. The
3707			 * fudge factor is because the Vars are probably correlated but we
3708			 * don't know by how much.  We should never clamp to less than the
3709			 * largest ndistinct value for any of the Vars, though, since
3710			 * there will surely be at least that many groups.
3711			 */
3712			double		clamp = rel->tuples;
3713
3714			if (relvarcount > 1)
3715			{
3716				clamp *= 0.1;
3717				if (clamp < relmaxndistinct)
3718				{
3719					clamp = relmaxndistinct;
3720					/* for sanity in case some ndistinct is too large: */
3721					if (clamp > rel->tuples)
3722						clamp = rel->tuples;
3723				}
3724			}
3725			if (reldistinct > clamp)
3726				reldistinct = clamp;
3727
3728			/*
3729			 * Update the estimate based on the restriction selectivity,
3730			 * guarding against division by zero when reldistinct is zero.
3731			 * Also skip this if we know that we are returning all rows.
3732			 */
3733			if (reldistinct > 0 && rel->rows < rel->tuples)
3734			{
3735				/*
3736				 * Given a table containing N rows with n distinct values in a
3737				 * uniform distribution, if we select p rows at random then
3738				 * the expected number of distinct values selected is
3739				 *
3740				 * n * (1 - product((N-N/n-i)/(N-i), i=0..p-1))
3741				 *
3742				 * = n * (1 - (N-N/n)! / (N-N/n-p)! * (N-p)! / N!)
3743				 *
3744				 * See "Approximating block accesses in database
3745				 * organizations", S. B. Yao, Communications of the ACM,
3746				 * Volume 20 Issue 4, April 1977 Pages 260-261.
3747				 *
3748				 * Alternatively, re-arranging the terms from the factorials,
3749				 * this may be written as
3750				 *
3751				 * n * (1 - product((N-p-i)/(N-i), i=0..N/n-1))
3752				 *
3753				 * This form of the formula is more efficient to compute in
3754				 * the common case where p is larger than N/n.  Additionally,
3755				 * as pointed out by Dell'Era, if i << N for all terms in the
3756				 * product, it can be approximated by
3757				 *
3758				 * n * (1 - ((N-p)/N)^(N/n))
3759				 *
3760				 * See "Expected distinct values when selecting from a bag
3761				 * without replacement", Alberto Dell'Era,
3762				 * http://www.adellera.it/investigations/distinct_balls/.
3763				 *
3764				 * The condition i << N is equivalent to n >> 1, so this is a
3765				 * good approximation when the number of distinct values in
3766				 * the table is large.  It turns out that this formula also
3767				 * works well even when n is small.
3768				 */
3769				reldistinct *=
3770					(1 - pow((rel->tuples - rel->rows) / rel->tuples,
3771							 rel->tuples / reldistinct));
3772			}
3773			reldistinct = clamp_row_est(reldistinct);
3774
3775			/*
3776			 * Update estimate of total distinct groups.
3777			 */
3778			numdistinct *= reldistinct;
3779		}
3780
3781		varinfos = newvarinfos;
3782	} while (varinfos != NIL);
3783
3784	/* Now we can account for the effects of any SRFs */
3785	numdistinct *= srf_multiplier;
3786
3787	/* Round off */
3788	numdistinct = ceil(numdistinct);
3789
3790	/* Guard against out-of-range answers */
3791	if (numdistinct > input_rows)
3792		numdistinct = input_rows;
3793	if (numdistinct < 1.0)
3794		numdistinct = 1.0;
3795
3796	return numdistinct;
3797}
3798
3799/*
3800 * Try to estimate the bucket size of the hash join inner side when the join
3801 * condition contains two or more clauses by employing extended statistics.
3802 *
3803 * The main idea of this approach is that the distinct value generated by
3804 * multivariate estimation on two or more columns would provide less bucket size
3805 * than estimation on one separate column.
3806 *
3807 * IMPORTANT: It is crucial to synchronize the approach of combining different
3808 * estimations with the caller's method.
3809 *
3810 * Return a list of clauses that didn't fetch any extended statistics.
3811 */
3812List *
3813estimate_multivariate_bucketsize(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *inner,
3814								 List *hashclauses,
3815								 Selectivity *innerbucketsize)
3816{
3817	List	   *clauses = list_copy(hashclauses);
3818	List	   *otherclauses = NIL;
3819	double		ndistinct = 1.0;
3820
3821	if (list_length(hashclauses) <= 1)
3822
3823		/*
3824		 * Nothing to do for a single clause.  Could we employ univariate
3825		 * extended stat here?
3826		 */
3827		return hashclauses;
3828
3829	while (clauses != NIL)
3830	{
3831		ListCell   *lc;
3832		int			relid = -1;
3833		List	   *varinfos = NIL;
3834		List	   *origin_rinfos = NIL;
3835		double		mvndistinct;
3836		List	   *origin_varinfos;
3837		int			group_relid = -1;
3838		RelOptInfo *group_rel = NULL;
3839		ListCell   *lc1,
3840				   *lc2;
3841
3842		/*
3843		 * Find clauses, referencing the same single base relation and try to
3844		 * estimate such a group with extended statistics.  Create varinfo for
3845		 * an approved clause, push it to otherclauses, if it can't be
3846		 * estimated here or ignore to process at the next iteration.
3847		 */
3848		foreach(lc, clauses)
3849		{
3850			RestrictInfo *rinfo = lfirst_node(RestrictInfo, lc);
3851			Node	   *expr;
3852			Relids		relids;
3853			GroupVarInfo *varinfo;
3854
3855			/*
3856			 * Find the inner side of the join, which we need to estimate the
3857			 * number of buckets.  Use outer_is_left because the
3858			 * clause_sides_match_join routine has called on hash clauses.
3859			 */
3860			relids = rinfo->outer_is_left ?
3861				rinfo->right_relids : rinfo->left_relids;
3862			expr = rinfo->outer_is_left ?
3863				get_rightop(rinfo->clause) : get_leftop(rinfo->clause);
3864
3865			if (bms_get_singleton_member(relids, &relid) &&
3866				root->simple_rel_array[relid]->statlist != NIL)
3867			{
3868				bool		is_duplicate = false;
3869
3870				/*
3871				 * This inner-side expression references only one relation.
3872				 * Extended statistics on this clause can exist.
3873				 */
3874				if (group_relid < 0)
3875				{
3876					RangeTblEntry *rte = root->simple_rte_array[relid];
3877
3878					if (!rte || (rte->relkind != RELKIND_RELATION &&
3879								 rte->relkind != RELKIND_MATVIEW &&
3880								 rte->relkind != RELKIND_FOREIGN_TABLE &&
3881								 rte->relkind != RELKIND_PARTITIONED_TABLE))
3882					{
3883						/* Extended statistics can't exist in principle */
3884						otherclauses = lappend(otherclauses, rinfo);
3885						clauses = foreach_delete_current(clauses, lc);
3886						continue;
3887					}
3888
3889					group_relid = relid;
3890					group_rel = root->simple_rel_array[relid];
3891				}
3892				else if (group_relid != relid)
3893
3894					/*
3895					 * Being in the group forming state we don't need other
3896					 * clauses.
3897					 */
3898					continue;
3899
3900				/*
3901				 * We're going to add the new clause to the varinfos list.  We
3902				 * might re-use add_unique_group_var(), but we don't do so for
3903				 * two reasons.
3904				 *
3905				 * 1) We must keep the origin_rinfos list ordered exactly the
3906				 * same way as varinfos.
3907				 *
3908				 * 2) add_unique_group_var() is designed for
3909				 * estimate_num_groups(), where a larger number of groups is
3910				 * worse.   While estimating the number of hash buckets, we
3911				 * have the opposite: a lesser number of groups is worse.
3912				 * Therefore, we don't have to remove "known equal" vars: the
3913				 * removed var may valuably contribute to the multivariate
3914				 * statistics to grow the number of groups.
3915				 */
3916
3917				/*
3918				 * Clear nullingrels to correctly match hash keys.  See
3919				 * add_unique_group_var()'s comment for details.
3920				 */
3921				expr = remove_nulling_relids(expr, root->outer_join_rels, NULL);
3922
3923				/*
3924				 * Detect and exclude exact duplicates from the list of hash
3925				 * keys (like add_unique_group_var does).
3926				 */
3927				foreach(lc1, varinfos)
3928				{
3929					varinfo = (GroupVarInfo *) lfirst(lc1);
3930
3931					if (!equal(expr, varinfo->var))
3932						continue;
3933
3934					is_duplicate = true;
3935					break;
3936				}
3937
3938				if (is_duplicate)
3939				{
3940					/*
3941					 * Skip exact duplicates. Adding them to the otherclauses
3942					 * list also doesn't make sense.
3943					 */
3944					continue;
3945				}
3946
3947				/*
3948				 * Initialize GroupVarInfo.  We only use it to call
3949				 * estimate_multivariate_ndistinct(), which doesn't care about
3950				 * ndistinct and isdefault fields.  Thus, skip these fields.
3951				 */
3952				varinfo = (GroupVarInfo *) palloc0(sizeof(GroupVarInfo));
3953				varinfo->var = expr;
3954				varinfo->rel = root->simple_rel_array[relid];
3955				varinfos = lappend(varinfos, varinfo);
3956
3957				/*
3958				 * Remember the link to RestrictInfo for the case the clause
3959				 * is failed to be estimated.
3960				 */
3961				origin_rinfos = lappend(origin_rinfos, rinfo);
3962			}
3963			else
3964			{
3965				/* This clause can't be estimated with extended statistics */
3966				otherclauses = lappend(otherclauses, rinfo);
3967			}
3968
3969			clauses = foreach_delete_current(clauses, lc);
3970		}
3971
3972		if (list_length(varinfos) < 2)
3973		{
3974			/*
3975			 * Multivariate statistics doesn't apply to single columns except
3976			 * for expressions, but it has not been implemented yet.
3977			 */
3978			otherclauses = list_concat(otherclauses, origin_rinfos);
3979			list_free_deep(varinfos);
3980			list_free(origin_rinfos);
3981			continue;
3982		}
3983
3984		Assert(group_rel != NULL);
3985
3986		/* Employ the extended statistics. */
3987		origin_varinfos = varinfos;
3988		for (;;)
3989		{
3990			bool		estimated = estimate_multivariate_ndistinct(root,
3991																	group_rel,
3992																	&varinfos,
3993																	&mvndistinct);
3994
3995			if (!estimated)
3996				break;
3997
3998			/*
3999			 * We've got an estimation.  Use ndistinct value in a consistent
4000			 * way - according to the caller's logic (see
4001			 * final_cost_hashjoin).
4002			 */
4003			if (ndistinct < mvndistinct)
4004				ndistinct = mvndistinct;
4005			Assert(ndistinct >= 1.0);
4006		}
4007
4008		Assert(list_length(origin_varinfos) == list_length(origin_rinfos));
4009
4010		/* Collect unmatched clauses as otherclauses. */
4011		forboth(lc1, origin_varinfos, lc2, origin_rinfos)
4012		{
4013			GroupVarInfo *vinfo = lfirst(lc1);
4014
4015			if (!list_member_ptr(varinfos, vinfo))
4016				/* Already estimated */
4017				continue;
4018
4019			/* Can't be estimated here - push to the returning list */
4020			otherclauses = lappend(otherclauses, lfirst(lc2));
4021		}
4022	}
4023
4024	*innerbucketsize = 1.0 / ndistinct;
4025	return otherclauses;
4026}
4027
4028/*
4029 * Estimate hash bucket statistics when the specified expression is used
4030 * as a hash key for the given number of buckets.
4031 *
4032 * This attempts to determine two values:
4033 *
4034 * 1. The frequency of the most common value of the expression (returns
4035 * zero into *mcv_freq if we can't get that).
4036 *
4037 * 2. The "bucketsize fraction", ie, average number of entries in a bucket
4038 * divided by total tuples in relation.
4039 *
4040 * XXX This is really pretty bogus since we're effectively assuming that the
4041 * distribution of hash keys will be the same after applying restriction
4042 * clauses as it was in the underlying relation.  However, we are not nearly
4043 * smart enough to figure out how the restrict clauses might change the
4044 * distribution, so this will have to do for now.
4045 *
4046 * We are passed the number of buckets the executor will use for the given
4047 * input relation.  If the data were perfectly distributed, with the same
4048 * number of tuples going into each available bucket, then the bucketsize
4049 * fraction would be 1/nbuckets.  But this happy state of affairs will occur
4050 * only if (a) there are at least nbuckets distinct data values, and (b)
4051 * we have a not-too-skewed data distribution.  Otherwise the buckets will
4052 * be nonuniformly occupied.  If the other relation in the join has a key
4053 * distribution similar to this one's, then the most-loaded buckets are
4054 * exactly those that will be probed most often.  Therefore, the "average"
4055 * bucket size for costing purposes should really be taken as something close
4056 * to the "worst case" bucket size.  We try to estimate this by adjusting the
4057 * fraction if there are too few distinct data values, and then scaling up
4058 * by the ratio of the most common value's frequency to the average frequency.
4059 *
4060 * If no statistics are available, use a default estimate of 0.1.  This will
4061 * discourage use of a hash rather strongly if the inner relation is large,
4062 * which is what we want.  We do not want to hash unless we know that the
4063 * inner rel is well-dispersed (or the alternatives seem much worse).
4064 *
4065 * The caller should also check that the mcv_freq is not so large that the
4066 * most common value would by itself require an impractically large bucket.
4067 * In a hash join, the executor can split buckets if they get too big, but
4068 * obviously that doesn't help for a bucket that contains many duplicates of
4069 * the same value.
4070 */
4071void
4072estimate_hash_bucket_stats(PlannerInfo *root, Node *hashkey, double nbuckets,
4073						   Selectivity *mcv_freq,
4074						   Selectivity *bucketsize_frac)
4075{
4076	VariableStatData vardata;
4077	double		estfract,
4078				ndistinct,
4079				stanullfrac,
4080				avgfreq;
4081	bool		isdefault;
4082	AttStatsSlot sslot;
4083
4084	examine_variable(root, hashkey, 0, &vardata);
4085
4086	/* Look up the frequency of the most common value, if available */
4087	*mcv_freq = 0.0;
4088
4089	if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata.statsTuple))
4090	{
4091		if (get_attstatsslot(&sslot, vardata.statsTuple,
4092							 STATISTIC_KIND_MCV, InvalidOid,
4093							 ATTSTATSSLOT_NUMBERS))
4094		{
4095			/*
4096			 * The first MCV stat is for the most common value.
4097			 */
4098			if (sslot.nnumbers > 0)
4099				*mcv_freq = sslot.numbers[0];
4100			free_attstatsslot(&sslot);
4101		}
4102	}
4103
4104	/* Get number of distinct values */
4105	ndistinct = get_variable_numdistinct(&vardata, &isdefault);
4106
4107	/*
4108	 * If ndistinct isn't real, punt.  We normally return 0.1, but if the
4109	 * mcv_freq is known to be even higher than that, use it instead.
4110	 */
4111	if (isdefault)
4112	{
4113		*bucketsize_frac = (Selectivity) Max(0.1, *mcv_freq);
4114		ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
4115		return;
4116	}
4117
4118	/* Get fraction that are null */
4119	if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata.statsTuple))
4120	{
4121		Form_pg_statistic stats;
4122
4123		stats = (Form_pg_statistic) GETSTRUCT(vardata.statsTuple);
4124		stanullfrac = stats->stanullfrac;
4125	}
4126	else
4127		stanullfrac = 0.0;
4128
4129	/* Compute avg freq of all distinct data values in raw relation */
4130	avgfreq = (1.0 - stanullfrac) / ndistinct;
4131
4132	/*
4133	 * Adjust ndistinct to account for restriction clauses.  Observe we are
4134	 * assuming that the data distribution is affected uniformly by the
4135	 * restriction clauses!
4136	 *
4137	 * XXX Possibly better way, but much more expensive: multiply by
4138	 * selectivity of rel's restriction clauses that mention the target Var.
4139	 */
4140	if (vardata.rel && vardata.rel->tuples > 0)
4141	{
4142		ndistinct *= vardata.rel->rows / vardata.rel->tuples;
4143		ndistinct = clamp_row_est(ndistinct);
4144	}
4145
4146	/*
4147	 * Initial estimate of bucketsize fraction is 1/nbuckets as long as the
4148	 * number of buckets is less than the expected number of distinct values;
4149	 * otherwise it is 1/ndistinct.
4150	 */
4151	if (ndistinct > nbuckets)
4152		estfract = 1.0 / nbuckets;
4153	else
4154		estfract = 1.0 / ndistinct;
4155
4156	/*
4157	 * Adjust estimated bucketsize upward to account for skewed distribution.
4158	 */
4159	if (avgfreq > 0.0 && *mcv_freq > avgfreq)
4160		estfract *= *mcv_freq / avgfreq;
4161
4162	/*
4163	 * Clamp bucketsize to sane range (the above adjustment could easily
4164	 * produce an out-of-range result).  We set the lower bound a little above
4165	 * zero, since zero isn't a very sane result.
4166	 */
4167	if (estfract < 1.0e-6)
4168		estfract = 1.0e-6;
4169	else if (estfract > 1.0)
4170		estfract = 1.0;
4171
4172	*bucketsize_frac = (Selectivity) estfract;
4173
4174	ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
4175}
4176
4177/*
4178 * estimate_hashagg_tablesize
4179 *	  estimate the number of bytes that a hash aggregate hashtable will
4180 *	  require based on the agg_costs, path width and number of groups.
4181 *
4182 * We return the result as "double" to forestall any possible overflow
4183 * problem in the multiplication by dNumGroups.
4184 *
4185 * XXX this may be over-estimating the size now that hashagg knows to omit
4186 * unneeded columns from the hashtable.  Also for mixed-mode grouping sets,
4187 * grouping columns not in the hashed set are counted here even though hashagg
4188 * won't store them.  Is this a problem?
4189 */
4190double
4191estimate_hashagg_tablesize(PlannerInfo *root, Path *path,
4192						   const AggClauseCosts *agg_costs, double dNumGroups)
4193{
4194	Size		hashentrysize;
4195
4196	hashentrysize = hash_agg_entry_size(list_length(root->aggtransinfos),
4197										path->pathtarget->width,
4198										agg_costs->transitionSpace);
4199
4200	/*
4201	 * Note that this disregards the effect of fill-factor and growth policy
4202	 * of the hash table.  That's probably ok, given that the default
4203	 * fill-factor is relatively high.  It'd be hard to meaningfully factor in
4204	 * "double-in-size" growth policies here.
4205	 */
4206	return hashentrysize * dNumGroups;
4207}
4208
4209
4210/*-------------------------------------------------------------------------
4211 *
4212 * Support routines
4213 *
4214 *-------------------------------------------------------------------------
4215 */
4216
4217/*
4218 * Find the best matching ndistinct extended statistics for the given list of
4219 * GroupVarInfos.
4220 *
4221 * Callers must ensure that the given GroupVarInfos all belong to 'rel' and
4222 * the GroupVarInfos list does not contain any duplicate Vars or expressions.
4223 *
4224 * When statistics are found that match > 1 of the given GroupVarInfo, the
4225 * *ndistinct parameter is set according to the ndistinct estimate and a new
4226 * list is built with the matching GroupVarInfos removed, which is output via
4227 * the *varinfos parameter before returning true.  When no matching stats are
4228 * found, false is returned and the *varinfos and *ndistinct parameters are
4229 * left untouched.
4230 */
4231static bool
4232estimate_multivariate_ndistinct(PlannerInfo *root, RelOptInfo *rel,
4233								List **varinfos, double *ndistinct)
4234{
4235	ListCell   *lc;
4236	int			nmatches_vars;
4237	int			nmatches_exprs;
4238	Oid			statOid = InvalidOid;
4239	MVNDistinct *stats;
4240	StatisticExtInfo *matched_info = NULL;
4241	RangeTblEntry *rte = planner_rt_fetch(rel->relid, root);
4242
4243	/* bail out immediately if the table has no extended statistics */
4244	if (!rel->statlist)
4245		return false;
4246
4247	/* look for the ndistinct statistics object matching the most vars */
4248	nmatches_vars = 0;			/* we require at least two matches */
4249	nmatches_exprs = 0;
4250	foreach(lc, rel->statlist)
4251	{
4252		ListCell   *lc2;
4253		StatisticExtInfo *info = (StatisticExtInfo *) lfirst(lc);
4254		int			nshared_vars = 0;
4255		int			nshared_exprs = 0;
4256
4257		/* skip statistics of other kinds */
4258		if (info->kind != STATS_EXT_NDISTINCT)
4259			continue;
4260
4261		/* skip statistics with mismatching stxdinherit value */
4262		if (info->inherit != rte->inh)
4263			continue;
4264
4265		/*
4266		 * Determine how many expressions (and variables in non-matched
4267		 * expressions) match. We'll then use these numbers to pick the
4268		 * statistics object that best matches the clauses.
4269		 */
4270		foreach(lc2, *varinfos)
4271		{
4272			ListCell   *lc3;
4273			GroupVarInfo *varinfo = (GroupVarInfo *) lfirst(lc2);
4274			AttrNumber	attnum;
4275
4276			Assert(varinfo->rel == rel);
4277
4278			/* simple Var, search in statistics keys directly */
4279			if (IsA(varinfo->var, Var))
4280			{
4281				attnum = ((Var *) varinfo->var)->varattno;
4282
4283				/*
4284				 * Ignore system attributes - we don't support statistics on
4285				 * them, so can't match them (and it'd fail as the values are
4286				 * negative).
4287				 */
4288				if (!AttrNumberIsForUserDefinedAttr(attnum))
4289					continue;
4290
4291				if (bms_is_member(attnum, info->keys))
4292					nshared_vars++;
4293
4294				continue;
4295			}
4296
4297			/* expression - see if it's in the statistics object */
4298			foreach(lc3, info->exprs)
4299			{
4300				Node	   *expr = (Node *) lfirst(lc3);
4301
4302				if (equal(varinfo->var, expr))
4303				{
4304					nshared_exprs++;
4305					break;
4306				}
4307			}
4308		}
4309
4310		/*
4311		 * The ndistinct extended statistics contain estimates for a minimum
4312		 * of pairs of columns which the statistics are defined on and
4313		 * certainly not single columns.  Here we skip unless we managed to
4314		 * match to at least two columns.
4315		 */
4316		if (nshared_vars + nshared_exprs < 2)
4317			continue;
4318
4319		/*
4320		 * Check if these statistics are a better match than the previous best
4321		 * match and if so, take note of the StatisticExtInfo.
4322		 *
4323		 * The statslist is sorted by statOid, so the StatisticExtInfo we
4324		 * select as the best match is deterministic even when multiple sets
4325		 * of statistics match equally as well.
4326		 */
4327		if ((nshared_exprs > nmatches_exprs) ||
4328			(((nshared_exprs == nmatches_exprs)) && (nshared_vars > nmatches_vars)))
4329		{
4330			statOid = info->statOid;
4331			nmatches_vars = nshared_vars;
4332			nmatches_exprs = nshared_exprs;
4333			matched_info = info;
4334		}
4335	}
4336
4337	/* No match? */
4338	if (statOid == InvalidOid)
4339		return false;
4340
4341	Assert(nmatches_vars + nmatches_exprs > 1);
4342
4343	stats = statext_ndistinct_load(statOid, rte->inh);
4344
4345	/*
4346	 * If we have a match, search it for the specific item that matches (there
4347	 * must be one), and construct the output values.
4348	 */
4349	if (stats)
4350	{
4351		int			i;
4352		List	   *newlist = NIL;
4353		MVNDistinctItem *item = NULL;
4354		ListCell   *lc2;
4355		Bitmapset  *matched = NULL;
4356		AttrNumber	attnum_offset;
4357
4358		/*
4359		 * How much we need to offset the attnums? If there are no
4360		 * expressions, no offset is needed. Otherwise offset enough to move
4361		 * the lowest one (which is equal to number of expressions) to 1.
4362		 */
4363		if (matched_info->exprs)
4364			attnum_offset = (list_length(matched_info->exprs) + 1);
4365		else
4366			attnum_offset = 0;
4367
4368		/* see what actually matched */
4369		foreach(lc2, *varinfos)
4370		{
4371			ListCell   *lc3;
4372			int			idx;
4373			bool		found = false;
4374
4375			GroupVarInfo *varinfo = (GroupVarInfo *) lfirst(lc2);
4376
4377			/*
4378			 * Process a simple Var expression, by matching it to keys
4379			 * directly. If there's a matching expression, we'll try matching
4380			 * it later.
4381			 */
4382			if (IsA(varinfo->var, Var))
4383			{
4384				AttrNumber	attnum = ((Var *) varinfo->var)->varattno;
4385
4386				/*
4387				 * Ignore expressions on system attributes. Can't rely on the
4388				 * bms check for negative values.
4389				 */
4390				if (!AttrNumberIsForUserDefinedAttr(attnum))
4391					continue;
4392
4393				/* Is the variable covered by the statistics object? */
4394				if (!bms_is_member(attnum, matched_info->keys))
4395					continue;
4396
4397				attnum = attnum + attnum_offset;
4398
4399				/* ensure sufficient offset */
4400				Assert(AttrNumberIsForUserDefinedAttr(attnum));
4401
4402				matched = bms_add_member(matched, attnum);
4403
4404				found = true;
4405			}
4406
4407			/*
4408			 * XXX Maybe we should allow searching the expressions even if we
4409			 * found an attribute matching the expression? That would handle
4410			 * trivial expressions like "(a)" but it seems fairly useless.
4411			 */
4412			if (found)
4413				continue;
4414
4415			/* expression - see if it's in the statistics object */
4416			idx = 0;
4417			foreach(lc3, matched_info->exprs)
4418			{
4419				Node	   *expr = (Node *) lfirst(lc3);
4420
4421				if (equal(varinfo->var, expr))
4422				{
4423					AttrNumber	attnum = -(idx + 1);
4424
4425					attnum = attnum + attnum_offset;
4426
4427					/* ensure sufficient offset */
4428					Assert(AttrNumberIsForUserDefinedAttr(attnum));
4429
4430					matched = bms_add_member(matched, attnum);
4431
4432					/* there should be just one matching expression */
4433					break;
4434				}
4435
4436				idx++;
4437			}
4438		}
4439
4440		/* Find the specific item that exactly matches the combination */
4441		for (i = 0; i < stats->nitems; i++)
4442		{
4443			int			j;
4444			MVNDistinctItem *tmpitem = &stats->items[i];
4445
4446			if (tmpitem->nattributes != bms_num_members(matched))
4447				continue;
4448
4449			/* assume it's the right item */
4450			item = tmpitem;
4451
4452			/* check that all item attributes/expressions fit the match */
4453			for (j = 0; j < tmpitem->nattributes; j++)
4454			{
4455				AttrNumber	attnum = tmpitem->attributes[j];
4456
4457				/*
4458				 * Thanks to how we constructed the matched bitmap above, we
4459				 * can just offset all attnums the same way.
4460				 */
4461				attnum = attnum + attnum_offset;
4462
4463				if (!bms_is_member(attnum, matched))
4464				{
4465					/* nah, it's not this item */
4466					item = NULL;
4467					break;
4468				}
4469			}
4470
4471			/*
4472			 * If the item has all the matched attributes, we know it's the
4473			 * right one - there can't be a better one. matching more.
4474			 */
4475			if (item)
4476				break;
4477		}
4478
4479		/*
4480		 * Make sure we found an item. There has to be one, because ndistinct
4481		 * statistics includes all combinations of attributes.
4482		 */
4483		if (!item)
4484			elog(ERROR, "corrupt MVNDistinct entry");
4485
4486		/* Form the output varinfo list, keeping only unmatched ones */
4487		foreach(lc, *varinfos)
4488		{
4489			GroupVarInfo *varinfo = (GroupVarInfo *) lfirst(lc);
4490			ListCell   *lc3;
4491			bool		found = false;
4492
4493			/*
4494			 * Let's look at plain variables first, because it's the most
4495			 * common case and the check is quite cheap. We can simply get the
4496			 * attnum and check (with an offset) matched bitmap.
4497			 */
4498			if (IsA(varinfo->var, Var))
4499			{
4500				AttrNumber	attnum = ((Var *) varinfo->var)->varattno;
4501
4502				/*
4503				 * If it's a system attribute, we're done. We don't support
4504				 * extended statistics on system attributes, so it's clearly
4505				 * not matched. Just keep the expression and continue.
4506				 */
4507				if (!AttrNumberIsForUserDefinedAttr(attnum))
4508				{
4509					newlist = lappend(newlist, varinfo);
4510					continue;
4511				}
4512
4513				/* apply the same offset as above */
4514				attnum += attnum_offset;
4515
4516				/* if it's not matched, keep the varinfo */
4517				if (!bms_is_member(attnum, matched))
4518					newlist = lappend(newlist, varinfo);
4519
4520				/* The rest of the loop deals with complex expressions. */
4521				continue;
4522			}
4523
4524			/*
4525			 * Process complex expressions, not just simple Vars.
4526			 *
4527			 * First, we search for an exact match of an expression. If we
4528			 * find one, we can just discard the whole GroupVarInfo, with all
4529			 * the variables we extracted from it.
4530			 *
4531			 * Otherwise we inspect the individual vars, and try matching it
4532			 * to variables in the item.
4533			 */
4534			foreach(lc3, matched_info->exprs)
4535			{
4536				Node	   *expr = (Node *) lfirst(lc3);
4537
4538				if (equal(varinfo->var, expr))
4539				{
4540					found = true;
4541					break;
4542				}
4543			}
4544
4545			/* found exact match, skip */
4546			if (found)
4547				continue;
4548
4549			newlist = lappend(newlist, varinfo);
4550		}
4551
4552		*varinfos = newlist;
4553		*ndistinct = item->ndistinct;
4554		return true;
4555	}
4556
4557	return false;
4558}
4559
4560/*
4561 * convert_to_scalar
4562 *	  Convert non-NULL values of the indicated types to the comparison
4563 *	  scale needed by scalarineqsel().
4564 *	  Returns "true" if successful.
4565 *
4566 * XXX this routine is a hack: ideally we should look up the conversion
4567 * subroutines in pg_type.
4568 *
4569 * All numeric datatypes are simply converted to their equivalent
4570 * "double" values.  (NUMERIC values that are outside the range of "double"
4571 * are clamped to +/- HUGE_VAL.)
4572 *
4573 * String datatypes are converted by convert_string_to_scalar(),
4574 * which is explained below.  The reason why this routine deals with
4575 * three values at a time, not just one, is that we need it for strings.
4576 *
4577 * The bytea datatype is just enough different from strings that it has
4578 * to be treated separately.
4579 *
4580 * The several datatypes representing absolute times are all converted
4581 * to Timestamp, which is actually an int64, and then we promote that to
4582 * a double.  Note this will give correct results even for the "special"
4583 * values of Timestamp, since those are chosen to compare correctly;
4584 * see timestamp_cmp.
4585 *
4586 * The several datatypes representing relative times (intervals) are all
4587 * converted to measurements expressed in seconds.
4588 */
4589static bool
4590convert_to_scalar(Datum value, Oid valuetypid, Oid collid, double *scaledvalue,
4591				  Datum lobound, Datum hibound, Oid boundstypid,
4592				  double *scaledlobound, double *scaledhibound)
4593{
4594	bool		failure = false;
4595
4596	/*
4597	 * Both the valuetypid and the boundstypid should exactly match the
4598	 * declared input type(s) of the operator we are invoked for.  However,
4599	 * extensions might try to use scalarineqsel as estimator for operators
4600	 * with input type(s) we don't handle here; in such cases, we want to
4601	 * return false, not fail.  In any case, we mustn't assume that valuetypid
4602	 * and boundstypid are identical.
4603	 *
4604	 * XXX The histogram we are interpolating between points of could belong
4605	 * to a column that's only binary-compatible with the declared type. In
4606	 * essence we are assuming that the semantics of binary-compatible types
4607	 * are enough alike that we can use a histogram generated with one type's
4608	 * operators to estimate selectivity for the other's.  This is outright
4609	 * wrong in some cases --- in particular signed versus unsigned
4610	 * interpretation could trip us up.  But it's useful enough in the
4611	 * majority of cases that we do it anyway.  Should think about more
4612	 * rigorous ways to do it.
4613	 */
4614	switch (valuetypid)
4615	{
4616			/*
4617			 * Built-in numeric types
4618			 */
4619		case BOOLOID:
4620		case INT2OID:
4621		case INT4OID:
4622		case INT8OID:
4623		case FLOAT4OID:
4624		case FLOAT8OID:
4625		case NUMERICOID:
4626		case OIDOID:
4627		case REGPROCOID:
4628		case REGPROCEDUREOID:
4629		case REGOPEROID:
4630		case REGOPERATOROID:
4631		case REGCLASSOID:
4632		case REGTYPEOID:
4633		case REGCOLLATIONOID:
4634		case REGCONFIGOID:
4635		case REGDICTIONARYOID:
4636		case REGROLEOID:
4637		case REGNAMESPACEOID:
4638			*scaledvalue = convert_numeric_to_scalar(value, valuetypid,
4639													 &failure);
4640			*scaledlobound = convert_numeric_to_scalar(lobound, boundstypid,
4641													   &failure);
4642			*scaledhibound = convert_numeric_to_scalar(hibound, boundstypid,
4643													   &failure);
4644			return !failure;
4645
4646			/*
4647			 * Built-in string types
4648			 */
4649		case CHAROID:
4650		case BPCHAROID:
4651		case VARCHAROID:
4652		case TEXTOID:
4653		case NAMEOID:
4654			{
4655				char	   *valstr = convert_string_datum(value, valuetypid,
4656														  collid, &failure);
4657				char	   *lostr = convert_string_datum(lobound, boundstypid,
4658														 collid, &failure);
4659				char	   *histr = convert_string_datum(hibound, boundstypid,
4660														 collid, &failure);
4661
4662				/*
4663				 * Bail out if any of the values is not of string type.  We
4664				 * might leak converted strings for the other value(s), but
4665				 * that's not worth troubling over.
4666				 */
4667				if (failure)
4668					return false;
4669
4670				convert_string_to_scalar(valstr, scaledvalue,
4671										 lostr, scaledlobound,
4672										 histr, scaledhibound);
4673				pfree(valstr);
4674				pfree(lostr);
4675				pfree(histr);
4676				return true;
4677			}
4678
4679			/*
4680			 * Built-in bytea type
4681			 */
4682		case BYTEAOID:
4683			{
4684				/* We only support bytea vs bytea comparison */
4685				if (boundstypid != BYTEAOID)
4686					return false;
4687				convert_bytea_to_scalar(value, scaledvalue,
4688										lobound, scaledlobound,
4689										hibound, scaledhibound);
4690				return true;
4691			}
4692
4693			/*
4694			 * Built-in time types
4695			 */
4696		case TIMESTAMPOID:
4697		case TIMESTAMPTZOID:
4698		case DATEOID:
4699		case INTERVALOID:
4700		case TIMEOID:
4701		case TIMETZOID:
4702			*scaledvalue = convert_timevalue_to_scalar(value, valuetypid,
4703													   &failure);
4704			*scaledlobound = convert_timevalue_to_scalar(lobound, boundstypid,
4705														 &failure);
4706			*scaledhibound = convert_timevalue_to_scalar(hibound, boundstypid,
4707														 &failure);
4708			return !failure;
4709
4710			/*
4711			 * Built-in network types
4712			 */
4713		case INETOID:
4714		case CIDROID:
4715		case MACADDROID:
4716		case MACADDR8OID:
4717			*scaledvalue = convert_network_to_scalar(value, valuetypid,
4718													 &failure);
4719			*scaledlobound = convert_network_to_scalar(lobound, boundstypid,
4720													   &failure);
4721			*scaledhibound = convert_network_to_scalar(hibound, boundstypid,
4722													   &failure);
4723			return !failure;
4724	}
4725	/* Don't know how to convert */
4726	*scaledvalue = *scaledlobound = *scaledhibound = 0;
4727	return false;
4728}
4729
4730/*
4731 * Do convert_to_scalar()'s work for any numeric data type.
4732 *
4733 * On failure (e.g., unsupported typid), set *failure to true;
4734 * otherwise, that variable is not changed.
4735 */
4736static double
4737convert_numeric_to_scalar(Datum value, Oid typid, bool *failure)
4738{
4739	switch (typid)
4740	{
4741		case BOOLOID:
4742			return (double) DatumGetBool(value);
4743		case INT2OID:
4744			return (double) DatumGetInt16(value);
4745		case INT4OID:
4746			return (double) DatumGetInt32(value);
4747		case INT8OID:
4748			return (double) DatumGetInt64(value);
4749		case FLOAT4OID:
4750			return (double) DatumGetFloat4(value);
4751		case FLOAT8OID:
4752			return (double) DatumGetFloat8(value);
4753		case NUMERICOID:
4754			/* Note: out-of-range values will be clamped to +-HUGE_VAL */
4755			return (double)
4756				DatumGetFloat8(DirectFunctionCall1(numeric_float8_no_overflow,
4757												   value));
4758		case OIDOID:
4759		case REGPROCOID:
4760		case REGPROCEDUREOID:
4761		case REGOPEROID:
4762		case REGOPERATOROID:
4763		case REGCLASSOID:
4764		case REGTYPEOID:
4765		case REGCOLLATIONOID:
4766		case REGCONFIGOID:
4767		case REGDICTIONARYOID:
4768		case REGROLEOID:
4769		case REGNAMESPACEOID:
4770			/* we can treat OIDs as integers... */
4771			return (double) DatumGetObjectId(value);
4772	}
4773
4774	*failure = true;
4775	return 0;
4776}
4777
4778/*
4779 * Do convert_to_scalar()'s work for any character-string data type.
4780 *
4781 * String datatypes are converted to a scale that ranges from 0 to 1,
4782 * where we visualize the bytes of the string as fractional digits.
4783 *
4784 * We do not want the base to be 256, however, since that tends to
4785 * generate inflated selectivity estimates; few databases will have
4786 * occurrences of all 256 possible byte values at each position.
4787 * Instead, use the smallest and largest byte values seen in the bounds
