7125											  NULL);
7126
7127	/*
7128	 * If caller didn't give us an estimate, estimate the number of index
7129	 * tuples that will be visited.  We do it in this rather peculiar-looking
7130	 * way in order to get the right answer for partial indexes.
7131	 */
7132	numIndexTuples = costs->numIndexTuples;
7133	if (numIndexTuples <= 0.0)
7134	{
7135		numIndexTuples = indexSelectivity * index->rel->tuples;
7136
7137		/*
7138		 * The above calculation counts all the tuples visited across all
7139		 * scans induced by ScalarArrayOpExpr nodes.  We want to consider the
7140		 * average per-indexscan number, so adjust.  This is a handy place to
7141		 * round to integer, too.  (If caller supplied tuple estimate, it's
7142		 * responsible for handling these considerations.)
7143		 */
7144		numIndexTuples = rint(numIndexTuples / num_sa_scans);
7145	}
7146
7147	/*
7148	 * We can bound the number of tuples by the index size in any case. Also,
7149	 * always estimate at least one tuple is touched, even when
7150	 * indexSelectivity estimate is tiny.
7151	 */
7152	if (numIndexTuples > index->tuples)
7153		numIndexTuples = index->tuples;
7154	if (numIndexTuples < 1.0)
7155		numIndexTuples = 1.0;
7156
7157	/*
7158	 * Estimate the number of index pages that will be retrieved.
7159	 *
7160	 * We use the simplistic method of taking a pro-rata fraction of the total
7161	 * number of index pages.  In effect, this counts only leaf pages and not
7162	 * any overhead such as index metapage or upper tree levels.
7163	 *
7164	 * In practice access to upper index levels is often nearly free because
7165	 * those tend to stay in cache under load; moreover, the cost involved is
7166	 * highly dependent on index type.  We therefore ignore such costs here
7167	 * and leave it to the caller to add a suitable charge if needed.
7168	 */
7169	if (index->pages > 1 && index->tuples > 1)
7170		numIndexPages = ceil(numIndexTuples * index->pages / index->tuples);
7171	else
7172		numIndexPages = 1.0;
7173
7174	/* fetch estimated page cost for tablespace containing index */
7175	get_tablespace_page_costs(index->reltablespace,
7176							  &spc_random_page_cost,
7177							  NULL);
7178
7179	/*
7180	 * Now compute the disk access costs.
7181	 *
7182	 * The above calculations are all per-index-scan.  However, if we are in a
7183	 * nestloop inner scan, we can expect the scan to be repeated (with
7184	 * different search keys) for each row of the outer relation.  Likewise,
7185	 * ScalarArrayOpExpr quals result in multiple index scans.  This creates
7186	 * the potential for cache effects to reduce the number of disk page
7187	 * fetches needed.  We want to estimate the average per-scan I/O cost in
7188	 * the presence of caching.
7189	 *
7190	 * We use the Mackert-Lohman formula (see costsize.c for details) to
7191	 * estimate the total number of page fetches that occur.  While this
7192	 * wasn't what it was designed for, it seems a reasonable model anyway.
7193	 * Note that we are counting pages not tuples anymore, so we take N = T =
7194	 * index size, as if there were one "tuple" per page.
7195	 */
7196	num_outer_scans = loop_count;
7197	num_scans = num_sa_scans * num_outer_scans;
7198
7199	if (num_scans > 1)
7200	{
7201		double		pages_fetched;
7202
7203		/* total page fetches ignoring cache effects */
7204		pages_fetched = numIndexPages * num_scans;
7205
7206		/* use Mackert and Lohman formula to adjust for cache effects */
7207		pages_fetched = index_pages_fetched(pages_fetched,
7208											index->pages,
7209											(double) index->pages,
7210											root);
7211
7212		/*
7213		 * Now compute the total disk access cost, and then report a pro-rated
7214		 * share for each outer scan.  (Don't pro-rate for ScalarArrayOpExpr,
7215		 * since that's internal to the indexscan.)
7216		 */
7217		indexTotalCost = (pages_fetched * spc_random_page_cost)
7218			/ num_outer_scans;
7219	}
7220	else
7221	{
7222		/*
7223		 * For a single index scan, we just charge spc_random_page_cost per
7224		 * page touched.
7225		 */
7226		indexTotalCost = numIndexPages * spc_random_page_cost;
7227	}
7228
7229	/*
7230	 * CPU cost: any complex expressions in the indexquals will need to be
7231	 * evaluated once at the start of the scan to reduce them to runtime keys
7232	 * to pass to the index AM (see nodeIndexscan.c).  We model the per-tuple
7233	 * CPU costs as cpu_index_tuple_cost plus one cpu_operator_cost per
7234	 * indexqual operator.  Because we have numIndexTuples as a per-scan
7235	 * number, we have to multiply by num_sa_scans to get the correct result
7236	 * for ScalarArrayOpExpr cases.  Similarly add in costs for any index
7237	 * ORDER BY expressions.
7238	 *
7239	 * Note: this neglects the possible costs of rechecking lossy operators.
7240	 * Detecting that that might be needed seems more expensive than it's
7241	 * worth, though, considering all the other inaccuracies here ...
7242	 */
7243	qual_arg_cost = index_other_operands_eval_cost(root, indexQuals) +
7244		index_other_operands_eval_cost(root, indexOrderBys);
7245	qual_op_cost = cpu_operator_cost *
7246		(list_length(indexQuals) + list_length(indexOrderBys));
7247
7248	indexStartupCost = qual_arg_cost;
7249	indexTotalCost += qual_arg_cost;
7250	indexTotalCost += numIndexTuples * num_sa_scans * (cpu_index_tuple_cost + qual_op_cost);
7251
7252	/*
7253	 * Generic assumption about index correlation: there isn't any.
7254	 */
7255	indexCorrelation = 0.0;
7256
7257	/*
7258	 * Return everything to caller.
7259	 */
7260	costs->indexStartupCost = indexStartupCost;
7261	costs->indexTotalCost = indexTotalCost;
7262	costs->indexSelectivity = indexSelectivity;
7263	costs->indexCorrelation = indexCorrelation;
7264	costs->numIndexPages = numIndexPages;
7265	costs->numIndexTuples = numIndexTuples;
7266	costs->spc_random_page_cost = spc_random_page_cost;
7267	costs->num_sa_scans = num_sa_scans;
7268}
7269
7270/*
7271 * If the index is partial, add its predicate to the given qual list.
7272 *
7273 * ANDing the index predicate with the explicitly given indexquals produces
7274 * a more accurate idea of the index's selectivity.  However, we need to be
7275 * careful not to insert redundant clauses, because clauselist_selectivity()
7276 * is easily fooled into computing a too-low selectivity estimate.  Our
7277 * approach is to add only the predicate clause(s) that cannot be proven to
7278 * be implied by the given indexquals.  This successfully handles cases such
7279 * as a qual "x = 42" used with a partial index "WHERE x >= 40 AND x < 50".
7280 * There are many other cases where we won't detect redundancy, leading to a
7281 * too-low selectivity estimate, which will bias the system in favor of using
7282 * partial indexes where possible.  That is not necessarily bad though.
7283 *
7284 * Note that indexQuals contains RestrictInfo nodes while the indpred
7285 * does not, so the output list will be mixed.  This is OK for both
7286 * predicate_implied_by() and clauselist_selectivity(), but might be
7287 * problematic if the result were passed to other things.
7288 */
7289List *
7290add_predicate_to_index_quals(IndexOptInfo *index, List *indexQuals)
7291{
7292	List	   *predExtraQuals = NIL;
7293	ListCell   *lc;
7294
7295	if (index->indpred == NIL)
7296		return indexQuals;
7297
7298	foreach(lc, index->indpred)
7299	{
7300		Node	   *predQual = (Node *) lfirst(lc);
7301		List	   *oneQual = list_make1(predQual);
7302
7303		if (!predicate_implied_by(oneQual, indexQuals, false))
7304			predExtraQuals = list_concat(predExtraQuals, oneQual);
7305	}
7306	return list_concat(predExtraQuals, indexQuals);
7307}
7308
7309/*
7310 * Estimate correlation of btree index's first column.
7311 *
7312 * If we can get an estimate of the first column's ordering correlation C
7313 * from pg_statistic, estimate the index correlation as C for a single-column
7314 * index, or C * 0.75 for multiple columns.  The idea here is that multiple
7315 * columns dilute the importance of the first column's ordering, but don't
7316 * negate it entirely.
7317 *
7318 * We already filled in the stats tuple for *vardata when called.
7319 */
7320static double
7321btcost_correlation(IndexOptInfo *index, VariableStatData *vardata)
7322{
7323	Oid			sortop;
7324	AttStatsSlot sslot;
7325	double		indexCorrelation = 0;
7326
7327	Assert(HeapTupleIsValid(vardata->statsTuple));
7328
7329	sortop = get_opfamily_member(index->opfamily[0],
7330								 index->opcintype[0],
7331								 index->opcintype[0],
7332								 BTLessStrategyNumber);
7333	if (OidIsValid(sortop) &&
7334		get_attstatsslot(&sslot, vardata->statsTuple,
7335						 STATISTIC_KIND_CORRELATION, sortop,
7336						 ATTSTATSSLOT_NUMBERS))
7337	{
7338		double		varCorrelation;
7339
7340		Assert(sslot.nnumbers == 1);
7341		varCorrelation = sslot.numbers[0];
7342
7343		if (index->reverse_sort[0])
7344			varCorrelation = -varCorrelation;
7345
7346		if (index->nkeycolumns > 1)
7347			indexCorrelation = varCorrelation * 0.75;
7348		else
7349			indexCorrelation = varCorrelation;
7350
7351		free_attstatsslot(&sslot);
7352	}
7353
7354	return indexCorrelation;
7355}
7356
7357void
7358btcostestimate(PlannerInfo *root, IndexPath *path, double loop_count,
7359			   Cost *indexStartupCost, Cost *indexTotalCost,
7360			   Selectivity *indexSelectivity, double *indexCorrelation,
7361			   double *indexPages)
7362{
7363	IndexOptInfo *index = path->indexinfo;
7364	GenericCosts costs = {0};
7365	VariableStatData vardata = {0};
7366	double		numIndexTuples;
7367	Cost		descentCost;
7368	List	   *indexBoundQuals;
7369	List	   *indexSkipQuals;
7370	int			indexcol;
7371	bool		eqQualHere;
7372	bool		found_row_compare;
7373	bool		found_array;
7374	bool		found_is_null_op;
7375	bool		have_correlation = false;
7376	double		num_sa_scans;
7377	double		correlation = 0.0;
7378	ListCell   *lc;
7379
7380	/*
7381	 * For a btree scan, only leading '=' quals plus inequality quals for the
7382	 * immediately next attribute contribute to index selectivity (these are
7383	 * the "boundary quals" that determine the starting and stopping points of
7384	 * the index scan).  Additional quals can suppress visits to the heap, so
7385	 * it's OK to count them in indexSelectivity, but they should not count
7386	 * for estimating numIndexTuples.  So we must examine the given indexquals
7387	 * to find out which ones count as boundary quals.  We rely on the
7388	 * knowledge that they are given in index column order.  Note that nbtree
7389	 * preprocessing can add skip arrays that act as leading '=' quals in the
7390	 * absence of ordinary input '=' quals, so in practice _most_ input quals
7391	 * are able to act as index bound quals (which we take into account here).
7392	 *
7393	 * For a RowCompareExpr, we consider only the first column, just as
7394	 * rowcomparesel() does.
7395	 *
7396	 * If there's a SAOP or skip array in the quals, we'll actually perform up
7397	 * to N index descents (not just one), but the underlying array key's
7398	 * operator can be considered to act the same as it normally does.
7399	 */
7400	indexBoundQuals = NIL;
7401	indexSkipQuals = NIL;
7402	indexcol = 0;
7403	eqQualHere = false;
7404	found_row_compare = false;
7405	found_array = false;
7406	found_is_null_op = false;
7407	num_sa_scans = 1;
7408	foreach(lc, path->indexclauses)
7409	{
7410		IndexClause *iclause = lfirst_node(IndexClause, lc);
7411		ListCell   *lc2;
7412
7413		if (indexcol < iclause->indexcol)
7414		{
7415			double		num_sa_scans_prev_cols = num_sa_scans;
7416
7417			/*
7418			 * Beginning of a new column's quals.
7419			 *
7420			 * Skip scans use skip arrays, which are ScalarArrayOp style
7421			 * arrays that generate their elements procedurally and on demand.
7422			 * Given a multi-column index on "(a, b)", and an SQL WHERE clause
7423			 * "WHERE b = 42", a skip scan will effectively use an indexqual
7424			 * "WHERE a = ANY('{every col a value}') AND b = 42".  (Obviously,
7425			 * the array on "a" must also return "IS NULL" matches, since our
7426			 * WHERE clause used no strict operator on "a").
7427			 *
7428			 * Here we consider how nbtree will backfill skip arrays for any
7429			 * index columns that lacked an '=' qual.  This maintains our
7430			 * num_sa_scans estimate, and determines if this new column (the
7431			 * "iclause->indexcol" column, not the prior "indexcol" column)
7432			 * can have its RestrictInfos/quals added to indexBoundQuals.
7433			 *
7434			 * We'll need to handle columns that have inequality quals, where
7435			 * the skip array generates values from a range constrained by the
7436			 * quals (not every possible value).  We've been maintaining
7437			 * indexSkipQuals to help with this; it will now contain all of
7438			 * the prior column's quals (that is, indexcol's quals) when they
7439			 * might be used for this.
7440			 */
7441			if (found_row_compare)
7442			{
7443				/*
7444				 * Skip arrays can't be added after a RowCompare input qual
7445				 * due to limitations in nbtree
7446				 */
7447				break;
7448			}
7449			if (eqQualHere)
7450			{
7451				/*
7452				 * Don't need to add a skip array for an indexcol that already
7453				 * has an '=' qual/equality constraint
7454				 */
7455				indexcol++;
7456				indexSkipQuals = NIL;
7457			}
7458			eqQualHere = false;
7459
7460			while (indexcol < iclause->indexcol)
7461			{
7462				double		ndistinct;
7463				bool		isdefault = true;
7464
7465				found_array = true;
7466
7467				/*
7468				 * A skipped attribute's ndistinct forms the basis of our
7469				 * estimate of the total number of "array elements" used by
7470				 * its skip array at runtime.  Look that up first.
7471				 */
7472				examine_indexcol_variable(root, index, indexcol, &vardata);
7473				ndistinct = get_variable_numdistinct(&vardata, &isdefault);
7474
7475				if (indexcol == 0)
7476				{
7477					/*
7478					 * Get an estimate of the leading column's correlation in
7479					 * passing (avoids rereading variable stats below)
7480					 */
7481					if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata.statsTuple))
7482						correlation = btcost_correlation(index, &vardata);
7483					have_correlation = true;
7484				}
7485
7486				ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
7487
7488				/*
7489				 * If ndistinct is a default estimate, conservatively assume
7490				 * that no skipping will happen at runtime
7491				 */
7492				if (isdefault)
7493				{
7494					num_sa_scans = num_sa_scans_prev_cols;
7495					break;		/* done building indexBoundQuals */
7496				}
7497
7498				/*
7499				 * Apply indexcol's indexSkipQuals selectivity to ndistinct
7500				 */
7501				if (indexSkipQuals != NIL)
7502				{
7503					List	   *partialSkipQuals;
7504					Selectivity ndistinctfrac;
7505
7506					/*
7507					 * If the index is partial, AND the index predicate with
7508					 * the index-bound quals to produce a more accurate idea
7509					 * of the number of distinct values for prior indexcol
7510					 */
7511					partialSkipQuals = add_predicate_to_index_quals(index,
7512																	indexSkipQuals);
7513
7514					ndistinctfrac = clauselist_selectivity(root, partialSkipQuals,
7515														   index->rel->relid,
7516														   JOIN_INNER,
7517														   NULL);
7518
7519					/*
7520					 * If ndistinctfrac is selective (on its own), the scan is
7521					 * unlikely to benefit from repositioning itself using
7522					 * later quals.  Do not allow iclause->indexcol's quals to
7523					 * be added to indexBoundQuals (it would increase descent
7524					 * costs, without lowering numIndexTuples costs by much).
7525					 */
7526					if (ndistinctfrac < DEFAULT_RANGE_INEQ_SEL)
7527					{
7528						num_sa_scans = num_sa_scans_prev_cols;
7529						break;	/* done building indexBoundQuals */
7530					}
7531
7532					/* Adjust ndistinct downward */
7533					ndistinct = rint(ndistinct * ndistinctfrac);
7534					ndistinct = Max(ndistinct, 1);
7535				}
7536
7537				/*
7538				 * When there's no inequality quals, account for the need to
7539				 * find an initial value by counting -inf/+inf as a value.
7540				 *
7541				 * We don't charge anything extra for possible next/prior key
7542				 * index probes, which are sometimes used to find the next
7543				 * valid skip array element (ahead of using the located
7544				 * element value to relocate the scan to the next position
7545				 * that might contain matching tuples).  It seems hard to do
7546				 * better here.  Use of the skip support infrastructure often
7547				 * avoids most next/prior key probes.  But even when it can't,
7548				 * there's a decent chance that most individual next/prior key
7549				 * probes will locate a leaf page whose key space overlaps all
7550				 * of the scan's keys (even the lower-order keys) -- which
7551				 * also avoids the need for a separate, extra index descent.
7552				 * Note also that these probes are much cheaper than non-probe
7553				 * primitive index scans: they're reliably very selective.
7554				 */
7555				if (indexSkipQuals == NIL)
7556					ndistinct += 1;
7557
7558				/*
7559				 * Update num_sa_scans estimate by multiplying by ndistinct.
7560				 *
7561				 * We make the pessimistic assumption that there is no
7562				 * naturally occurring cross-column correlation.  This is
7563				 * often wrong, but it seems best to err on the side of not
7564				 * expecting skipping to be helpful...
7565				 */
7566				num_sa_scans *= ndistinct;
7567
7568				/*
7569				 * ...but back out of adding this latest group of 1 or more
7570				 * skip arrays when num_sa_scans exceeds the total number of
7571				 * index pages (revert to num_sa_scans from before indexcol).
7572				 * This causes a sharp discontinuity in cost (as a function of
7573				 * the indexcol's ndistinct), but that is representative of
7574				 * actual runtime costs.
7575				 *
7576				 * Note that skipping is helpful when each primitive index
7577				 * scan only manages to skip over 1 or 2 irrelevant leaf pages
7578				 * on average.  Skip arrays bring savings in CPU costs due to
7579				 * the scan not needing to evaluate indexquals against every
7580				 * tuple, which can greatly exceed any savings in I/O costs.
7581				 * This test is a test of whether num_sa_scans implies that
7582				 * we're past the point where the ability to skip ceases to
7583				 * lower the scan's costs (even qual evaluation CPU costs).
7584				 */
7585				if (index->pages < num_sa_scans)
7586				{
7587					num_sa_scans = num_sa_scans_prev_cols;
7588					break;		/* done building indexBoundQuals */
7589				}
7590
7591				indexcol++;
7592				indexSkipQuals = NIL;
7593			}
7594
7595			/*
7596			 * Finished considering the need to add skip arrays to bridge an
7597			 * initial eqQualHere gap between the old and new index columns
7598			 * (or there was no initial eqQualHere gap in the first place).
7599			 *
7600			 * If an initial gap could not be bridged, then new column's quals
7601			 * (i.e. iclause->indexcol's quals) won't go into indexBoundQuals,
7602			 * and so won't affect our final numIndexTuples estimate.
7603			 */
7604			if (indexcol != iclause->indexcol)
7605				break;			/* done building indexBoundQuals */
7606		}
7607
7608		Assert(indexcol == iclause->indexcol);
7609
7610		/* Examine each indexqual associated with this index clause */
7611		foreach(lc2, iclause->indexquals)
7612		{
7613			RestrictInfo *rinfo = lfirst_node(RestrictInfo, lc2);
7614			Expr	   *clause = rinfo->clause;
7615			Oid			clause_op = InvalidOid;
7616			int			op_strategy;
7617
7618			if (IsA(clause, OpExpr))
7619			{
7620				OpExpr	   *op = (OpExpr *) clause;
7621
7622				clause_op = op->opno;
7623			}
7624			else if (IsA(clause, RowCompareExpr))
7625			{
7626				RowCompareExpr *rc = (RowCompareExpr *) clause;
7627
7628				clause_op = linitial_oid(rc->opnos);
7629				found_row_compare = true;
7630			}
7631			else if (IsA(clause, ScalarArrayOpExpr))
7632			{
7633				ScalarArrayOpExpr *saop = (ScalarArrayOpExpr *) clause;
7634				Node	   *other_operand = (Node *) lsecond(saop->args);
7635				double		alength = estimate_array_length(root, other_operand);
7636
7637				clause_op = saop->opno;
7638				found_array = true;
7639				/* estimate SA descents by indexBoundQuals only */
7640				if (alength > 1)
7641					num_sa_scans *= alength;
7642			}
7643			else if (IsA(clause, NullTest))
7644			{
7645				NullTest   *nt = (NullTest *) clause;
7646
7647				if (nt->nulltesttype == IS_NULL)
7648				{
7649					found_is_null_op = true;
7650					/* IS NULL is like = for selectivity/skip scan purposes */
7651					eqQualHere = true;
7652				}
7653			}
7654			else
7655				elog(ERROR, "unsupported indexqual type: %d",
7656					 (int) nodeTag(clause));
7657
7658			/* check for equality operator */
7659			if (OidIsValid(clause_op))
7660			{
7661				op_strategy = get_op_opfamily_strategy(clause_op,
7662													   index->opfamily[indexcol]);
7663				Assert(op_strategy != 0);	/* not a member of opfamily?? */
7664				if (op_strategy == BTEqualStrategyNumber)
7665					eqQualHere = true;
7666			}
7667
7668			indexBoundQuals = lappend(indexBoundQuals, rinfo);
7669
7670			/*
7671			 * We apply inequality selectivities to estimate index descent
7672			 * costs with scans that use skip arrays.  Save this indexcol's
7673			 * RestrictInfos if it looks like they'll be needed for that.
7674			 */
7675			if (!eqQualHere && !found_row_compare &&
7676				indexcol < index->nkeycolumns - 1)
7677				indexSkipQuals = lappend(indexSkipQuals, rinfo);
7678		}
7679	}
7680
7681	/*
7682	 * If index is unique and we found an '=' clause for each column, we can
7683	 * just assume numIndexTuples = 1 and skip the expensive
7684	 * clauselist_selectivity calculations.  However, an array or NullTest
7685	 * always invalidates that theory (even when eqQualHere has been set).
7686	 */
7687	if (index->unique &&
7688		indexcol == index->nkeycolumns - 1 &&
7689		eqQualHere &&
7690		!found_array &&
7691		!found_is_null_op)
7692		numIndexTuples = 1.0;
7693	else
7694	{
7695		List	   *selectivityQuals;
7696		Selectivity btreeSelectivity;
7697
7698		/*
7699		 * If the index is partial, AND the index predicate with the
7700		 * index-bound quals to produce a more accurate idea of the number of
7701		 * rows covered by the bound conditions.
7702		 */
7703		selectivityQuals = add_predicate_to_index_quals(index, indexBoundQuals);
7704
7705		btreeSelectivity = clauselist_selectivity(root, selectivityQuals,
7706												  index->rel->relid,
7707												  JOIN_INNER,
7708												  NULL);
7709		numIndexTuples = btreeSelectivity * index->rel->tuples;
7710
7711		/*
7712		 * btree automatically combines individual array element primitive
7713		 * index scans whenever the tuples covered by the next set of array
7714		 * keys are close to tuples covered by the current set.  That puts a
7715		 * natural ceiling on the worst case number of descents -- there
7716		 * cannot possibly be more than one descent per leaf page scanned.
7717		 *
7718		 * Clamp the number of descents to at most 1/3 the number of index
7719		 * pages.  This avoids implausibly high estimates with low selectivity
7720		 * paths, where scans usually require only one or two descents.  This
7721		 * is most likely to help when there are several SAOP clauses, where
7722		 * naively accepting the total number of distinct combinations of
7723		 * array elements as the number of descents would frequently lead to
7724		 * wild overestimates.
7725		 *
7726		 * We somewhat arbitrarily don't just make the cutoff the total number
7727		 * of leaf pages (we make it 1/3 the total number of pages instead) to
7728		 * give the btree code credit for its ability to continue on the leaf
7729		 * level with low selectivity scans.
7730		 *
7731		 * Note: num_sa_scans includes both ScalarArrayOp array elements and
7732		 * skip array elements whose qual affects our numIndexTuples estimate.
7733		 */
7734		num_sa_scans = Min(num_sa_scans, ceil(index->pages * 0.3333333));
7735		num_sa_scans = Max(num_sa_scans, 1);
7736
7737		/*
7738		 * As in genericcostestimate(), we have to adjust for any array quals
7739		 * included in indexBoundQuals, and then round to integer.
7740		 *
7741		 * It is tempting to make genericcostestimate behave as if array
7742		 * clauses work in almost the same way as scalar operators during
7743		 * btree scans, making the top-level scan look like a continuous scan
7744		 * (as opposed to num_sa_scans-many primitive index scans).  After
7745		 * all, btree scans mostly work like that at runtime.  However, such a
7746		 * scheme would badly bias genericcostestimate's simplistic approach
7747		 * to calculating numIndexPages through prorating.
7748		 *
7749		 * Stick with the approach taken by non-native SAOP scans for now.
7750		 * genericcostestimate will use the Mackert-Lohman formula to
7751		 * compensate for repeat page fetches, even though that definitely
7752		 * won't happen during btree scans (not for leaf pages, at least).
7753		 * We're usually very pessimistic about the number of primitive index
7754		 * scans that will be required, but it's not clear how to do better.
7755		 */
7756		numIndexTuples = rint(numIndexTuples / num_sa_scans);
7757	}
7758
7759	/*
7760	 * Now do generic index cost estimation.
7761	 */
7762	costs.numIndexTuples = numIndexTuples;
7763	costs.num_sa_scans = num_sa_scans;
7764
7765	genericcostestimate(root, path, loop_count, &costs);
7766
7767	/*
7768	 * Add a CPU-cost component to represent the costs of initial btree
7769	 * descent.  We don't charge any I/O cost for touching upper btree levels,
7770	 * since they tend to stay in cache, but we still have to do about log2(N)
7771	 * comparisons to descend a btree of N leaf tuples.  We charge one
7772	 * cpu_operator_cost per comparison.
7773	 *
7774	 * If there are SAOP or skip array keys, charge this once per estimated
7775	 * index descent.  The ones after the first one are not startup cost so
7776	 * far as the overall plan goes, so just add them to "total" cost.
7777	 */
7778	if (index->tuples > 1)		/* avoid computing log(0) */
7779	{
7780		descentCost = ceil(log(index->tuples) / log(2.0)) * cpu_operator_cost;
7781		costs.indexStartupCost += descentCost;
7782		costs.indexTotalCost += costs.num_sa_scans * descentCost;
7783	}
7784
7785	/*
7786	 * Even though we're not charging I/O cost for touching upper btree pages,
7787	 * it's still reasonable to charge some CPU cost per page descended
7788	 * through.  Moreover, if we had no such charge at all, bloated indexes
7789	 * would appear to have the same search cost as unbloated ones, at least
7790	 * in cases where only a single leaf page is expected to be visited.  This
7791	 * cost is somewhat arbitrarily set at 50x cpu_operator_cost per page
7792	 * touched.  The number of such pages is btree tree height plus one (ie,
7793	 * we charge for the leaf page too).  As above, charge once per estimated
7794	 * SAOP/skip array descent.
7795	 */
7796	descentCost = (index->tree_height + 1) * DEFAULT_PAGE_CPU_MULTIPLIER * cpu_operator_cost;
7797	costs.indexStartupCost += descentCost;
7798	costs.indexTotalCost += costs.num_sa_scans * descentCost;
7799
7800	if (!have_correlation)
7801	{
7802		examine_indexcol_variable(root, index, 0, &vardata);
7803		if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata.statsTuple))
7804			costs.indexCorrelation = btcost_correlation(index, &vardata);
7805		ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
7806	}
7807	else
7808	{
7809		/* btcost_correlation already called earlier on */
7810		costs.indexCorrelation = correlation;
7811	}
7812
7813	*indexStartupCost = costs.indexStartupCost;
7814	*indexTotalCost = costs.indexTotalCost;
7815	*indexSelectivity = costs.indexSelectivity;
7816	*indexCorrelation = costs.indexCorrelation;
7817	*indexPages = costs.numIndexPages;
7818}
7819
7820void
7821hashcostestimate(PlannerInfo *root, IndexPath *path, double loop_count,
7822				 Cost *indexStartupCost, Cost *indexTotalCost,
7823				 Selectivity *indexSelectivity, double *indexCorrelation,
7824				 double *indexPages)
7825{
7826	GenericCosts costs = {0};
7827
7828	genericcostestimate(root, path, loop_count, &costs);
7829
7830	/*
7831	 * A hash index has no descent costs as such, since the index AM can go
7832	 * directly to the target bucket after computing the hash value.  There
7833	 * are a couple of other hash-specific costs that we could conceivably add
7834	 * here, though:
7835	 *
7836	 * Ideally we'd charge spc_random_page_cost for each page in the target
7837	 * bucket, not just the numIndexPages pages that genericcostestimate
7838	 * thought we'd visit.  However in most cases we don't know which bucket
7839	 * that will be.  There's no point in considering the average bucket size
7840	 * because the hash AM makes sure that's always one page.
7841	 *
7842	 * Likewise, we could consider charging some CPU for each index tuple in
7843	 * the bucket, if we knew how many there were.  But the per-tuple cost is
7844	 * just a hash value comparison, not a general datatype-dependent
7845	 * comparison, so any such charge ought to be quite a bit less than
7846	 * cpu_operator_cost; which makes it probably not worth worrying about.
7847	 *
7848	 * A bigger issue is that chance hash-value collisions will result in
7849	 * wasted probes into the heap.  We don't currently attempt to model this
7850	 * cost on the grounds that it's rare, but maybe it's not rare enough.
7851	 * (Any fix for this ought to consider the generic lossy-operator problem,
7852	 * though; it's not entirely hash-specific.)
7853	 */
7854
7855	*indexStartupCost = costs.indexStartupCost;
7856	*indexTotalCost = costs.indexTotalCost;
7857	*indexSelectivity = costs.indexSelectivity;
7858	*indexCorrelation = costs.indexCorrelation;
7859	*indexPages = costs.numIndexPages;
7860}
7861
7862void
7863gistcostestimate(PlannerInfo *root, IndexPath *path, double loop_count,
7864				 Cost *indexStartupCost, Cost *indexTotalCost,
7865				 Selectivity *indexSelectivity, double *indexCorrelation,
7866				 double *indexPages)
7867{
7868	IndexOptInfo *index = path->indexinfo;
7869	GenericCosts costs = {0};
7870	Cost		descentCost;
7871
7872	genericcostestimate(root, path, loop_count, &costs);
7873
7874	/*
7875	 * We model index descent costs similarly to those for btree, but to do
7876	 * that we first need an idea of the tree height.  We somewhat arbitrarily
7877	 * assume that the fanout is 100, meaning the tree height is at most
7878	 * log100(index->pages).
7879	 *
7880	 * Although this computation isn't really expensive enough to require
7881	 * caching, we might as well use index->tree_height to cache it.
7882	 */
7883	if (index->tree_height < 0) /* unknown? */
7884	{
7885		if (index->pages > 1)	/* avoid computing log(0) */
7886			index->tree_height = (int) (log(index->pages) / log(100.0));
7887		else
7888			index->tree_height = 0;
7889	}
7890
7891	/*
7892	 * Add a CPU-cost component to represent the costs of initial descent. We
7893	 * just use log(N) here not log2(N) since the branching factor isn't
7894	 * necessarily two anyway.  As for btree, charge once per SA scan.
7895	 */
7896	if (index->tuples > 1)		/* avoid computing log(0) */
7897	{
7898		descentCost = ceil(log(index->tuples)) * cpu_operator_cost;
7899		costs.indexStartupCost += descentCost;
7900		costs.indexTotalCost += costs.num_sa_scans * descentCost;
7901	}
7902
7903	/*
7904	 * Likewise add a per-page charge, calculated the same as for btrees.
7905	 */
7906	descentCost = (index->tree_height + 1) * DEFAULT_PAGE_CPU_MULTIPLIER * cpu_operator_cost;
7907	costs.indexStartupCost += descentCost;
7908	costs.indexTotalCost += costs.num_sa_scans * descentCost;
7909
7910	*indexStartupCost = costs.indexStartupCost;
7911	*indexTotalCost = costs.indexTotalCost;
7912	*indexSelectivity = costs.indexSelectivity;
7913	*indexCorrelation = costs.indexCorrelation;
7914	*indexPages = costs.numIndexPages;
7915}
7916
7917void
7918spgcostestimate(PlannerInfo *root, IndexPath *path, double loop_count,
7919				Cost *indexStartupCost, Cost *indexTotalCost,
7920				Selectivity *indexSelectivity, double *indexCorrelation,
7921				double *indexPages)
7922{
7923	IndexOptInfo *index = path->indexinfo;
7924	GenericCosts costs = {0};
7925	Cost		descentCost;
7926
7927	genericcostestimate(root, path, loop_count, &costs);
7928
7929	/*
7930	 * We model index descent costs similarly to those for btree, but to do
7931	 * that we first need an idea of the tree height.  We somewhat arbitrarily
7932	 * assume that the fanout is 100, meaning the tree height is at most
7933	 * log100(index->pages).
7934	 *
7935	 * Although this computation isn't really expensive enough to require
7936	 * caching, we might as well use index->tree_height to cache it.
7937	 */
7938	if (index->tree_height < 0) /* unknown? */
7939	{
7940		if (index->pages > 1)	/* avoid computing log(0) */
7941			index->tree_height = (int) (log(index->pages) / log(100.0));
7942		else
7943			index->tree_height = 0;
7944	}
7945
7946	/*
7947	 * Add a CPU-cost component to represent the costs of initial descent. We
7948	 * just use log(N) here not log2(N) since the branching factor isn't
7949	 * necessarily two anyway.  As for btree, charge once per SA scan.
7950	 */
7951	if (index->tuples > 1)		/* avoid computing log(0) */
7952	{
7953		descentCost = ceil(log(index->tuples)) * cpu_operator_cost;
7954		costs.indexStartupCost += descentCost;
7955		costs.indexTotalCost += costs.num_sa_scans * descentCost;
7956	}
7957
7958	/*
7959	 * Likewise add a per-page charge, calculated the same as for btrees.
7960	 */
7961	descentCost = (index->tree_height + 1) * DEFAULT_PAGE_CPU_MULTIPLIER * cpu_operator_cost;
7962	costs.indexStartupCost += descentCost;
7963	costs.indexTotalCost += costs.num_sa_scans * descentCost;
7964
7965	*indexStartupCost = costs.indexStartupCost;
7966	*indexTotalCost = costs.indexTotalCost;
7967	*indexSelectivity = costs.indexSelectivity;
7968	*indexCorrelation = costs.indexCorrelation;
7969	*indexPages = costs.numIndexPages;
7970}
7971
7972
7973/*
7974 * Support routines for gincostestimate
7975 */
7976
7977typedef struct
7978{
7979	bool		attHasFullScan[INDEX_MAX_KEYS];
7980	bool		attHasNormalScan[INDEX_MAX_KEYS];
7981	double		partialEntries;
7982	double		exactEntries;
7983	double		searchEntries;
7984	double		arrayScans;
7985} GinQualCounts;
7986
7987/*
7988 * Estimate the number of index terms that need to be searched for while
7989 * testing the given GIN query, and increment the counts in *counts
7990 * appropriately.  If the query is unsatisfiable, return false.
7991 */
7992static bool
7993gincost_pattern(IndexOptInfo *index, int indexcol,
7994				Oid clause_op, Datum query,
7995				GinQualCounts *counts)
7996{
7997	FmgrInfo	flinfo;
7998	Oid			extractProcOid;
7999	Oid			collation;
8000	int			strategy_op;
8001	Oid			lefttype,
8002				righttype;
8003	int32		nentries = 0;
8004	bool	   *partial_matches = NULL;
8005	Pointer    *extra_data = NULL;
8006	bool	   *nullFlags = NULL;
8007	int32		searchMode = GIN_SEARCH_MODE_DEFAULT;
8008	int32		i;
8009
8010	Assert(indexcol < index->nkeycolumns);
8011
8012	/*
8013	 * Get the operator's strategy number and declared input data types within
8014	 * the index opfamily.  (We don't need the latter, but we use
8015	 * get_op_opfamily_properties because it will throw error if it fails to
8016	 * find a matching pg_amop entry.)
8017	 */
8018	get_op_opfamily_properties(clause_op, index->opfamily[indexcol], false,
8019							   &strategy_op, &lefttype, &righttype);
8020
8021	/*
8022	 * GIN always uses the "default" support functions, which are those with
8023	 * lefttype == righttype == the opclass' opcintype (see
8024	 * IndexSupportInitialize in relcache.c).
8025	 */
8026	extractProcOid = get_opfamily_proc(index->opfamily[indexcol],
8027									   index->opcintype[indexcol],
8028									   index->opcintype[indexcol],
8029									   GIN_EXTRACTQUERY_PROC);
8030
8031	if (!OidIsValid(extractProcOid))
8032	{
8033		/* should not happen; throw same error as index_getprocinfo */
8034		elog(ERROR, "missing support function %d for attribute %d of index \"%s\"",
8035			 GIN_EXTRACTQUERY_PROC, indexcol + 1,
8036			 get_rel_name(index->indexoid));
8037	}
8038
8039	/*
8040	 * Choose collation to pass to extractProc (should match initGinState).
8041	 */
8042	if (OidIsValid(index->indexcollations[indexcol]))
8043		collation = index->indexcollations[indexcol];
8044	else
8045		collation = DEFAULT_COLLATION_OID;
8046
8047	fmgr_info(extractProcOid, &flinfo);
8048
8049	set_fn_opclass_options(&flinfo, index->opclassoptions[indexcol]);
8050
8051	FunctionCall7Coll(&flinfo,
8052					  collation,
8053					  query,
8054					  PointerGetDatum(&nentries),
8055					  UInt16GetDatum(strategy_op),
8056					  PointerGetDatum(&partial_matches),
8057					  PointerGetDatum(&extra_data),
8058					  PointerGetDatum(&nullFlags),
8059					  PointerGetDatum(&searchMode));
8060
8061	if (nentries <= 0 && searchMode == GIN_SEARCH_MODE_DEFAULT)
8062	{
8063		/* No match is possible */
8064		return false;
8065	}
8066
8067	for (i = 0; i < nentries; i++)
8068	{
8069		/*
8070		 * For partial match we haven't any information to estimate number of
8071		 * matched entries in index, so, we just estimate it as 100
8072		 */
8073		if (partial_matches && partial_matches[i])
8074			counts->partialEntries += 100;
8075		else
8076			counts->exactEntries++;
8077
8078		counts->searchEntries++;
8079	}
8080
8081	if (searchMode == GIN_SEARCH_MODE_DEFAULT)
8082	{
8083		counts->attHasNormalScan[indexcol] = true;
8084	}
8085	else if (searchMode == GIN_SEARCH_MODE_INCLUDE_EMPTY)
8086	{
8087		/* Treat "include empty" like an exact-match item */
8088		counts->attHasNormalScan[indexcol] = true;
8089		counts->exactEntries++;
8090		counts->searchEntries++;
8091	}
8092	else
8093	{
8094		/* It's GIN_SEARCH_MODE_ALL */
8095		counts->attHasFullScan[indexcol] = true;
8096	}
8097
8098	return true;
8099}
8100
8101/*
8102 * Estimate the number of index terms that need to be searched for while
8103 * testing the given GIN index clause, and increment the counts in *counts
8104 * appropriately.  If the query is unsatisfiable, return false.
8105 */
8106static bool
8107gincost_opexpr(PlannerInfo *root,
8108			   IndexOptInfo *index,
8109			   int indexcol,
8110			   OpExpr *clause,
8111			   GinQualCounts *counts)
8112{
8113	Oid			clause_op = clause->opno;
8114	Node	   *operand = (Node *) lsecond(clause->args);
8115
8116	/* aggressively reduce to a constant, and look through relabeling */
8117	operand = estimate_expression_value(root, operand);
8118
8119	if (IsA(operand, RelabelType))
8120		operand = (Node *) ((RelabelType *) operand)->arg;
8121
8122	/*
8123	 * It's impossible to call extractQuery method for unknown operand. So
8124	 * unless operand is a Const we can't do much; just assume there will be
8125	 * one ordinary search entry from the operand at runtime.
8126	 */
8127	if (!IsA(operand, Const))
8128	{
8129		counts->exactEntries++;
8130		counts->searchEntries++;
8131		return true;
8132	}
8133
8134	/* If Const is null, there can be no matches */
8135	if (((Const *) operand)->constisnull)
8136		return false;
8137
8138	/* Otherwise, apply extractQuery and get the actual term counts */
8139	return gincost_pattern(index, indexcol, clause_op,
8140						   ((Const *) operand)->constvalue,
8141						   counts);
8142}
8143
8144/*
8145 * Estimate the number of index terms that need to be searched for while
8146 * testing the given GIN index clause, and increment the counts in *counts
8147 * appropriately.  If the query is unsatisfiable, return false.
8148 *
8149 * A ScalarArrayOpExpr will give rise to N separate indexscans at runtime,
8150 * each of which involves one value from the RHS array, plus all the
8151 * non-array quals (if any).  To model this, we average the counts across
8152 * the RHS elements, and add the averages to the counts in *counts (which
8153 * correspond to per-indexscan costs).  We also multiply counts->arrayScans
8154 * by N, causing gincostestimate to scale up its estimates accordingly.
8155 */
8156static bool
8157gincost_scalararrayopexpr(PlannerInfo *root,
8158						  IndexOptInfo *index,
8159						  int indexcol,
8160						  ScalarArrayOpExpr *clause,
8161						  double numIndexEntries,
8162						  GinQualCounts *counts)
8163{
8164	Oid			clause_op = clause->opno;
8165	Node	   *rightop = (Node *) lsecond(clause->args);
8166	ArrayType  *arrayval;
8167	int16		elmlen;
8168	bool		elmbyval;
8169	char		elmalign;
8170	int			numElems;
8171	Datum	   *elemValues;
8172	bool	   *elemNulls;
8173	GinQualCounts arraycounts;
8174	int			numPossible = 0;
8175	int			i;
8176
8177	Assert(clause->useOr);
8178
8179	/* aggressively reduce to a constant, and look through relabeling */
8180	rightop = estimate_expression_value(root, rightop);
8181
8182	if (IsA(rightop, RelabelType))
8183		rightop = (Node *) ((RelabelType *) rightop)->arg;
8184
8185	/*
8186	 * It's impossible to call extractQuery method for unknown operand. So
8187	 * unless operand is a Const we can't do much; just assume there will be
8188	 * one ordinary search entry from each array entry at runtime, and fall
8189	 * back on a probably-bad estimate of the number of array entries.
8190	 */
8191	if (!IsA(rightop, Const))
8192	{
8193		counts->exactEntries++;
8194		counts->searchEntries++;
8195		counts->arrayScans *= estimate_array_length(root, rightop);
8196		return true;
8197	}
8198
8199	/* If Const is null, there can be no matches */
8200	if (((Const *) rightop)->constisnull)
8201		return false;
8202
8203	/* Otherwise, extract the array elements and iterate over them */
8204	arrayval = DatumGetArrayTypeP(((Const *) rightop)->constvalue);
8205	get_typlenbyvalalign(ARR_ELEMTYPE(arrayval),
8206						 &elmlen, &elmbyval, &elmalign);
8207	deconstruct_array(arrayval,
8208					  ARR_ELEMTYPE(arrayval),
8209					  elmlen, elmbyval, elmalign,
8210					  &elemValues, &elemNulls, &numElems);
8211
8212	memset(&arraycounts, 0, sizeof(arraycounts));
8213
8214	for (i = 0; i < numElems; i++)
8215	{
8216		GinQualCounts elemcounts;
8217
8218		/* NULL can't match anything, so ignore, as the executor will */
8219		if (elemNulls[i])
8220			continue;
8221
8222		/* Otherwise, apply extractQuery and get the actual term counts */
8223		memset(&elemcounts, 0, sizeof(elemcounts));
8224
8225		if (gincost_pattern(index, indexcol, clause_op, elemValues[i],
8226							&elemcounts))
8227		{
8228			/* We ignore array elements that are unsatisfiable patterns */
8229			numPossible++;
8230
8231			if (elemcounts.attHasFullScan[indexcol] &&
8232				!elemcounts.attHasNormalScan[indexcol])
8233			{
8234				/*
8235				 * Full index scan will be required.  We treat this as if
8236				 * every key in the index had been listed in the query; is
8237				 * that reasonable?
8238				 */
8239				elemcounts.partialEntries = 0;
8240				elemcounts.exactEntries = numIndexEntries;
8241				elemcounts.searchEntries = numIndexEntries;
8242			}
8243			arraycounts.partialEntries += elemcounts.partialEntries;
8244			arraycounts.exactEntries += elemcounts.exactEntries;
8245			arraycounts.searchEntries += elemcounts.searchEntries;
8246		}
8247	}
8248
8249	if (numPossible == 0)
8250	{
8251		/* No satisfiable patterns in the array */
8252		return false;
8253	}
8254
8255	/*
8256	 * Now add the averages to the global counts.  This will give us an
8257	 * estimate of the average number of terms searched for in each indexscan,
8258	 * including contributions from both array and non-array quals.
8259	 */
8260	counts->partialEntries += arraycounts.partialEntries / numPossible;
8261	counts->exactEntries += arraycounts.exactEntries / numPossible;
8262	counts->searchEntries += arraycounts.searchEntries / numPossible;
8263
8264	counts->arrayScans *= numPossible;
8265
8266	return true;
8267}
8268
8269/*
8270 * GIN has search behavior completely different from other index types
8271 */
8272void
8273gincostestimate(PlannerInfo *root, IndexPath *path, double loop_count,
8274				Cost *indexStartupCost, Cost *indexTotalCost,
8275				Selectivity *indexSelectivity, double *indexCorrelation,
8276				double *indexPages)
8277{
8278	IndexOptInfo *index = path->indexinfo;
8279	List	   *indexQuals = get_quals_from_indexclauses(path->indexclauses);
8280	List	   *selectivityQuals;
8281	double		numPages = index->pages,
8282				numTuples = index->tuples;
8283	double		numEntryPages,
8284				numDataPages,
8285				numPendingPages,
8286				numEntries;
8287	GinQualCounts counts;
8288	bool		matchPossible;
8289	bool		fullIndexScan;
8290	double		partialScale;
8291	double		entryPagesFetched,
8292				dataPagesFetched,
8293				dataPagesFetchedBySel;
8294	double		qual_op_cost,
8295				qual_arg_cost,
8296				spc_random_page_cost,
8297				outer_scans;
8298	Cost		descentCost;
8299	Relation	indexRel;
8300	GinStatsData ginStats;
8301	ListCell   *lc;
8302	int			i;
8303
8304	/*
8305	 * Obtain statistical information from the meta page, if possible.  Else
8306	 * set ginStats to zeroes, and we'll cope below.
8307	 */
8308	if (!index->hypothetical)
8309	{
8310		/* Lock should have already been obtained in plancat.c */
8311		indexRel = index_open(index->indexoid, NoLock);
8312		ginGetStats(indexRel, &ginStats);
8313		index_close(indexRel, NoLock);
8314	}
8315	else
8316	{
8317		memset(&ginStats, 0, sizeof(ginStats));
8318	}
8319
8320	/*
8321	 * Assuming we got valid (nonzero) stats at all, nPendingPages can be
8322	 * trusted, but the other fields are data as of the last VACUUM.  We can
8323	 * scale them up to account for growth since then, but that method only
8324	 * goes so far; in the worst case, the stats might be for a completely
8325	 * empty index, and scaling them will produce pretty bogus numbers.
8326	 * Somewhat arbitrarily, set the cutoff for doing scaling at 4X growth; if
8327	 * it's grown more than that, fall back to estimating things only from the
8328	 * assumed-accurate index size.  But we'll trust nPendingPages in any case
8329	 * so long as it's not clearly insane, ie, more than the index size.
8330	 */
8331	if (ginStats.nPendingPages < numPages)
8332		numPendingPages = ginStats.nPendingPages;
8333	else
8334		numPendingPages = 0;
8335
8336	if (numPages > 0 && ginStats.nTotalPages <= numPages &&
8337		ginStats.nTotalPages > numPages / 4 &&
8338		ginStats.nEntryPages > 0 && ginStats.nEntries > 0)
8339	{
8340		/*
8341		 * OK, the stats seem close enough to sane to be trusted.  But we
8342		 * still need to scale them by the ratio numPages / nTotalPages to
8343		 * account for growth since the last VACUUM.
8344		 */
8345		double		scale = numPages / ginStats.nTotalPages;
8346
8347		numEntryPages = ceil(ginStats.nEntryPages * scale);
8348		numDataPages = ceil(ginStats.nDataPages * scale);
8349		numEntries = ceil(ginStats.nEntries * scale);
8350		/* ensure we didn't round up too much */
8351		numEntryPages = Min(numEntryPages, numPages - numPendingPages);
8352		numDataPages = Min(numDataPages,
8353						   numPages - numPendingPages - numEntryPages);
8354	}
8355	else
8356	{
8357		/*
8358		 * We might get here because it's a hypothetical index, or an index
8359		 * created pre-9.1 and never vacuumed since upgrading (in which case
8360		 * its stats would read as zeroes), or just because it's grown too
8361		 * much since the last VACUUM for us to put our faith in scaling.
8362		 *
8363		 * Invent some plausible internal statistics based on the index page
8364		 * count (and clamp that to at least 10 pages, just in case).  We
8365		 * estimate that 90% of the index is entry pages, and the rest is data
8366		 * pages.  Estimate 100 entries per entry page; this is rather bogus
8367		 * since it'll depend on the size of the keys, but it's more robust
8368		 * than trying to predict the number of entries per heap tuple.
8369		 */
8370		numPages = Max(numPages, 10);
8371		numEntryPages = floor((numPages - numPendingPages) * 0.90);
8372		numDataPages = numPages - numPendingPages - numEntryPages;
8373		numEntries = floor(numEntryPages * 100);
8374	}
8375
8376	/* In an empty index, numEntries could be zero.  Avoid divide-by-zero */
8377	if (numEntries < 1)
8378		numEntries = 1;
8379
8380	/*
8381	 * If the index is partial, AND the index predicate with the index-bound
8382	 * quals to produce a more accurate idea of the number of rows covered by
8383	 * the bound conditions.
8384	 */
8385	selectivityQuals = add_predicate_to_index_quals(index, indexQuals);
8386
8387	/* Estimate the fraction of main-table tuples that will be visited */
8388	*indexSelectivity = clauselist_selectivity(root, selectivityQuals,
8389											   index->rel->relid,
8390											   JOIN_INNER,
8391											   NULL);
8392
8393	/* fetch estimated page cost for tablespace containing index */
8394	get_tablespace_page_costs(index->reltablespace,
8395							  &spc_random_page_cost,
8396							  NULL);
8397
8398	/*
8399	 * Generic assumption about index correlation: there isn't any.
8400	 */
8401	*indexCorrelation = 0.0;
8402
8403	/*
8404	 * Examine quals to estimate number of search entries & partial matches
8405	 */
8406	memset(&counts, 0, sizeof(counts));
8407	counts.arrayScans = 1;
8408	matchPossible = true;
8409
8410	foreach(lc, path->indexclauses)
8411	{
8412		IndexClause *iclause = lfirst_node(IndexClause, lc);
8413		ListCell   *lc2;
8414
8415		foreach(lc2, iclause->indexquals)
8416		{
8417			RestrictInfo *rinfo = lfirst_node(RestrictInfo, lc2);
8418			Expr	   *clause = rinfo->clause;
8419
8420			if (IsA(clause, OpExpr))
8421			{
8422				matchPossible = gincost_opexpr(root,
8423											   index,
8424											   iclause->indexcol,
8425											   (OpExpr *) clause,
8426											   &counts);
8427				if (!matchPossible)
8428					break;
8429			}
8430			else if (IsA(clause, ScalarArrayOpExpr))
8431			{
8432				matchPossible = gincost_scalararrayopexpr(root,
8433														  index,
8434														  iclause->indexcol,
8435														  (ScalarArrayOpExpr *) clause,
8436														  numEntries,
8437														  &counts);
8438				if (!matchPossible)
8439					break;
8440			}
8441			else
8442			{
8443				/* shouldn't be anything else for a GIN index */
8444				elog(ERROR, "unsupported GIN indexqual type: %d",
8445					 (int) nodeTag(clause));
8446			}
8447		}
8448	}
8449
8450	/* Fall out if there were any provably-unsatisfiable quals */
8451	if (!matchPossible)
8452	{
8453		*indexStartupCost = 0;
8454		*indexTotalCost = 0;
8455		*indexSelectivity = 0;
8456		return;
8457	}
8458
8459	/*
8460	 * If attribute has a full scan and at the same time doesn't have normal
8461	 * scan, then we'll have to scan all non-null entries of that attribute.
8462	 * Currently, we don't have per-attribute statistics for GIN.  Thus, we
8463	 * must assume the whole GIN index has to be scanned in this case.
8464	 */
8465	fullIndexScan = false;
8466	for (i = 0; i < index->nkeycolumns; i++)
8467	{
8468		if (counts.attHasFullScan[i] && !counts.attHasNormalScan[i])
8469		{
8470			fullIndexScan = true;
8471			break;
8472		}
8473	}
8474
8475	if (fullIndexScan || indexQuals == NIL)
8476	{
8477		/*
8478		 * Full index scan will be required.  We treat this as if every key in
8479		 * the index had been listed in the query; is that reasonable?
8480		 */
8481		counts.partialEntries = 0;
8482		counts.exactEntries = numEntries;
8483		counts.searchEntries = numEntries;
8484	}
8485
8486	/* Will we have more than one iteration of a nestloop scan? */
8487	outer_scans = loop_count;
8488
8489	/*
8490	 * Compute cost to begin scan, first of all, pay attention to pending
8491	 * list.
8492	 */
8493	entryPagesFetched = numPendingPages;
8494
8495	/*
8496	 * Estimate number of entry pages read.  We need to do
8497	 * counts.searchEntries searches.  Use a power function as it should be,
8498	 * but tuples on leaf pages usually is much greater. Here we include all
8499	 * searches in entry tree, including search of first entry in partial
8500	 * match algorithm
8501	 */
8502	entryPagesFetched += ceil(counts.searchEntries * rint(pow(numEntryPages, 0.15)));
8503
8504	/*
8505	 * Add an estimate of entry pages read by partial match algorithm. It's a
8506	 * scan over leaf pages in entry tree.  We haven't any useful stats here,
8507	 * so estimate it as proportion.  Because counts.partialEntries is really
8508	 * pretty bogus (see code above), it's possible that it is more than
8509	 * numEntries; clamp the proportion to ensure sanity.
8510	 */
8511	partialScale = counts.partialEntries / numEntries;
8512	partialScale = Min(partialScale, 1.0);
8513
8514	entryPagesFetched += ceil(numEntryPages * partialScale);
8515
8516	/*
8517	 * Partial match algorithm reads all data pages before doing actual scan,
8518	 * so it's a startup cost.  Again, we haven't any useful stats here, so
8519	 * estimate it as proportion.
8520	 */
8521	dataPagesFetched = ceil(numDataPages * partialScale);
8522
8523	*indexStartupCost = 0;
8524	*indexTotalCost = 0;
8525
8526	/*
8527	 * Add a CPU-cost component to represent the costs of initial entry btree
8528	 * descent.  We don't charge any I/O cost for touching upper btree levels,
8529	 * since they tend to stay in cache, but we still have to do about log2(N)
8530	 * comparisons to descend a btree of N leaf tuples.  We charge one
8531	 * cpu_operator_cost per comparison.
8532	 *
8533	 * If there are ScalarArrayOpExprs, charge this once per SA scan.  The
8534	 * ones after the first one are not startup cost so far as the overall
8535	 * plan is concerned, so add them only to "total" cost.
8536	 */
8537	if (numEntries > 1)			/* avoid computing log(0) */
8538	{
8539		descentCost = ceil(log(numEntries) / log(2.0)) * cpu_operator_cost;
8540		*indexStartupCost += descentCost * counts.searchEntries;
8541		*indexTotalCost += counts.arrayScans * descentCost * counts.searchEntries;
8542	}
8543
8544	/*
8545	 * Add a cpu cost per entry-page fetched. This is not amortized over a
8546	 * loop.
8547	 */
8548	*indexStartupCost += entryPagesFetched * DEFAULT_PAGE_CPU_MULTIPLIER * cpu_operator_cost;
8549	*indexTotalCost += entryPagesFetched * counts.arrayScans * DEFAULT_PAGE_CPU_MULTIPLIER * cpu_operator_cost;
8550
8551	/*
8552	 * Add a cpu cost per data-page fetched. This is also not amortized over a
8553	 * loop. Since those are the data pages from the partial match algorithm,
8554	 * charge them as startup cost.
8555	 */
8556	*indexStartupCost += DEFAULT_PAGE_CPU_MULTIPLIER * cpu_operator_cost * dataPagesFetched;
8557
8558	/*
8559	 * Since we add the startup cost to the total cost later on, remove the
8560	 * initial arrayscan from the total.
8561	 */
8562	*indexTotalCost += dataPagesFetched * (counts.arrayScans - 1) * DEFAULT_PAGE_CPU_MULTIPLIER * cpu_operator_cost;
8563
8564	/*
8565	 * Calculate cache effects if more than one scan due to nestloops or array
8566	 * quals.  The result is pro-rated per nestloop scan, but the array qual
8567	 * factor shouldn't be pro-rated (compare genericcostestimate).
8568	 */
8569	if (outer_scans > 1 || counts.arrayScans > 1)
8570	{
8571		entryPagesFetched *= outer_scans * counts.arrayScans;
8572		entryPagesFetched = index_pages_fetched(entryPagesFetched,
8573												(BlockNumber) numEntryPages,
8574												numEntryPages, root);
8575		entryPagesFetched /= outer_scans;
8576		dataPagesFetched *= outer_scans * counts.arrayScans;
8577		dataPagesFetched = index_pages_fetched(dataPagesFetched,
8578											   (BlockNumber) numDataPages,
8579											   numDataPages, root);
8580		dataPagesFetched /= outer_scans;
8581	}
8582
8583	/*
8584	 * Here we use random page cost because logically-close pages could be far
8585	 * apart on disk.
8586	 */
8587	*indexStartupCost += (entryPagesFetched + dataPagesFetched) * spc_random_page_cost;
8588
8589	/*
8590	 * Now compute the number of data pages fetched during the scan.
8591	 *
8592	 * We assume every entry to have the same number of items, and that there
8593	 * is no overlap between them. (XXX: tsvector and array opclasses collect
8594	 * statistics on the frequency of individual keys; it would be nice to use
8595	 * those here.)
8596	 */
8597	dataPagesFetched = ceil(numDataPages * counts.exactEntries / numEntries);
8598
8599	/*
8600	 * If there is a lot of overlap among the entries, in particular if one of
8601	 * the entries is very frequent, the above calculation can grossly
8602	 * under-estimate.  As a simple cross-check, calculate a lower bound based
8603	 * on the overall selectivity of the quals.  At a minimum, we must read
8604	 * one item pointer for each matching entry.
8605	 *
8606	 * The width of each item pointer varies, based on the level of
8607	 * compression.  We don't have statistics on that, but an average of
8608	 * around 3 bytes per item is fairly typical.
8609	 */
8610	dataPagesFetchedBySel = ceil(*indexSelectivity *
8611								 (numTuples / (BLCKSZ / 3)));
8612	if (dataPagesFetchedBySel > dataPagesFetched)
8613		dataPagesFetched = dataPagesFetchedBySel;
8614
8615	/* Add one page cpu-cost to the startup cost */
8616	*indexStartupCost += DEFAULT_PAGE_CPU_MULTIPLIER * cpu_operator_cost * counts.searchEntries;
8617
8618	/*
8619	 * Add once again a CPU-cost for those data pages, before amortizing for
8620	 * cache.
8621	 */
8622	*indexTotalCost += dataPagesFetched * counts.arrayScans * DEFAULT_PAGE_CPU_MULTIPLIER * cpu_operator_cost;
8623
8624	/* Account for cache effects, the same as above */
8625	if (outer_scans > 1 || counts.arrayScans > 1)
8626	{
8627		dataPagesFetched *= outer_scans * counts.arrayScans;
8628		dataPagesFetched = index_pages_fetched(dataPagesFetched,
8629											   (BlockNumber) numDataPages,
8630											   numDataPages, root);
8631		dataPagesFetched /= outer_scans;
8632	}
8633
8634	/* And apply random_page_cost as the cost per page */
8635	*indexTotalCost += *indexStartupCost +
8636		dataPagesFetched * spc_random_page_cost;
8637
8638	/*
8639	 * Add on index qual eval costs, much as in genericcostestimate. We charge
8640	 * cpu but we can disregard indexorderbys, since GIN doesn't support
8641	 * those.
8642	 */
8643	qual_arg_cost = index_other_operands_eval_cost(root, indexQuals);
8644	qual_op_cost = cpu_operator_cost * list_length(indexQuals);
8645
8646	*indexStartupCost += qual_arg_cost;
8647	*indexTotalCost += qual_arg_cost;
8648
8649	/*
8650	 * Add a cpu cost per search entry, corresponding to the actual visited
8651	 * entries.
8652	 */
8653	*indexTotalCost += (counts.searchEntries * counts.arrayScans) * (qual_op_cost);
8654	/* Now add a cpu cost per tuple in the posting lists / trees */
8655	*indexTotalCost += (numTuples * *indexSelectivity) * (cpu_index_tuple_cost);
8656	*indexPages = dataPagesFetched;
8657}
8658
8659/*
8660 * BRIN has search behavior completely different from other index types
8661 */
8662void
8663brincostestimate(PlannerInfo *root, IndexPath *path, double loop_count,
8664				 Cost *indexStartupCost, Cost *indexTotalCost,
8665				 Selectivity *indexSelectivity, double *indexCorrelation,
8666				 double *indexPages)
8667{
8668	IndexOptInfo *index = path->indexinfo;
8669	List	   *indexQuals = get_quals_from_indexclauses(path->indexclauses);
8670	double		numPages = index->pages;
8671	RelOptInfo *baserel = index->rel;
8672	RangeTblEntry *rte = planner_rt_fetch(baserel->relid, root);
8673	Cost		spc_seq_page_cost;
8674	Cost		spc_random_page_cost;
8675	double		qual_arg_cost;
8676	double		qualSelectivity;
8677	BrinStatsData statsData;
8678	double		indexRanges;
8679	double		minimalRanges;
8680	double		estimatedRanges;
8681	double		selec;
8682	Relation	indexRel;
8683	ListCell   *l;
8684	VariableStatData vardata;
8685
8686	Assert(rte->rtekind == RTE_RELATION);
8687
8688	/* fetch estimated page cost for the tablespace containing the index */
8689	get_tablespace_page_costs(index->reltablespace,
8690							  &spc_random_page_cost,
8691							  &spc_seq_page_cost);
8692
8693	/*
8694	 * Obtain some data from the index itself, if possible.  Otherwise invent
8695	 * some plausible internal statistics based on the relation page count.
8696	 */
8697	if (!index->hypothetical)
8698	{
8699		/*
8700		 * A lock should have already been obtained on the index in plancat.c.
8701		 */
8702		indexRel = index_open(index->indexoid, NoLock);
8703		brinGetStats(indexRel, &statsData);
8704		index_close(indexRel, NoLock);
8705
8706		/* work out the actual number of ranges in the index */
8707		indexRanges = Max(ceil((double) baserel->pages /
8708							   statsData.pagesPerRange), 1.0);
8709	}
8710	else
8711	{
8712		/*
8713		 * Assume default number of pages per range, and estimate the number
8714		 * of ranges based on that.
8715		 */
8716		indexRanges = Max(ceil((double) baserel->pages /
8717							   BRIN_DEFAULT_PAGES_PER_RANGE), 1.0);
8718
8719		statsData.pagesPerRange = BRIN_DEFAULT_PAGES_PER_RANGE;
8720		statsData.revmapNumPages = (indexRanges / REVMAP_PAGE_MAXITEMS) + 1;
8721	}
8722
8723	/*
8724	 * Compute index correlation
8725	 *
8726	 * Because we can use all index quals equally when scanning, we can use
8727	 * the largest correlation (in absolute value) among columns used by the
8728	 * query.  Start at zero, the worst possible case.  If we cannot find any
8729	 * correlation statistics, we will keep it as 0.
8730	 */
8731	*indexCorrelation = 0;
8732
8733	foreach(l, path->indexclauses)
8734	{
8735		IndexClause *iclause = lfirst_node(IndexClause, l);
8736		AttrNumber	attnum = index->indexkeys[iclause->indexcol];
8737
8738		/* attempt to lookup stats in relation for this index column */
8739		if (attnum != 0)
8740		{
8741			/* Simple variable -- look to stats for the underlying table */
8742			if (get_relation_stats_hook &&
8743				(*get_relation_stats_hook) (root, rte, attnum, &vardata))
8744			{
8745				/*
8746				 * The hook took control of acquiring a stats tuple.  If it
8747				 * did supply a tuple, it'd better have supplied a freefunc.
8748				 */
8749				if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata.statsTuple) && !vardata.freefunc)
8750					elog(ERROR,
8751						 "no function provided to release variable stats with");
8752			}
8753			else
8754			{
8755				vardata.statsTuple =
8756					SearchSysCache3(STATRELATTINH,
8757									ObjectIdGetDatum(rte->relid),
8758									Int16GetDatum(attnum),
8759									BoolGetDatum(false));
8760				vardata.freefunc = ReleaseSysCache;
8761			}
8762		}
8763		else
8764		{
8765			/*
8766			 * Looks like we've found an expression column in the index. Let's
8767			 * see if there's any stats for it.
8768			 */
8769
8770			/* get the attnum from the 0-based index. */
8771			attnum = iclause->indexcol + 1;
8772
8773			if (get_index_stats_hook &&
8774				(*get_index_stats_hook) (root, index->indexoid, attnum, &vardata))
8775			{
8776				/*
8777				 * The hook took control of acquiring a stats tuple.  If it
8778				 * did supply a tuple, it'd better have supplied a freefunc.
8779				 */
8780				if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata.statsTuple) &&
8781					!vardata.freefunc)
8782					elog(ERROR, "no function provided to release variable stats with");
8783			}
8784			else
8785			{
8786				vardata.statsTuple = SearchSysCache3(STATRELATTINH,
8787													 ObjectIdGetDatum(index->indexoid),
8788													 Int16GetDatum(attnum),
8789													 BoolGetDatum(false));
8790				vardata.freefunc = ReleaseSysCache;
8791			}
8792		}
8793
8794		if (HeapTupleIsValid(vardata.statsTuple))
8795		{
8796			AttStatsSlot sslot;
8797
8798			if (get_attstatsslot(&sslot, vardata.statsTuple,
8799								 STATISTIC_KIND_CORRELATION, InvalidOid,
8800								 ATTSTATSSLOT_NUMBERS))
8801			{
8802				double		varCorrelation = 0.0;
8803
8804				if (sslot.nnumbers > 0)
8805					varCorrelation = fabs(sslot.numbers[0]);
8806
8807				if (varCorrelation > *indexCorrelation)
8808					*indexCorrelation = varCorrelation;
8809
8810				free_attstatsslot(&sslot);
8811			}
8812		}
8813
8814		ReleaseVariableStats(vardata);
8815	}
8816
8817	qualSelectivity = clauselist_selectivity(root, indexQuals,
8818											 baserel->relid,
8819											 JOIN_INNER, NULL);
8820
8821	/*
8822	 * Now calculate the minimum possible ranges we could match with if all of
8823	 * the rows were in the perfect order in the table's heap.
8824	 */
8825	minimalRanges = ceil(indexRanges * qualSelectivity);
8826
8827	/*
8828	 * Now estimate the number of ranges that we'll touch by using the
8829	 * indexCorrelation from the stats. Careful not to divide by zero (note
8830	 * we're using the absolute value of the correlation).
8831	 */
8832	if (*indexCorrelation < 1.0e-10)
8833		estimatedRanges = indexRanges;
8834	else
8835		estimatedRanges = Min(minimalRanges / *indexCorrelation, indexRanges);
8836
8837	/* we expect to visit this portion of the table */
8838	selec = estimatedRanges / indexRanges;
8839
8840	CLAMP_PROBABILITY(selec);
8841
8842	*indexSelectivity = selec;
8843
8844	/*
8845	 * Compute the index qual costs, much as in genericcostestimate, to add to
8846	 * the index costs.  We can disregard indexorderbys, since BRIN doesn't
8847	 * support those.
8848	 */
8849	qual_arg_cost = index_other_operands_eval_cost(root, indexQuals);
8850
8851	/*
8852	 * Compute the startup cost as the cost to read the whole revmap
8853	 * sequentially, including the cost to execute the index quals.
8854	 */
8855	*indexStartupCost =
8856		spc_seq_page_cost * statsData.revmapNumPages * loop_count;
8857	*indexStartupCost += qual_arg_cost;
8858
8859	/*
8860	 * To read a BRIN index there might be a bit of back and forth over
8861	 * regular pages, as revmap might point to them out of sequential order;
8862	 * calculate the total cost as reading the whole index in random order.
8863	 */
8864	*indexTotalCost = *indexStartupCost +
8865		spc_random_page_cost * (numPages - statsData.revmapNumPages) * loop_count;
8866
8867	/*
8868	 * Charge a small amount per range tuple which we expect to match to. This
8869	 * is meant to reflect the costs of manipulating the bitmap. The BRIN scan
8870	 * will set a bit for each page in the range when we find a matching
8871	 * range, so we must multiply the charge by the number of pages in the
8872	 * range.
8873	 */
8874	*indexTotalCost += 0.1 * cpu_operator_cost * estimatedRanges *
8875		statsData.pagesPerRange;
8876
8877	*indexPages = index->pages;
8878}
